channel.title: Zeteo
channel.description: Mehdi Hasan's Zeteo — First Draft and news analysis

item.title: 7 Ways the World Cup Is Already a Disaster
item.description: Under the authoritarian rule of Donald Trump and amid the war in Iran, World Cup teams and fans were already expecting a subpar tournament in U.S. host cities this year. More than a quarter of the countries participating in the 2026 FIFA World Cup face U.S. travel bans, restrictions, or high visa…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Under the authoritarian rule of Donald Trump and amid the war in Iran, World Cup teams and fans were already expecting a subpar tournament in U.S. host cities this year. More than a quarter of the countries participating in the 2026 FIFA World Cup face U.S. travel bans, restrictions, or high visa rejection rates for fans. Human rights groups have been warning for months about the promised presence of ICE in U.S. host cities. And tickets were out of reach for many fans of the world’s most popular sport due to FIFA’s exorbitant prices this year.

But as teams, fans, and journalists arrive in the U.S. ahead of this week’s opening matches, it’s clear the 2026 World Cup is turning out to be even more of a shitshow than expected.

From teams being treated like “criminals” at U.S. airports, to American immigration officials handing out a red card to a Somali referee, to FIFA allegedly scamming those looking to attend the games, here are seven of the biggest ways the 2026 FIFA World Cup is already a disaster:

The U.S. is facing accusations of treating the national teams of Senegal and Uzbekistan like “criminals” at airports over what appeared to be intense security checks.

Videos and photos showed Senegal’s national team standing on the tarmac in San Antonio upon arrival for inspections by airport officials.

Uzbekistan’s national team also faced similarly intense screenings in New York, which reportedly included drug-sniffing dogs.

In the shadow of the illegal U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, Iranian officials have called on FIFA to address the “deliberate and discriminatory treatment against Iran’s national football team” by the U.S.

The Islamic Republic’s national team lost its ticket allocations for its matches just days before the World Cup is set to begin. Plus, the country’s team must reportedly enter and exit the U.S. on the same day of its games, and several key staffers were denied entry visas into the U.S.

Somalia’s Omar Artan, the 2025 Confederation of African Football (Caf) men’s referee of the year, was barred from entering the U.S. at Miami International Airport after an 11-hour immigration interview.

“I had the right papers and everything. I had the right visa,” said Artan.

It should be noted that Somalis have been frequent targets of Trump’s racist outbursts since he retook power last year.

Teams arriving at U.S. airports are experiencing the full brunt of Trump’s harsh immigration policies. Iraq’s striker Aymen Hussein was questioned for nearly seven hours at Chicago’s O’Hare and had his phone inspected before entering the country. The team’s official photographer was denied entry after also being questioned for hours.

International journalists are being denied the necessary visas to cover the tournament, which is being hosted by the U.S., Mexico, and Canada. “Countless” African and Iranian journalists can’t follow their teams across the host countries because they were given “single-entry” visas to the U.S., according to the International Sports Press Association.

There were 132 tickets still available for the U.S. opener against Paraguay as of Tuesday afternoon.

Plus, FIFA faced criticism over accusations that it was dumping unsold tickets for several matches on third-party resale sites for a fraction of the original cost. Critics accused FIFA of attempting to avoid chargeback and refund requests for the original tickets sold at exorbitant prices on the official FIFA website.

Hospitality and food and beverage workers across U.S. host cities are threatening to strike and walk off the job unless their demands for wage increases and protections from Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) are met.

***

The FIFA World Cup unites people across the world every four years in support of the beautiful game, but in 2026, it will likely unite them in their disdain for Trump’s weaponization of the sport, rather than the sport itself.

Zeteo’s Mohammed Abdelbary contributed to this piece.

Check out more of Zeteo’s recent coverage of the World Cup, including the first episode of our new 3-part series: ‘ The Dark Side of the World Cup’:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: 💥 BREAKING NEWS: We’re Launching Zeteo UK!
item.description: Dear Zeteo Subscribers, I’m here to share some big news from Zeteo – and I could not be prouder… or more excited. Zeteo is going global. Today, we launched Zeteo UK in London. That’s right. Since we launched in the United States in 2024, Zeteo has become an international brand of independent…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Dear Zeteo Subscribers,

I’m here to share some big news from Zeteo – and I could not be prouder… or more excited.

Zeteo is going global. Today, we launched Zeteo UK in London.

That’s right. Since we launched in the United States in 2024, Zeteo has become an international brand of independent journalism synonymous with bold, unfiltered, and accountability-driven coverage in this insane global political climate. And thanks to your support for the last two years, we’ve been able to build and grow to the point where we’re now ready to expand into the UK market and disrupt that country’s tired and discredited media, too.

Why now? Britain is currently experiencing a moment of political craziness unprecedented in modern history, as compelling as it is consequential. Yet, much of the UK’s mainstream media isn’t up to the task of covering it. Newspapers and TV channels are plagued by political bias, corporate interests, and institutional caution. That’s why Zeteo is stepping in. We believe this is a moment to build a new media outlet in the UK that is truly independent, unfiltered, and unafraid, with reporting geared towards informing people on the ground instead of protecting those in power.

Our all-star cast of contributors and journalists in the UK includes broadcasters Shehab Khan and Sangita Myska, and columnists Owen Jones, Peter Oborne, Grace Blakeley, and Afua Hirsch.

If you’re already a paid subscriber to Zeteo, thank you! Immensely. Look at what you’ve helped build. A truly global news organization!

If you’re interested in getting full, premium access to Zeteo UK, check out our bundle offer:

For just $11 a month on the annual plan, you can now get access to both Zeteo and Zeteo UK – that’s less than $5 more a month than just Zeteo alone ($7) and a savings of 20% compared to getting both subscriptions separately.

GET THE ZETEO + ZETEO UK BUNDLE NOW!

If you’re still a free subscriber to Zeteo itself, consider upgrading to a paid subscription today (with or without Zeteo UK bundled in). Our independent media movement is growing, globally… but we will not survive without financial support from independent people like you.

HELP SUPPORT OUR WORK

Stay tuned, as we have so much more exciting news to share in the coming weeks and months.

Let’s continue to change our broken media together.

Sincerely,

Mehdi

item.title: 🏀 🇮🇷 😒 ‘Louder Than Any Boos I’ve Heard’: Trump’s 48 Hours of Humiliation
item.description: 🚨 BREAKING NEWS: We’re Launching Happy Tuesday; it’s Swin 🙂 ☕. You may remember that in February, we reported that as President Donald Trump weighed whether to attend the Super Bowl, several close advisers privately assessed that he shouldn’t because the chances of him getting lustily booed on live…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
🚨 BREAKING NEWS: We’re Launching

Happy Tuesday; it’s Swin 🙂 ☕. You may remember that in February, we reported that as President Donald Trump weighed whether to attend the Super Bowl, several close advisers privately assessed that he shouldn’t because the chances of him getting lustily booed on live TV were – to put it mildly – high. The president ultimately didn’t attend, and told an obvious lie about why. But this week, Trump opted to attend a different high-profile sporting event – and it went about as well as you might’ve expected. And it wasn’t even the most embarrassing thing to happen to the twice-impeached, repeatedly indicted U.S. president within the past 48 hours.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we dive right into Trump’s recent, rapid-fire spree of dick-stepping. Relatedly, did his administration roll out the red carpet for flesh-eating screwworms? Plus, Trump wants Todd Blanche to stay on as his attorney general, and Bernie Sanders thinks he might be too old to run for president again.

Last night’s Game 3 between the New York Knicks and San Antonio Spurs took place in Manhattan. As I write this, I’m sitting here, on a Monday night, in Cincinnati. At one point, it was as if I could hear the booing all the way from over here in Ohio.

We can thank Donald J. Trump for that – even if it came at the cost of New Yorkers’ convenience and also the outdoor watch party meant for the people who couldn’t afford to get in.

Days before the president traveled back to his home city to attend the NBA Finals game – as a guest of Knicks owner and Trump donor James Dolan – administration officials knew what was coming.

“We are headed into hostile territory,” one White House official told me in the run-up to the game at Madison Square Garden. Other Trump administration officials I spoke to took it for granted that Trump would get loudly booed, and that the inevitable video clips would generate fodder for the president’s enemies. And boy, did he.

“Louder than the boos for the Spurs, louder than any boos I’ve heard in this building in a while,” tweeted Fox Sports analyst Rachel Nichols.

You don’t need to take her word for it. Listen and watch below:

As if to memorialize it in presidential history: According to the White House pool report, President Trump was “ thunderously booed” during the U.S. national anthem at Monday night’s game. And as our

It appears New York City came prepared for the moment. And Trump’s team was right to think he should’ve stayed the hell away from this year’s Super Bowl.

Still, Trump knew how big a deal Monday’s basketball game was, and his Blob-like ego couldn’t help but try to bask in the glow. (The Knicks lost, by the way; and while it may be unfair to blame Trump, Knicks fans are not not blaming him.) Never mind that seemingly no one in the crowd wanted him there, and that people in attendance and outside the arena apparently agreed with the solid majority of Americans that his administration resplendently sucks.

This may seem, on a cosmic level, like small potatoes: A woefully unpopular, scandal–plagued Republican president and wannabe dictator was vigorously booed when he showed his face at a major sporting event in one of the most notably liberal cities in the United States. It would have been bizarre if that hadn’t happened last night. However, Trump – for all of his supposedly tough talk, for all of his personality cultism, and for all of the violence and crackdowns he gleefully unleashes on those he deems easy targets – is objectively speaking one of the most thin-skinned crybabies to ever grace the column inches of Page Six. When the masses boo, it affects him. Don’t let anyone tell you otherwise.

“Getting booed in public really gets to him,” one former senior Trump administration official tells me. “When I worked with him, he would sometimes call me to complain about it, sometimes when I was trying to go to bed.”

And yet, Trump’s omni-shambolic Madison Square Garden cameo somehow wasn’t the most humiliating thing to happen to this president within the past two days. Though Trump enjoyed a minor, temporary reprieve by Monday evening, the Israeli and Iranian governments spent much of the last couple of days firing at one another, even as Trump practically begged the Israelis to knock it off.

It was merely the latest in a long string of highly public instances of a limp, feckless President Trump flailing in an illegal war of aggression that he started in February – and that is cratering his dismal poll numbers even farther down.

At its core, the farcically authoritarian cult of Trump is powered by two lies: that he can quickly solve problems that other leaders can’t, because other nations fear and respect him, and that he is a resoundingly popular political figure who the American people worship as their Definitely Very Healthy demigod.

Both things were – once again – conclusively proven to be false within the past 48 hours.

  • Strikes pause:Trump asked Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu to stop the strikes on Iran and said a breakthrough over Iran’s nuclear ambitions was near, theNew York Timesreported. AxiossaidTrump warned that Israel could lose U.S. support. The PM said Israel would “respond with force” if attacked again.
  • Hot air:After Trumpclaimedthat “final negotiations on ‘Peace’ are proceeding, subject to ignorance or stupidity getting in its way,” Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, the Iranian speaker,saidTehran wanted a deal but did not trust “the other side.”
  • Nuclear ask:The head of the International Atomic Energy Agencycalledfor Iran to “re-engage.” The U.S., UK, France, and Germany called for “precise information on nuclear material accountancy and safeguarded nuclear facilities.”
  • Helicopter goes down:Two American crew members were rescued after a U.S. Army Apache helicopter went down near the Strait of Hormuz on Monday, theNew York Timesreported. It wasn’t immediately clear why the helicopter went down.
  • Lebanon latest:Lebanese President Joseph Aoun said he would not meet Netanyahu until the war ended. The Israeli military issued forced displacement orders for most of the southern city of Tyre and its surroundings, where strikescontinued. Israeli attacksreportedlykilledat least eight people in the city on Tuesday. More than 3,635 people have beenkilledby Israel in Lebanon since the start of the latest escalation in March.

Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins led Trump aides in blaming Joe Biden for the re-emergence of flesh-eating screwworm in the U.S., a looming disaster for a cattle industry battered by Donald Trump’s tariffs and cost-of-living crisis.

Speaking to CNBC, Rollins, whose department has been heavily hit by Trump staff cuts, said: “Under the last administration, with the massive movement under the open borders policy, the cartels, etc, border security, that’s when it began to make its way back up toward America, hitting Mexico in early 2023 moving its way up through Mexico in 2024.”

On Fox, Senator Roger Marshall (R-Kan.), of the agriculture committee, said that thanks to Biden, screwworm could have entered on pets and people. Under Trump, it’s on an American pet, a New Mexico dog found on Monday, as Texas cattle cases climbed to four, with a goat infected too.

Others showed what the Trumpers left out.

Jeremy Konyndyk, a former senior official at the U.S. Agency for International Development, wrote: “The closure of USAID by 20-something DOGE bros (who probably thought ‘screwworm’ was a hilarious joke) will haunt us for years to come.”

Konyndyk linked to a Forbes report which said: “The [Elon] Musk-led Department of Government Efficiency… cut funding for a project monitoring and containing New World screwworm in Central America. The funding was axed days before the U.S. ended a temporary suspension of cattle imports from Mexico, meaning livestock was allowed to cross the border without any monitoring previously funded by USAID.”

Cattle industry leaders “pleaded with the government to step in as they monitored screwworm infections moving north through Mexico – but were ignored.”

Elsewhere on Monday, asked about preparations for screwworm in humans, Health Secretary Robert Kennedy stayed silent. To be charitable, recent reporting suggests he might not have had a clue what the reporter was talking about.

  • Todd nod:Donald TrumpnominatedTodd Blanche, the acting attorney general, for the full role. An architect of unprecedented corruption, whosaid“it isn’t a crime to party with Mr. Epstein,” Blanche is not set to enjoy an easy confirmation.
  • What’s too old?Bernie Sanders, 84, doesnot soundlike he is running for president in 2028. “I know I look like I’m 30; I am not. And that’s that,” he said.
  • Immigration news:The administrationannouncedan expansion of its push to strip naturalized Americans convicted of crimes of their citizenship. A federal judgestruck downa policy to impose $100,000 fees on visas for skilled foreign workers.
  • Platner backed:Minnesota Senator Tina SmithsaidGraham Platner, under fire but set to be Democrats’ Senate pick in Maine, “connected with Mainers on what they really care about: How this country can work for them, not just the wealthy.”
  • Take California:Trump baselesslyclaimedRepublican Spencer Pratt lost out in the primary for LA mayor because Democrats cheated, and claimed California GOP gubernatorial candidate Steve Hilton would lose the same way.
  • Endorse this:A lawyer who defended Ken Paxton on criminal charges and in his impeachment trialbackedthe GOP Texas AG’s Democratic opponent, James Talarico, for U.S. Senate.
  • SBF BFF:Imprisoned crypto fraudster Sam Bankman-Friedaskedfor a pardon. Accordingly, the man formerly known as SBF has beenheapingpraise on Trump.

Who, in April 2002, was the first non-human to testify before Congress?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:The Israeli militarykilledsix people, including a child, and expanded its occupied zone. Israel has killed nearly 980 people since a supposed “ceasefire” last October and now controls more than 60% of Gaza.
  • 🇬🇧 Khan suspended:The chief prosecutor of the International Criminal Court, Karim Khan, whoissuedarrest warrants for Benjamin Netanyahu and former Israeli defense minister Yoav Gallant, wassuspendedafter being referred for disciplinary action over sexual misconduct allegations that the British lawyer denies.

  • 🇱🇻 🇺🇦 🇷🇺

    Drone alarm:NATO jets shot down a drone the Latvian militarysaidentered its airspace “as a result of Russian electronic warfare,” suggesting the craft originated in Ukraine but was affected by electronic jamming.

  • 🇦🇱

    Drastic resort:Albania Prime Minister Edi Ramasaida luxury development planned by Donald Trump’s son-in-law, Jared Kushner, would go ahead despite persistent protests over its environmental impact on a remote stretch of Balkan coastline.
  • 🇵🇭 Philippines quake:An offshore earthquakemeasuring7.8 on the Richter scale killedat least 37 people,injured more than 200 others, and sent a tsunami to hit coastal villages.


Speaker Mike Johnson has thoughts about supposed election fraud in California – but not based on anything so passé as evidence. Johnson’s words are more in the vein of what the merciless satirist Chris Morris once coaxed out of a popular DJ: “Now that is scientific fact. There’s no real evidence for it, but it’s scientific fact.”

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Netanyahu Isn’t 'Defying' Trump Any More Than He Ever 'Defied' Biden
item.description: “I am going to call Bibi right now and tell him not to retaliate,” Donald Trump declared last night, just hours before Benjamin “Bibi” Netanyahu did in fact retaliate, striking Iran. Trump claims he called back, threatening, “Bibi, you better be careful, or you will be on your own very soon.” If we…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
“I am going to call Bibi right now and tell him not to retaliate,” Donald Trump declared last night, just hours before Benjamin “Bibi” Netanyahu did in fact retaliate, striking Iran. Trump claims he called back, threatening, “Bibi, you better be careful, or you will be on your own very soon.”

If we are to believe the multiple interviews the president made time for during a 24-hour geopolitical crisis, Trump put an end to the latest ceasefire-threatening cycle of escalation, which began yesterday when the Israeli prime minister bombed Beirut, prompting Tehran to attack Israel for the first time since the start of the April ceasefire, leading Netanyahu to cut off humanitarian aid to Gaza and, despite Trump proclaiming “I call all the shots. He doesn’t call the shots,” bombing Iran anyway. After a plaintive Trump Truth Social post calling for peace, and Yemen’s Houthis firing at Israel and threatening to close the Red Sea to Israeli shipping, Tehran and Tel Aviv stopped shooting at each other, at least for now.

Trump’s critics have called Netanyahu’s “defiance” of Trump a “humiliation“ for the president. That’s giving Trump a little too much credit.

Accusing Netanyahu of defying Trump means taking Trump at his word that he actually wanted to restrain Netanyahu last night but failed to do so. Believing him would not only require overlooking reports that Israeli officials coordinated the strikes with Secretary of State Marco Rubio, but also the post-October 7 presidential tradition of griping about Netanyahu to the press while giving him anything he wants, as well as the undeniable material realities of the U.S.-Israel relationship.

First, a reminder of where exactly Israel’s military power comes from: The Israeli Air Force jets that bombed Iran this morning were U.S.-built, likely carrying U.S.-made bombs, and couldn’t get off the tarmac without U.S.-produced repair parts, maintenance from U.S. defense contractors, and, critically, U.S.-supplied jet fuel. They were able to land safely because their bases are protected by Israeli air defense systems using U.S.-made interceptors, augmented by U.S. air defense units deployed to Israeli soil and around the region, including some that tried to shoot down the most recent Iranian missile volley. Trump could effectively ground the Israeli air force any time he wanted, if he wanted. If Trump is in the passenger’s seat for this war, he’s there by choice.

But if Trump didn’t really try to stop Netanyahu from bombing Iran, why set himself up for supposed “humiliation” by claiming he was going to? The president has adopted the same nuanced political comms strategy honed to an art by his predecessor: pretending to be mad at Netanyahu. In hopes of distracting Democratic voters from the unprecedented quantities of weapons they were shipping to sustain the genocide in Gaza, the Biden administration planted endless stories about how the then-president was, in fact, privately fuming and had even called Netanyahu an “asshole.” Flash forward to 2026, and we have Trump, not to be outdone, calling the Israeli prime minister “fucking crazy” in a series of articles nearly indistinguishable from the Biden pieces – many even written by the same journalist. In Trump’s mind, as in Biden’s, he’s not being embarrassed by Netanyahu; he’s deftly placating his base without having to actually cut down on the bombing.

Trump does fear a return to war enough that he has successfully applied some pressure, forcing Netanyahu to make concessions even if they were insufficient to secure a peace or protect civilians from the Israeli military. Trump’s April Iran ceasefire and even his largely ignored Lebanon ceasefire attempts were politically costly for the Israeli prime minister; ironically, Netanyahu’s critics say he was the one humiliated by Trump. It could be true that Trump’s threats got Netanyahu to stop bombing Iran today, but the problem is that Netanyahu knew there’d be no consequences for his initial round of airstrikes – other than a cascade of press “exclusives” about how the president got mad or averted disaster with masterful diplomacy.

Why hasn’t Trump actually carried out his threat to make Bibi fight on his own? It’s unlikely that any of Trump’s in-house or on-TV advisers have even suggested he try to cut military support for Israel; the Israel skeptics of Trumpworld have all been marginalized and reformed like J.D. Vance or expelled like Tulsi Gabbard and Tucker Carlson. While Trump is no self-proclaimed Zionist like Biden, he’s made no secret of his healthy respect for the power of the U.S. Israel lobby and probably prefers to avoid stirring that hornet’s nest if he doesn’t have to. And, as I wrote Saturday, Trump doesn’t feel like he has to, because he erroneously believes the current indefinitely-extended temporary ceasefire has more or less ended the war, and that he can take his time looking for his dream peace deal. Unfortunately for Trump, both Tel Aviv and Tehran beg to differ.

That difference of opinion was on full display this weekend. Netanyahu is quixotically committed to “finishing the job” and attacked Beirut to provoke Iran’s leaders into escalating and pulling Trump back into the war. Tehran wants closure through a permanent deal offering betrayal-proof gains like releasing frozen Iranian funds, but believes a return to war may be the only way to achieve that, given Trump’s foot-dragging since the April ceasefire. Iran’s leaders violated the ceasefire by striking Israel not only to come to the aid of Hezbollah, but to show Trump that if he can’t meaningfully restrain Netanyahu, he’s going to get war. It’s a message Trump should heed. Until he imposes meaningful consequences, the Israeli government will continue to find opportunities to escalate, and the Iranian government will respond in kind, eventually resuming attacks against Gulf states and U.S. forces in the region.

Trump has to choose: Go back to war or make some concessions to get to peace. Given his aversion to making tough decisions on this conflict, he’ll probably end up having to do both.

Harrison Mann is a former US Army major and executive officer of the Defense Intelligence Agency’s Middle East/Africa Regional Center who resigned in protest of his office’s support for Israel’s war in Gaza under the Biden administration. He is currently with the group Win Without War and recently became a Zeteo contributor.

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Graham Platner vs. the Machine
item.description: PORTLAND, Maine – Tuesday is primary day for Graham Platner, the populist Marine combat veteran and oysterman, whom Maine is expected to nominate for Senate over the objections of the U.S. political establishment, the far-right, and the corporate media class. If he wins, he will face five-term…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
PORTLAND, Maine – Tuesday is primary day for Graham Platner, the populist Marine combat veteran and oysterman, whom Maine is expected to nominate for Senate over the objections of the U.S. political establishment, the far-right, and the corporate media class. If he wins, he will face five-term Republican Senator Susan Collins in the fall.

More than any other election in the U.S. this year, the Maine Senate race has become a test of what people think should matter: policy goals (in Platner’s case, an expressed desire to fight corporate power, end wars, and stop Donald Trump’s authoritarian blitz), or the narratives that political operatives and journalists concoct together about politics and discuss in place of any of the many problems people experience as a result of policy decisions made in Washington, DC.

The outside observers keyed in on Platner would have voters believe the race is about morality – as if there is any to be found in the candidate’s most prominent critics, some of whom are committed genocide deniers and unrepentant war propagandists, or in a political consensus that distributes death and despair outward, and ever-more wealth upward.

The question that’s actually on the ballot in Maine is more fundamental. It is a question that politicos and journalists avoid engaging with in a serious way: “Which side are you on?”

“We are talking about taking back the politics of this country from the moneyed class, from the oligarchs, from the corporate power that for decades now has owned almost every section of our political system,” Platner said Sunday evening, speaking to an overflow crowd outside his town hall in Portland, before going in and speaking for over an hour with voters about what he would do as a senator.

“What everybody fails to understand in the rest of this country is they think this is a race that looks like all the other ones,” he continued. “They think this is a race about me. They think this is a race about the performative politics that everyone’s used to. What they don’t understand is this is a race about us. It’s about the people of Maine.”

The media and political establishment outside Maine would have voters see the Senate race differently.

Journalists have spent news cycle after news cycle applying a rare level of scrutiny to Platner and his personal history: his decade-old misogynist Reddit posts; the Nazi-style skull-and-crossbones tattoo he got covered up last fall; and even whether he should really be described as working-class. Recently came revelations that Platner had sexted other women early in his marriage.

Last week, an ex-girlfriend, Lyndsey Fifield, alleged in the New York Times that Platner had been toxic and crass when they dated off and on between 2013 and 2015. She said he had been physically intimidating with her at times, in the wake of his combat deployments overseas.

In a bit of heavy-handed irony, Fifield is a right-wing operative who once led an effort called “Ladies for Kavanaugh” to defend then-Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh from claims of sexual assault dating back to when he was a teenager. (Kavanaugh denied the accusations.) Fifield has also worked for the Independent Women’s Forum, a conservative group that famously wrote the talking points that Susan Collins used in her speech to set aside the allegations against Kavanaugh and justify voting for him.

It has since come out that Fifield was the source who anonymously told news outlets that Platner had described his skull-and-crossbones tattoo as “my Totenkopf.” Platner has said he and two fellow Marines chose the symbol accidentally on a drunken night in Croatia without knowing its meaning, and that he only learned about the resemblance in the fall. Fifield told the Times that Platner and his fellow Marines selected the tattoo “deliberately,” a notion one of the other Marines dismissed in an interview with Zeteo last week.

Platner has denied Fifield’s claims, and the Times wrote that it could not corroborate her accusations, nor could her friends.

Any candidate for political office, no matter who, merits scrutiny. But lost in the wall-to-wall coverage of Platner’s scandals is the context in which they’re unfolding.

If you watched and read the media coverage and tweets about Platner and Maine’s Senate race, you might for a moment forget that Donald Trump, the current Republican president, has been found liable for sexual abuse by a jury, was close friends with infamous pedophile Jeffrey Epstein, and was caught on video bragging about how he could sexually assault women any time he wanted. The conservatives working to defeat Platner dutifully support and revere this man.

You might even fail to consider that Trump, with Collins’s help, pushed Kavanaugh through onto the Supreme Court, despite the sexual assault allegations, as part of the successful, long-term project to pack the judiciary with right-wing zealots who would vote to restrict abortion rights and destroy Black political representation. Fifield, Platner’s ex, played her own role in this effort, calling the allegations against Kavanaugh “baseless” and maintaining his accuser was a liar.

You might for a moment be tempted to put aside the pivotal role that incumbent Republican Senator Susan Collins has played in enabling Trump’s extreme agenda and the broader right-wing assault on Americans’ rights – or the role she’ll play in ensuring both remain successful going forward if she wins again in November.

But should you? Collins voted for four of the six conservatives on the Supreme Court who gutted federal protections for abortion rights. Abortion is now illegal in half the country, and in many red states, doctors cannot help women terminate complicated or dangerous pregnancies or administer appropriate miscarriage care until they are bleeding to death. Women die. This is violence on a mass scale.

The U.S. political and media class invariably tolerates, accepts, and approves of violence when it’s committed this way against people it doesn’t think much about: when Trump deploys masked ICE agents to kidnap and terrorize immigrants and maim or kill protesters; when the U.S. routinely bombs civilians overseas; and as Israel has committed genocide against Palestinians, livestreamed on social media day in and day out.

Collins has supported and worked to fund this staggering, senseless violence. You won’t find any of it mentioned in the New York Times article celebrating the senator’s historic 10,000th consecutive vote.

As Platner put it Sunday during his town hall, “I fought in Iraq and Afghanistan wars that I am fully convinced did absolutely nothing to make the state of Maine any better off – wars that were supported by people like Susan Collins over and over and over again, because it benefited their donors, it benefited their own personal political power, it benefited a system that they were tied to, and they did not care about the human cost. But war is not about money. War is not about political power. War is about suffering, it is about pain, it is about violence, it is about death. It is awful. One of the reasons I want to go to the United States Senate is I want to be a strong voice to stand up against war.”

Zeteo was the first news outlet to report on Platner’s launch, and we have actively followed his insurgent campaign on the ground.

It’s been clear for many months, even as the negative news stories mounted, that this was Platner’s primary to lose – well before the party’s preferred candidate, incumbent Governor Janet Mills, suspended her campaign. As the media scrutiny has reached fever pitch in recent weeks, there has been a greater sense of unease around Platner’s candidacy.

Over the weekend, Platner held a large rally with Democratic Rep. Ro Khanna in Bar Harbor and the final town hall of his primary, in Portland. Many national reporters flew in to cover both events, to document what has become perhaps the biggest story in U.S. politics: Platner’s controversies.

Journalists were stunned to find Democratic voters standing behind him.

“Easy choice. I’m not looking to be his friend,” one man told ABC News. “I’m looking for someone who will represent my interests.”

In Bar Harbor, one woman told Zeteo she and her family were still backing Platner because, she explained simply, “We’re not Trumpers.”

At the Portland event on Sunday, a middle-aged woman named Mary said she had been following the news coverage – and that it shouldn’t matter.

“I’ve been following everything, and no, I like the man’s politics,” she said. “I think that’s what we should be focusing on. I like what he has to say.”

Another woman told a TV reporter: “The money is going to do everything they can, and anything they can, to stop him. I disagree with that.”

Tim Sample, a well-known humorist from Maine who was a longtime CBS News ‘Sunday Morning’ contributor, told Zeteo on Sunday: “What people from away never seem to understand about Mainers … we have one thing in common that is kind of overarching. This is what Mainers care about: Do you have jumper cables, and will you stop? And that’s the kind of candidate Graham Platner is. He’s a jumper-cable candidate. It’s a jumper-cable moment.”

He continued: “We need help, and we need help from the bottom up, not the top down. We don’t need people coming and telling us this is how Mainers should think or feel.”

On Tuesday, this primary will finally be over – and the results will give a sense of the extent to which the media furor has affected Platner’s candidacy.

There will surely be more coverage of Platner’s scandals. The media will continue to demand that Democratic politicians across the country weigh in on Platner. At this point, it would be safe to expect more revelations about his past.

Lost in this discussion is the fact that politics is about policy, not perceptions determined by people with no stake or interest in outcomes on the ground.

Starting Tuesday, and over the next five months, the people of Maine will decide what matters to them – and which side they’re on.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.content:encoded:

Livestream tickets are available now for this special show where Mehdi will sit down with The Guardian’s Arwa Mahdawi, airing today at

They’ll discuss the crackdown on free speech, and the pro-Palestine movement; genocide in the Middle East; Trump, Starmer and Farage; and the awful state of our media and the assault on press freedoms.

Those who purchase livestream tickets will be able to ask questions of Mehdi and Arwavia via text from wherever in the world they are watching, and they will also have exclusive access to a video of the entire event for several weeks afterwards. Don’t miss a moment of this historic event!

item.title: Ranting, Screaming Trump Walks Out of NBC Interview
item.description: On this day in 1949, George Orwell’s dystopian novel ‘ Nineteen Eighty-Four’ was published. The British writer’s last book sold millions, changed the English language, and continues to be cited whenever authoritarian governments, including that of Donald Trump, seek to deny reality. Good morning……
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1949, George Orwell’s dystopian novel ‘ Nineteen Eighty-Four’ was published. The British writer’s last book sold millions, changed the English language, and continues to be cited whenever authoritarian governments, including that of Donald Trump, seek to deny reality.

Good morning… Martin here, delighted to be bringing you your ‘First Draft’ goodness for a Monday morning, which in DC dawns disgustingly hot and muggy, a sign of the utter delight awaiting those who, for some reason, live here, over the next three infernally sticky months. Hurrah for that, I say, attempting to type with sarcasm.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we watch Donald Trump’s ludicrous interview with NBC’s ‘Meet the Press,’ preposterous hissy fit and all, so you don’t have to, and ask a hallowed question: “What if Obama or Biden behaved like that?” NBC was onto a loser anyway, in that they taped Friday, and by Sunday, all hell had broken loose over Iran. But then, Trump’s sheer absurdity essentially speaks to any moment. Elsewhere, we consider the latest Bari Weiss noise at CBS, with fired anchor Scott Pelley alleging editorial interference and CBS not really denying it. Such, such are the joys. Read on…

Eleven years into the Trumpocene era, it’s basically a truism to ask, after watching the president’s latest outrageous demonstration of his sheer unfitness for office, “Imagine if Barack Obama did this? How would Republicans react? How would the media react?” Or if the footage shows Trump nodding off, “Imagine if ‘Sleepy Joe’ Biden did this?”

Trump did not fall asleep during the interview with NBC’s ‘Meet the Press’ he recorded in Chippewa Falls, Wisconsin, on Friday, for broadcast Sunday morning. But it was full of “Imagine if” moments that would surely have set the political world on fire if any other president even came close to matching them. There, Trump squatted, absurd orange makeup slapped on if rather haphazardly up by his ears, ski-jump combover wispily in some sort of place. And on he rambled, for 40 dreadful minutes, circular, fantastical, contradictory, vague, aggressive, outright rude, mostly about his war in Iran and its multiplying malign effects.

Trump repeatedly said a deal to end it was close. By the time the piece went out, on the 100th day of the war, Israel had hit Beirut despite Trump claiming to have a promise it would not, and Iran was about to strike back. So Trump went to Axios, as he always does, to dictate the line that he would tell Benjamin Netanyahu not to respond. Officials confirmed that he did. Guess what: Israel responded, hitting Iran overnight.

In Wisconsin for a “farmers’ roundtable,” taking time out for NBC, Trump made his usual complete lack of sense. The regime in Tehran has been “largely decapitated,” he said. You can be decapitated, or not. Ask Marie Antoinette. And then there were the classic rambles: “We’re going to either have a deal, a strong deal, a meaningful deal, not like the Obama thing, Obama was a path to a nuclear weapon. Mine is the exact opposite. Mine is a wall, it’s a wall, just like I built 1,000 miles of wall on our southern border, and by the way, I took the worst border in history, and I made it the best border in history in a matter of weeks, and…”

Kristin Welker interjected. Not to put Trump right about “1,000 miles of wall,” which he has not built, but to try to get some sort of sense about why he ripped up the deal Barack Obama negotiated, then didn’t try to negotiate another before turning to war.

To adapt the immortal words of John Cleese’s Reg, in ‘Monty Python’s Life of Brian,’ “What’s the point? What’s the point of trying to get sense out of Trump? Where’s the answer going to gestate? In a box?”

“Kristin, I know you,” Trump sneered. “You’re a big liberal, a big progressive.”

Welker simpered: “No, I’m just a journalist.”

It didn’t work. Trump plowed on, claiming to have “obliterated” the nuclear program he was now determined to stop; rambling weirdly about the “bravery” of the new Iranian leader, Mojtaba Khamenei; blathering about having “inherited a terrible military,” and having rebuilt “a tremendous military,” which he might as well use. And it was all one person’s fault. Raising his racist dog whistle, he rasped it out: “Barack Hussein Obama.”

The interview took place in a barn at Custer Farms. Forsaking the temptation to make a gag about last stands, it seemed somehow apt to invoke Hieronymus Bosch. There crouched Trump, lips pursed, shoulders hunched, burping out lies and invective like some infernal frog creature from a 15th-century apocalypse. The heavens played ball, sending down to Welker’s personal hell some very serious weather.

“Hear that sound, that sound of thunder, lightning?” Trump asked.

“Is that going to interrupt, guys?” Welker asked. “No. Okay.”

It did interrupt. Repeatedly. Set up an interview in a barn with a tin roof on a stormy summer’s day in the Midwest, and see what happens. This being Trump, the biggest thunderclap came at the end.

Welker asked about farmers, hurting under Trump. He claimed they “loved” him and “understand better than anybody” that though gas and fertilizer prices have skyrocketed, his war just had to be done. He said he wanted new Fed chair Kevin Warsh “to do whatever he wants,” then dictated that Warsh should not raise interest rates. And he ranted about his “Anti-Weaponization Fund,” meant to pay supporters who attacked Congress on Jan. 6, 2021, a ploy supposedly killed off by acting Attorney General Todd Blanche under pressure from pissed-off Republicans but in Trump’s mind evidently still a thing.

It was all so familiar. Trump claimed there was “tremendous evidence, nothing but evidence” that “dirty cops” like former FBI Director James Comey caused Jan. 6, then persecuted the poor innocents they lured into the Capitol, presumably to crush officers in doors and shit on the floors out of nothing but love for their country. Trump said the 2020 election was “rigged, it was a dirty election,” then claimed current California elections were “rigged” too, simply because it takes a long time to count.

Trump was angry now, really raging. He attacked Welker directly, snarling: “Your elections are crooked and you’re crooked, and ‘Meet the Press’ is crooked. And so is ABC and CBS and CNN.”

He might’ve boasted about remembering all those networks, like identifying a cat, a cow, and a wombat, or whatever, down at Walter Reed. Not this time. Trump was done.

“You’re a one-sided crooked network,” he said. “Sorry. Let’s call it quits because I’ve had enough. Thank you, darling. Have a good time.”

Welker tried pleading: “Mr. President, let’s – please, I traveled all the way to Wisconsin.” It didn’t work. Trump snapped off his mic, struggled to his feet, and shambled off, bent near-double, staring down the barrel of 80. NBC cut to Welker, back in her regular studio.

“I spoke with President Trump on Saturday,” she said, adopting her best “I’m a serious journalist” look to camera, Derek Zoolander’s Blue Steel with added self-importance. “We both acknowledged the complications during the interview posed by the rain. He agreed to sit down with me for another ‘Meet the Press’ interview.”

In all honesty, why bother? The immortal words of Reg, exasperated leader of the People’s Front of Judea, spring back to mind once again.

“What’s the point?”

  • Strike, counterstrike:On Saturday, Iranfiredmissiles and drones at Bahrain and Kuwait. Bahrain said the attacks were intercepted. Tehran said they were aimed at U.S. installations in response to strikes on Qeshm Island and near Sirk.
  • Beirut hit:On Sunday, Israelstruckthe southern suburbs of Beirut – days after Donald Trump said he rebuked Benjamin Netanyahu about such attacks, supposedly gaining agreement they would stop. U.S. officials did not say Israel asked for permission to strike. Lebanon’s Health Ministry said two people were killed and 20 were injured. Israel said the strike was in retaliation for a Hezbollah attack.
  • Iran responds:Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalifbafsaid: “Washington’s green light today to the Zionist regime, makes U.S. and Israeli bases and assets in the region legitimate targets.” Israel intercepted missiles fired from Iran andsaidit was approving strikes in return. Iran said further strikes would elicit a “crushing and comprehensive response.” Oil pricesrose.
  • ‘Had their fun’:TrumptoldAxios he was “going to call Bibi right now and tell him not to retaliate. Each of them had their fun … The Iranian strikes didn’t hurt anybody… if Bibi strikes them back, it’s just gonna keep going like the last 47 years – or the last 3,000 years.” Trump alsospoketo theFinancial Times, claiming, “I call all the shots. He [Netanyahu] doesn’t call the shots.”
  • Guess what:Israel ignored Trump andfired back. Iranian media said explosions were heard in Tehran, Tabriz, and Isfahan. Missile alerts sounded in Saudi Arabia.
  • Houthis issue Red Sea ban:As Iran and Israel continued to exchange fire on Monday, Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthi rebelsclaimedan attack on Israel and said any Israeli vessels in the Red Sea would be targeted.
  • OK, Trump:In his first public comments since Iran and Israel traded fire, Trumpsaidearly this morning on Truth Social: “Israel and Iran must immediately stop ‘shooting.’” About an hour later, Trumpsaid: “Both sides, Israel and Iran, are looking to do an immediate CEASEFIRE! Final negotiations on ‘Peace’ are proceeding, subject to ignorance or stupidity getting in its way. The Blockade will remain in place, and in full force and effect, until a ‘Final Deal’ is reached. Things should move quickly.”

Fired CBS ‘60 Minutes’ star Scott Pelley spoke to the New York Times. Discussing his firing, and his contention that CBS News editor-in-chief Bari Weiss is “murdering” the show at the behest of owner and Trump ally David Ellison, Pelley alleged direct editorial interference.

In February, Pelley said, he and his team were working on a story about anti-ICE protests in Minneapolis and the killings of protesters Renee Good and Alex Pretti. The team found footage of protesters being aggressive, Pelley said, including Pretti “kicking out a taillight on a police car.” The piece was finished.

But then, Pelley said, “Bari Weiss sends an email to my boss, Tanya Simon.”

Simon has also been fired.

Pelley said the email asked, “Can we make the protesters look more violent?” and said of Good, “You need to describe her as driving toward the officer.”

“This is not what you see on the video,” Pelley said, adding that though his team was sure “to show the protesters for the responsibility that they had… Somehow that wasn’t enough for Weiss. The video showed that the officer wasn’t standing in front of the car and [Good] wasn’t driving toward him, but that’s what [Donald Trump] said about that, and that’s the way [Weiss] wanted it described.”

Pelley said Weiss had “put a thumb on the scale for the president’s version of events that I felt was a level of political influence that I had never seen in 37 years at CBS News.”

A CBS News spokesperson said Weiss “made four points in the course of editorial back-and-forth” with “no political motivation,” proposing changes “solely to make the piece as strong, fair, and accurate as possible.”

Amid responses, Eric Schultz, a Barack Obama adviser, was succinct.

“Okay – then release the email!”

  • Un-FISA-ble:By naming housing official/MAGA warrior Bill Pulte acting director of national intelligence, Donald Trump “threw a live hand grenade” into talks to extend FISA Act surveillance authority past Friday, Senator Mark Warnersaid.
  • Taking a Liberty:DOJ lawyers defending Trump’s decision to tear down the East Wing of the White House to build a ballroomtoldfederal judges that courts would be powerless to stop the demolition of the Statue of Liberty, if Trump decided to do it.
  • Pete’s sake:Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth wasaccusedof “grotesque stupidity” after marking D-Day in northern France by claiming beaches where the Allies beat back the Nazis 82 years ago were now being “stormed” by immigrants.
  • Veep rebuke:British deputy PM David Lammysaidhe told JD Vance he was wrong about the murder of a white British teen by a Sikh man. The VP and MAGA figures, including Elon Musk, have used Henry Nowak’s death to incite unrest.
  • Slow progress:Vote-counting crawled on in California, with Democrat Xavier Becerrathroughto the gubernatorial election and Democrat Nithya Ramanedging outRepublican Spencer Pratt to face incumbent Karen Bass for LA mayor.
  • Pardon watch:Late Friday, TrumppardonedStephen Buyer, a former Indiana Republican congressman jailed in 2023, for insider trading.

Bob Packwood, a former GOP senator from Oregon, died Saturday aged 93. He quit Congress in 1995, on publication of a report detailing sexual misconduct. How many pages did the report have, and how much was it said to weigh?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israelkilledat least 13 Palestinians in Gaza, even as talks with Hamas opened in Egypt. At least 970 Palestinians in Gaza have been killed and more than 3,000 wounded since the so-called “ceasefire” last October.

  • 🇬🇧

    Labour Together:Drop Site Newspublishedan investigation into how pro-Israel money funded the group that purged left-wingers, powered Keir Starmer’s rise to 10 Downing Street, and last week helped bar Cenk Uygur andHasan Pikerfrom British soil.

  • 🇨🇺

    Cuba latest:Former Cuban President Raúl Castromadehis first public appearance since being indicted in the U.S. over a 1996 incident involving the downing of two planes flown by right-wing exiles. The appearance late Friday marked Castro’s 95th birthday.

  • 🇳🇬

    Hostages freed:The Nigerian armysaidit had freed 360 people abducted by Boko Haram militants in the country’s northeast.

In which acting, would-be permanent Attorney General Todd Blanche openly says he’s working to stop Democrats from prosecuting Donald Trump and associates if they take back power. And FBI Director Kash Patel looks worried.

  • Republican

    Rep. Brian FitzpatricktoldCNN’s ‘State of the Union’ it didn’t matter ifDonald Trumpcarried on musing about paying off supporters, including January 6 rioters, because “the votes are not there, and will not be there, to give a dime to” the so-called Anti-Weaponization Fund.Rep. Tom Suozzi, a New York Democrat, said Congress would block any attempts to find other ways to pay.

  • Asked about accusations of questionable behavior towards women by

    Graham Platner,Rep. Ro Khannabackedthe Democratic Maine Senate candidate, telling CBS’s ‘Face the Nation’ that Platner “took accountability” for his actions. Khanna added that voters in Maine “are willing to extend him grace and redemption, and their focus now is on what he’s running for.”

  • Former FBI Director

    James Comeyhad advice for acting Attorney GeneralTodd Blanche: “Bone up.” Speaking to NBC’s ‘Meet the Press’ about his ludicrous indictment for threatening the president via Instagram seashells, Comey added: “I don’t want to comment on it because then someone will say I’m commenting on the case. [Blanche] ought not to be talking about it, I can’t talk about it. That’s why we have a judicial process in a courtroom.”
  • Monday, June 8:The House returns, due to take up a $70 billion reconciliation bill to fund immigration enforcement, passed by the Senate. Donald Trump is expected to attend the NBA finals in New York, as the Knicks seek a first title since 1973.
  • Tuesday, June 9:Primary day in Maine sees Democrat Graham Platner, under fire over allegations about his past, still the clear favorite to advance and face GOP Senator Susan Collins. South Carolina, North Dakota, and Nevada also vote.
  • Friday, June 12:The deadline for renewal of Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act, which allows warrantless surveillance of overseas targets. Senate Democrats are against renewal over Trump's naming of Bill Pulte as acting director of national intelligence. Some Republicans want added protections for Americans.
  • Sunday, June 14:Trump turns 80, and celebrates by staging a slate of UFC fights on the South Lawn of the White House.

item.title: Ranting, Screaming Trump Walks Out of NBC Interview
item.description: On this day in 1949, George Orwell’s dystopian novel ‘ Nineteen Eighty-Four’ was published. The British writer’s last book sold millions, changed the English language, and continues to be cited whenever authoritarian governments, including that of Donald Trump, seek to deny reality. Good morning……
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1949, George Orwell’s dystopian novel ‘ Nineteen Eighty-Four’ was published. The British writer’s last book sold millions, changed the English language, and continues to be cited whenever authoritarian governments, including that of Donald Trump, seek to deny reality.

Good morning… Martin here, delighted to be bringing you your ‘First Draft’ goodness for a Monday morning, which in DC dawns disgustingly hot and muggy, a sign of the utter delight awaiting those who, for some reason, live here, over the next three infernally sticky months. Hurrah for that, I say, attempting to type with sarcasm.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we watch Donald Trump’s ludicrous interview with NBC’s ‘Meet the Press,’ preposterous hissy fit and all, so you don’t have to, and ask a hallowed question: “What if Obama or Biden behaved like that?” NBC was onto a loser anyway, in that they taped Friday, and by Sunday, all hell had broken loose over Iran. But then, Trump’s sheer absurdity essentially speaks to any moment. Elsewhere, we consider the latest Bari Weiss noise at CBS, with fired anchor Scott Pelley alleging editorial interference and CBS not really denying it. Such, such are the joys. Read on…

Eleven years into the Trumpocene era, it’s basically a truism to ask, after watching the president’s latest outrageous demonstration of his sheer unfitness for office, “Imagine if Barack Obama did this? How would Republicans react? How would the media react?” Or if the footage shows Trump nodding off, “Imagine if ‘Sleepy Joe’ Biden did this?”

Trump did not fall asleep during the interview with NBC’s ‘Meet the Press’ he recorded in Chippewa Falls, Wisconsin, on Friday, for broadcast Sunday morning. But it was full of “Imagine if” moments that would surely have set the political world on fire if any other president even came close to matching them. There, Trump squatted, absurd orange makeup slapped on if rather haphazardly up by his ears, ski-jump combover wispily in some sort of place. And on he rambled, for 40 dreadful minutes, circular, fantastical, contradictory, vague, aggressive, outright rude, mostly about his war in Iran and its multiplying malign effects.

Trump repeatedly said a deal to end it was close. By the time the piece went out, on the 100th day of the war, Israel had hit Beirut despite Trump claiming to have a promise it would not, and Iran was about to strike back. So Trump went to Axios, as he always does, to dictate the line that he would tell Benjamin Netanyahu not to respond. Officials confirmed that he did. Guess what: Israel responded, hitting Iran overnight.

In Wisconsin for a “farmers’ roundtable,” taking time out for NBC, Trump made his usual complete lack of sense. The regime in Tehran has been “largely decapitated,” he said. You can be decapitated, or not. Ask Marie Antoinette. And then there were the classic rambles: “We’re going to either have a deal, a strong deal, a meaningful deal, not like the Obama thing, Obama was a path to a nuclear weapon. Mine is the exact opposite. Mine is a wall, it’s a wall, just like I built 1,000 miles of wall on our southern border, and by the way, I took the worst border in history, and I made it the best border in history in a matter of weeks, and…”

Kristin Welker interjected. Not to put Trump right about “1,000 miles of wall,” which he has not built, but to try to get some sort of sense about why he ripped up the deal Barack Obama negotiated, then didn’t try to negotiate another before turning to war.

To adapt the immortal words of John Cleese’s Reg, in ‘Monty Python’s Life of Brian,’ “What’s the point? What’s the point of trying to get sense out of Trump? Where’s the answer going to gestate? In a box?”

“Kristin, I know you,” Trump sneered. “You’re a big liberal, a big progressive.”

Welker simpered: “No, I’m just a journalist.”

It didn’t work. Trump plowed on, claiming to have “obliterated” the nuclear program he was now determined to stop; rambling weirdly about the “bravery” of the new Iranian leader, Mojtaba Khamenei; blathering about having “inherited a terrible military,” and having rebuilt “a tremendous military,” which he might as well use. And it was all one person’s fault. Raising his racist dog whistle, he rasped it out: “Barack Hussein Obama.”

The interview took place in a barn at Custer Farms. Forsaking the temptation to make a gag about last stands, it seemed somehow apt to invoke Hieronymus Bosch. There crouched Trump, lips pursed, shoulders hunched, burping out lies and invective like some infernal frog creature from a 15th-century apocalypse. The heavens played ball, sending down to Welker’s personal hell some very serious weather.

“Hear that sound, that sound of thunder, lightning?” Trump asked.

“Is that going to interrupt, guys?” Welker asked. “No. Okay.”

It did interrupt. Repeatedly. Set up an interview in a barn with a tin roof on a stormy summer’s day in the Midwest, and see what happens. This being Trump, the biggest thunderclap came at the end.

Welker asked about farmers, hurting under Trump. He claimed they “loved” him and “understand better than anybody” that though gas and fertilizer prices have skyrocketed, his war just had to be done. He said he wanted new Fed chair Kevin Warsh “to do whatever he wants,” then dictated that Warsh should not raise interest rates. And he ranted about his “Anti-Weaponization Fund,” meant to pay supporters who attacked Congress on Jan. 6, 2021, a ploy supposedly killed off by acting Attorney General Todd Blanche under pressure from pissed-off Republicans but in Trump’s mind evidently still a thing.

It was all so familiar. Trump claimed there was “tremendous evidence, nothing but evidence” that “dirty cops” like former FBI Director James Comey caused Jan. 6, then persecuted the poor innocents they lured into the Capitol, presumably to crush officers in doors and shit on the floors out of nothing but love for their country. Trump said the 2020 election was “rigged, it was a dirty election,” then claimed current California elections were “rigged” too, simply because it takes a long time to count.

Trump was angry now, really raging. He attacked Welker directly, snarling: “Your elections are crooked and you’re crooked, and ‘Meet the Press’ is crooked. And so is ABC and CBS and CNN.”

He might’ve boasted about remembering all those networks, like identifying a cat, a cow, and a wombat, or whatever, down at Walter Reed. Not this time. Trump was done.

“You’re a one-sided crooked network,” he said. “Sorry. Let’s call it quits because I’ve had enough. Thank you, darling. Have a good time.”

Welker tried pleading: “Mr. President, let’s – please, I traveled all the way to Wisconsin.” It didn’t work. Trump snapped off his mic, struggled to his feet, and shambled off, bent near-double, staring down the barrel of 80. NBC cut to Welker, back in her regular studio.

“I spoke with President Trump on Saturday,” she said, adopting her best “I’m a serious journalist” look to camera, Derek Zoolander’s Blue Steel with added self-importance. “We both acknowledged the complications during the interview posed by the rain. He agreed to sit down with me for another ‘Meet the Press’ interview.”

In all honesty, why bother? The immortal words of Reg, exasperated leader of the People’s Front of Judea, spring back to mind once again.

“What’s the point?”

  • Strike, counterstrike:On Saturday, Iranfiredmissiles and drones at Bahrain and Kuwait. Bahrain said the attacks were intercepted. Tehran said they were aimed at U.S. installations in response to strikes on Qeshm Island and near Sirk.
  • Beirut hit:On Sunday, Israelstruckthe southern suburbs of Beirut – days after Donald Trump said he rebuked Benjamin Netanyahu about such attacks, supposedly gaining agreement they would stop. U.S. officials did not say Israel asked for permission to strike. Lebanon’s Health Ministry said two people were killed and 20 were injured. Israel said the strike was in retaliation for a Hezbollah attack.
  • Iran responds:Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalifbafsaid: “Washington’s green light today to the Zionist regime, makes U.S. and Israeli bases and assets in the region legitimate targets.” Israel intercepted missiles fired from Iran andsaidit was approving strikes in return. Iran said further strikes would elicit a “crushing and comprehensive response.” Oil pricesrose.
  • ‘Had their fun’:TrumptoldAxios he was “going to call Bibi right now and tell him not to retaliate. Each of them had their fun … The Iranian strikes didn’t hurt anybody… if Bibi strikes them back, it’s just gonna keep going like the last 47 years – or the last 3,000 years.” Trump alsospoketo theFinancial Times, claiming, “I call all the shots. He [Netanyahu] doesn’t call the shots.”
  • Guess what:Israel ignored Trump andfired back. Iranian media said explosions were heard in Tehran, Tabriz, and Isfahan. Missile alerts sounded in Saudi Arabia.
  • Houthis issue Red Sea ban:As Iran and Israel continued to exchange fire on Monday, Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthi rebelsclaimedan attack on Israel and said any Israeli vessels in the Red Sea would be targeted.
  • OK, Trump:In his first public comments since Iran and Israel traded fire, Trumpsaidearly this morning on Truth Social: “Israel and Iran must immediately stop ‘shooting.’” About an hour later, Trumpsaid: “Both sides, Israel and Iran, are looking to do an immediate CEASEFIRE! Final negotiations on ‘Peace’ are proceeding, subject to ignorance or stupidity getting in its way. The Blockade will remain in place, and in full force and effect, until a ‘Final Deal’ is reached. Things should move quickly.”

Fired CBS ‘60 Minutes’ star Scott Pelley spoke to the New York Times. Discussing his firing, and his contention that CBS News editor-in-chief Bari Weiss is “murdering” the show at the behest of owner and Trump ally David Ellison, Pelley alleged direct editorial interference.

In February, Pelley said, he and his team were working on a story about anti-ICE protests in Minneapolis and the killings of protesters Renee Good and Alex Pretti. The team found footage of protesters being aggressive, Pelley said, including Pretti “kicking out a taillight on a police car.” The piece was finished.

But then, Pelley said, “Bari Weiss sends an email to my boss, Tanya Simon.”

Simon has also been fired.

Pelley said the email asked, “Can we make the protesters look more violent?” and said of Good, “You need to describe her as driving toward the officer.”

“This is not what you see on the video,” Pelley said, adding that though his team was sure “to show the protesters for the responsibility that they had… Somehow that wasn’t enough for Weiss. The video showed that the officer wasn’t standing in front of the car and [Good] wasn’t driving toward him, but that’s what [Donald Trump] said about that, and that’s the way [Weiss] wanted it described.”

Pelley said Weiss had “put a thumb on the scale for the president’s version of events that I felt was a level of political influence that I had never seen in 37 years at CBS News.”

A CBS News spokesperson said Weiss “made four points in the course of editorial back-and-forth” with “no political motivation,” proposing changes “solely to make the piece as strong, fair, and accurate as possible.”

Amid responses, Eric Schultz, a Barack Obama adviser, was succinct.

“Okay – then release the email!”

  • Un-FISA-ble:By naming housing official/MAGA warrior Bill Pulte acting director of national intelligence, Donald Trump “threw a live hand grenade” into talks to extend FISA Act surveillance authority past Friday, Senator Mark Warnersaid.
  • Taking a Liberty:DOJ lawyers defending Trump’s decision to tear down the East Wing of the White House to build a ballroomtoldfederal judges that courts would be powerless to stop the demolition of the Statue of Liberty, if Trump decided to do it.
  • Pete’s sake:Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth wasaccusedof “grotesque stupidity” after marking D-Day in northern France by claiming beaches where the Allies beat back the Nazis 82 years ago were now being “stormed” by immigrants.
  • Veep rebuke:British deputy PM David Lammysaidhe told JD Vance he was wrong about the murder of a white British teen by a Sikh man. The VP and MAGA figures, including Elon Musk, have used Henry Nowak’s death to incite unrest.
  • Slow progress:Vote-counting crawled on in California, with Democrat Xavier Becerrathroughto the gubernatorial election and Democrat Nithya Ramanedging outRepublican Spencer Pratt to face incumbent Karen Bass for LA mayor.
  • Pardon watch:Late Friday, TrumppardonedStephen Buyer, a former Indiana Republican congressman jailed in 2023, for insider trading.

Bob Packwood, a former GOP senator from Oregon, died Saturday aged 93. He quit Congress in 1995, on publication of a report detailing sexual misconduct. How many pages did the report have, and how much was it said to weigh?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israelkilledat least 13 Palestinians in Gaza, even as talks with Hamas opened in Egypt. At least 970 Palestinians in Gaza have been killed and more than 3,000 wounded since the so-called “ceasefire” last October.

  • 🇬🇧

    Labour Together:Drop Site Newspublishedan investigation into how pro-Israel money funded the group that purged left-wingers, powered Keir Starmer’s rise to 10 Downing Street, and last week helped bar Cenk Uygur andHasan Pikerfrom British soil.

  • 🇨🇺

    Cuba latest:Former Cuban President Raúl Castromadehis first public appearance since being indicted in the U.S. over a 1996 incident involving the downing of two planes flown by right-wing exiles. The appearance late Friday marked Castro’s 95th birthday.

  • 🇳🇬

    Hostages freed:The Nigerian armysaidit had freed 360 people abducted by Boko Haram militants in the country’s northeast.

In which acting, would-be permanent Attorney General Todd Blanche openly says he’s working to stop Democrats from prosecuting Donald Trump and associates if they take back power. And FBI Director Kash Patel looks worried.

  • Republican

    Rep. Brian FitzpatricktoldCNN’s ‘State of the Union’ it didn’t matter ifDonald Trumpcarried on musing about paying off supporters, including January 6 rioters, because “the votes are not there, and will not be there, to give a dime to” the so-called Anti-Weaponization Fund.Rep. Tom Suozzi, a New York Democrat, said Congress would block any attempts to find other ways to pay.

  • Asked about accusations of questionable behavior towards women by

    Graham Platner,Rep. Ro Khannabackedthe Democratic Maine Senate candidate, telling CBS’s ‘Face the Nation’ that Platner “took accountability” for his actions. Khanna added that voters in Maine “are willing to extend him grace and redemption, and their focus now is on what he’s running for.”

  • Former FBI Director

    James Comeyhad advice for acting Attorney GeneralTodd Blanche: “Bone up.” Speaking to NBC’s ‘Meet the Press’ about his ludicrous indictment for threatening the president via Instagram seashells, Comey added: “I don’t want to comment on it because then someone will say I’m commenting on the case. [Blanche] ought not to be talking about it, I can’t talk about it. That’s why we have a judicial process in a courtroom.”
  • Monday, June 8:The House returns, due to take up a $70 billion reconciliation bill to fund immigration enforcement, passed by the Senate. Donald Trump is expected to attend the NBA finals in New York, as the Knicks seek a first title since 1973.
  • Tuesday, June 9:Primary day in Maine sees Democrat Graham Platner, under fire over allegations about his past, still the clear favorite to advance and face GOP Senator Susan Collins. South Carolina, North Dakota, and Nevada also vote.
  • Friday, June 12:The deadline for renewal of Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act, which allows warrantless surveillance of overseas targets. Senate Democrats are against renewal over Trump's naming of Bill Pulte as acting director of national intelligence. Some Republicans want added protections for Americans.
  • Sunday, June 14:Trump turns 80, and celebrates by staging a slate of UFC fights on the South Lawn of the White House.

item.title: 100 Days and Still No Answers for Over 100 Kids Killed at an Iranian School
item.description: It’s been 100 days since the U.S. launched its illegal war in Iran and bombed a girls school in Minab, killing more than 150 people, including more than 100 kids. Prem has been asking members of Congress about it ever since. Every single time, he has received shocking non-answers from Republicans…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
It’s been 100 days since the U.S. launched its illegal war in Iran and bombed a girls school in Minab, killing more than 150 people, including more than 100 kids.

Prem has been asking members of Congress about it ever since. Every single time, he has received shocking non-answers from Republicans (at best), and, from Democrats, frustration at Republican inaction.

In March, military investigators preliminarily determined that the U.S. was responsible for the strike. The same month, every single Senate Democrat except John Fetterman sent a letter to Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, demanding answers and accountability measures.

But Donald Trump’s administration has given no substantive answer on the matter. A spokesperson for the Defense Department told Zeteo “the incident is under investigation” and declined to comment.

The lack of answers about how this conflict may have started with a horrific war crime against children has not slowed this administration in continuing its onslaught.

Meanwhile, it’s been 860 days since Israeli forces in Gaza killed 5-year-old Hind Rajab. We repeatedly pressed U.S. officials on this killing – because if the U.S. could do nothing in response to the murder of a child whose name was heard around the world, what would it do about the other thousands of kids Israel has killed?

We never got answers. In one attack in Iran, the U.S. killed more than 100 kids just like Hind. And in Congress, particularly among Republicans, it’s just business as usual.

Watch the video above to see how members of Congress have answered questions about a massacre of children in which they may be complicit.

We are keeping this paywall–free. Please become a paid subscriber to Zeteo to help Prem keep pushing members of Congress in ways no one in the mainstream media will.

If you are already subscribed, please consider making a donation to help us keep building Zeteo into the type of media outlet you deserve.

Do you have any information or tips for Prem? You can text him on Signal at premthakker.35 your anonymity will be protected.

Zeteo’s Layla Nayfeh contributed to this video.
