item.title: This Week in Palestine: Gaza's Deadliest Month of 2026, and UN Experts Issue Stark Warning
item.description: May marked the deadliest month in Gaza this year. Of the 119 Palestinians killed, 30% were either children, women, or the elderly, according to Palestinian health officials . All during a supposed “ceasefire.” We, of course, know that ceasefires that Israel agrees to – whether in Gaza or Lebanon –…
item.author: This Week in Palestine | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
May marked the deadliest month in Gaza this year. Of the 119 Palestinians killed, 30% were either children, women, or the elderly, according to Palestinian health officials. All during a supposed “ceasefire.”

We, of course, know that ceasefires that Israel agrees to – whether in Gaza or Lebanon – are often in name only. The bombing, the shooting, the forced displacement continue.

In Gaza, Palestinians continue to have nowhere to run. Just this week, a family of five reportedly burned to death when their apartment building was targeted by Israeli forces. A doctor was killed not far from the hospital where he worked.

Meanwhile, a group of UN experts issued a fresh warning about Israeli settler brutality, and an Israel-based human rights group reported that a well-known Gaza doctor, abducted by Israel in late 2024, has been moved to solitary confinement.

That’s sadly not all. Here’s just a glimpse of another troubling week in Palestine:

In a telling report, three Israeli soldiers described to the Associated Press a sense of confusion and orders they were given to kill Palestinians during the so-called “ceasefire'“ in Gaza.

“It was a jungle,” one soldier in his 20s told AP. “After the ceasefire, the order was: If someone crosses the line, you shoot them,” he added, referring to the often unmarked “yellow line,” where Israeli forces withdrew as part of the ceasefire deal.

“To call it a ceasefire is a joke,” another soldier said.

Progressive streamer Hasan Piker and his uncle and Young Turks founder Cenk Uygur said the British government had revoked their authorizations to enter the country over their criticism of Israel.

Both had been scheduled to speak at events in the UK this week. The UK Home Office later said the pair’s travel authorizations were canceled after determining their presence in the country “may not be conducive to the public good.”

It’s a “sad state of affairs, where obviously the interests of Israel take the highest priority,” Piker said.

A group of UN experts, including Francesca Albanese, issued a stark warning about the unprecedented levels of Israeli “settler brutality” against Palestinians in the occupied West Bank this year.

“Relentless attacks by the settler-colonial movement, carried out with the support and acquiescence of the Israeli State, have become a daily terror in Palestinian lives,” the experts said. “The escalating violence, carried out with full impunity, serves as an instrument of coercion in the hands of the occupying power, facilitating ethnic cleansing.”

Israeli settlers have killed at least 13 Palestinians and injured hundreds since the start of the year.

Israel detained Mahmoud al-Najjar, a student from Gaza and the only survivor of the war in his immediate family, as he attempted to cross the Kerem Shalom border crossing after gaining permission from Israel to leave the Strip to study abroad in Rome, Drop Site reported, citing Palestinian journalist Muthanna al-Najjar.

As it often does, Israel later claimed, with no evidence, that al-Najjar was a Hamas operative.

There was no information about his whereabouts as of Tuesday.

Doctors Without Borders (MSF) reported that one year on, its teams in Gaza continue to treat scores of Palestinians injured by Israeli fire at aid sites run by the U.S.-Israel-backed Gaza Humanitarian Foundation.

MSF said its two facilities in Khan Younis had recorded at least 32 deaths and 1,885 injuries from patients who arrived from the sites from June to October 2025 (GHF began operating the sites in late May 2025).

“GHF was so humiliating; thousands of people would run towards it, then the IDF would shoot on us from fixed points.”
-Mustafa, a 17-year-old whose nephew was killed by a sniper, quoted by MSF

Israel pounded parts of Gaza, killing at least 11, including several children. An attack on a residential building killed at least nine people, Palestinian health officials said. A family of five was burned to death, Al Jazeera reported, citing the family’s neighbors.

Israel has killed at least 72,956 Palestinians since Oct. 7, including nearly 950 people who have been killed since the so-called “ceasefire” went into effect nearly eight months ago.

Dr. Hussam Abu Safiya, who was abducted from Gaza by Israel in December 2024, was transferred to solitary confinement at a maximum security prison in southern Israel, Physicians for Human Rights Israel (PHRI) said, per Haaretz. His lawyer was given no reason for the transfer.

Abu Safiya, who was the director of the Kamal Adwan Hospital, is one of 14 doctors abducted from Gaza since Oct. 7 who Israel continues to hold without charge. Abu Saifya’s lawyers and PHRI have previously said the doctor has faced torture and received inadequate medical treatment.

Abu Saifya was featured in the Zeteo film, ‘Gaza: Doctors Under Attack’:

Miss last week’s edition? Catch up here. Plus, check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: 5 Reasons Trump Still Hasn't Made a Deal to End His War in Iran
item.description: Since President Donald Trump joined Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in launching a surprise attack on Iran almost 100 days ago, Trump and his hapless underlings have proclaimed victory a dozen times , formally ended one named military operation and indefinitely “paused” another, and even…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Since President Donald Trump joined Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in launching a surprise attack on Iran almost 100 days ago, Trump and his hapless underlings have proclaimed victory a dozen times, formally ended one named military operation and indefinitely “paused” another, and even announced that the war was officially over, never mind that U.S. and Iranian forces have not stopped shooting.

News media – and financial markets – have breathlessly amplified weekly “leaks” about negotiations between Washington and Tehran, some more substantive than others, that nonetheless have produced no tangible progress since the two sides agreed to a “temporary” ceasefire two months ago.

So, why does a deal to end the war with Iran continue to elude the self-proclaimed master dealmaker? Here are five of the biggest reasons why:

I’m always reluctant to attribute crises to the personal shortcomings of Trump because doing so can obscure the structural, often bipartisan failures behind his policies, from ICE raids to the embrace of anti-democratic Big Tech to his illegal wars. But at this point, it’s hard to ignore the evidence that Trump’s lack of urgency to sign a deal with Tehran is in part because he’s been very slow to understand the actual situation on the ground. Trump said he launched his war hoping for an overnight regime swap à la Venezuela, and, in a sign the president had trouble letting go of that mental model, his administration was still trying to identify an Iranian Delcy Rodriguez weeks later. Trump’s trusted advisers – both in the Situation Room and on Fox News – rarely if ever deliver bad news about the war, whether out of ideological desire to see the president “finish the job,” or because they’re afraid he’ll shoot the messenger. Tucker Carlson’s accusation that Trump’s staff presents him with fake polling on the popularity of the war isn’t hard to believe, given reports that Trump’s Iran intel briefs don’t just minimize bad news; sometimes, they’re just videos of stuff blowing up.

We know that the Iranian government retains the drones, missiles, and underground bunkers it needs to keep fighting, in part because U.S. intelligence agencies keep leaking assessments to that effect, perhaps hoping they’ll reach Trump’s ears. I’m not sure those facts are getting through; likewise, I’d be surprised if Trump has absorbed that the U.S. economy is inching toward a recession because of Tehran’s closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran’s leaders hoped closing the Strait of Hormuz would make financial markets panic, persuading stock market-obsessed Trump to end the war; Iranian Speaker of Parliament Mohamed Bagher Ghalibaf even started tweeting at commodity traders to make sure they got the point. What Tehran didn’t count on was that traders would maintain an almost childlike faith that Trump would end the war quickly, despite mounting evidence to the contrary. Oil futures have, to the point of parody, plummeted after every Trump Truth social post or Axios article hinting at an impending deal. The S&P 500 has repeatedly hit all-time highs in the midst of spiraling global shortages in energy, fertilizer, and critical minerals. Are professional traders really that dumb? Some might be – JP Morgan infamously published an analysis last month premised on the assumption that the war had to end by June 1 (it did not). Others might just understand that while betting on a recession is risky, costly, and tough to time, there’s easy money betting on the rest of the market’s now-predictable reaction to the equally predictable weekly Monday ceasefire headlines.

Trump has so far been pathologically incapable of performing the most rudimentary trust-building steps needed to reach an agreement with Tehran. When the Iranian government announced in April that it would reopen the Strait of Hormuz, expecting Trump to lift his own blockade, Trump read the olive branch as a sign of weakness that meant he should instead double down on aggression, tanking a real opportunity to end the war. His recent short-lived demand that Arab states sign a peace deal with Israel as a precondition to ending the Iran war has been attributed to an obsession with getting a better Iran nuclear deal than Barack Obama did; whatever the cause, his compulsion to score the biggest win possible continues to undermine the chance for any deal at all.

It’s also tough to close a deal when you don’t actually know what you want – and Trump’s declared war aims have changed by the day. That’s why headlines about Iranian nuclear enrichment levels or lifting sanctions as “sticking points” in negotiations are misleading: There probably are not any hard-and-fast sticking points for Trump, who clearly isn’t ideologically committed to the specifics here; it’s not even clear he understands what uranium, which he calls “nuclear dust,” is, and I think he’s telling the truth when he says he’d like to meet Iran’s new supreme leader. The good news is this makes room for flexibility in peace negotiations if Trump actually wants to end the war; the bad news is that no specific Iranian concession is guaranteed to satisfy Trump as long as D.C. Iran hawks have his ear. Marco Rubio continues to demand de facto total disarmament of Iran – a recipe for endless war even if Tehran surrendered its nuclear program – pushing the tortured logic that Tehran must give up its conventional missiles and drones, since those could defend a hypothetical nuclear weapons program should Tehran decide to start one.

Tehran has zero trust in Trump, who has launched no less than two surprise attacks on Iran in the middle of negotiations. They have every reason to expect that Trump will break his word and return to full-scale war against Iran and that they can withstand the U.S. military a second time, and maybe even emerge with a stronger hand after inflicting further losses on U.S. forces and regional partners. There would be little to gain from accepting a deal that doesn’t offer immediate, irreversible payoff like releasing some of the $100 billion in Iranian funds frozen under threat of U.S. sanctions. Recognizing that their blockade of the Strait of Hormuz has failed to sufficiently motivate Trump, Iran’s leaders will likely escalate to try to force him to commit to either a lasting peace or a return to war.

Netanyahu is escalating his war against Hezbollah – and the Lebanese people – in the hope he can forestall Trump making a deal with Tehran, which has vowed that any lasting ceasefire deal must encompass Lebanon as well. Trump did just announce a new ceasefire in Lebanon, which would look like a promising effort to play ball with Tehran – if it were not an essentially one-sided ceasefire that demands Hezbollah, but not Israel, stop shooting. This latest fake Lebanon ceasefire, which Hezbollah has unsurprisingly rejected, will undoubtedly fail to convince Tehran that Trump is taking negotiations seriously and is prepared to seriously rein in Netanyahu.

The external obstacles to peace – the Israeli government’s spoiling attempts, the Iranian government’s hard-learned distrust, gripes from Fox News hawks – probably aren’t insurmountable for Trump, if he decides he really wants a deal. When that happens, he’ll proclaim he got a better deal than Obama did, whatever the truth of the matter is, and his lackeys in his Cabinet, Congress, and aligned media will fall in line like they always do. The problem is that Trump apparently views today’s pseudo-ceasefire double-blockade impasse as a satisfactory solution to a problem he’d rather forget, instead of a ticking time bomb. Unfortunately, it may take a new crisis within this crisis – an unmistakable recession or more U.S. troops killed in the Gulf – to change his mind.

Harrison Mann is a former US Army major and executive officer of the Defense Intelligence Agency’s Middle East/Africa Regional Center who resigned in protest of his office’s support for Israel’s war in Gaza under the Biden administration. He is currently with the group Win Without War and recently became a Zeteo contributor.

item.title: Did the New York Times Just Run an Anti-Platner Hit Job?
item.description: On this day in 1968, New York Senator and presidential candidate Robert F. Kennedy was assassinated at the Ambassador Hotel in Los Angeles. The murder, and that of Martin Luther King, Jr in the spring, precipitated a summer of violence. Good morning, and Happy Friday. Martin here in DC, having…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1968, New York Senator and presidential candidate Robert F. Kennedy was assassinated at the Ambassador Hotel in Los Angeles. The murder, and that of Martin Luther King, Jr in the spring, precipitated a summer of violence.

Good morning, and Happy Friday. Martin here in DC, having somehow conquered the urge to go watch the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool, thanks to Donald Trump now the Dr. Tobias Fünke of grand ceremonial architecture, slowly refill with water. As it was, Trump offered an apt Oval Office

Much more seriously, today’s ‘First Draft’ looks at a tough moment for Graham Platner’s Senate campaign. The insurgent progressive has given Democrats hope of a win in Maine, but Thursday brought a New York Times piece about Platner’s alleged behavior towards women he dated. The piece raised questions not just about Platner but about his chief accuser, and about the Times’s journalistic standards – or lack thereof. We’ll get into it, as well as looking at Rep. Ro Khanna’s attempt to derail a ridiculous pro-Israel measure, the Senate vote-a-rama to pass a $70 billion bill to fund Trump’s immigration crackdown, and more.

Lyndsey Fifield, one of three women who described to the New York Times alleged

In October 2018, amid a fierce fight over Kavanaugh’s nomination, the New York Post reported: “Inez Stepman and Lindsey [sic] Fifield are two millennial women who co-founded the group Ladies for Kavanaugh to show their support for the nominee when they felt that view was being left out of the public discourse. Their day jobs are in conservative politics, Stepman at [the] Independent Women’s Forum and Fifield at the Heritage Foundation. (Their pro-Kavanaugh group was formed on their own time.)”

“In the wake of the baseless, 11th-hour accusations orchestrated to stop Kavanaugh’s confirmation, we couldn’t stay silent anymore,” Fifield told the outlet.

The New York Times, however, was silent about this pretty pertinent part of Fifield’s background when it dropped its big ‘scoop’ on Graham Platner’s “volatile” and “toxic” relationships with former girlfriends, including Fifield, “that were unsettling and at times emotionally wrenching.”

Early on Thursday, UMass Lowell polling on the Maine Senate race showed the Democrat five points up on incumbent Republican Susan Collins – or at least, he was before a recent sexting scandal broke. Then, in the mid-afternoon, the Times launched a long-awaited piece in political circles. The headline: “Several Women Who Dated Graham Platner Recall ‘Unsettling’ Behavior.”

The Times interviewed former dates who spoke favorably of Platner (“gentle giant,” “great boyfriend”), but three women, prominently including Fifield, who dated Platner “from roughly 2013 to 2015,” described Platner as “charming and charismatic… but also demeaning to women and, in at least one case, even physically threatening. He drank heavily and was regularly unfaithful.”

In a statement, Platner “said he had ‘too often self-medicated with alcohol, and was a far from perfect boyfriend’ during… a ‘very dark period of my life,’” after combat tours in Iraq and Afghanistan. He added: “Any characterization beyond that is false, and I believe, politically motivated.”

Platner headed for MS Now to present his defense. As he did so, the Times found itself in some quarters the target of rising anger. Predominantly, observers questioned the motivations of Fifield, and the prominence the Times gave to her claims of abusive behavior that the paper itself said it could not corroborate, even while detailing close cooperation with their source.

Fifield was introduced as “a Virginia conservative who has worked for right-leaning groups and Republican campaigns,” then later said to be “affiliated” with Independent Women, described merely as a “conservative group.” Work for Nikki Haley’s 2024 GOP presidential campaign and for the Heritage Foundation, described only as a “conservative think tank,” were also mentioned.

But not examined or unpacked in any kind of detail. The Heritage Foundation, for example, helped formulate Project 2025 for Donald Trump.

Ryan Grim, of Drop Site News, noted Fifield’s spell co-hosting a podcast, Lady-Brains, with Bethany Mandel, a prominent pro-Israel and right-wing author married to Seth Mandel, also a prominent pro-Israel and right-wing writer. The Times did not mention the link.

Grim also noted other stops in GOP politics, including the American Action Network super PAC and the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, and described Independent Women as “a prominent dark money group best known for helping usher Brett Kavanaugh onto the Supreme Court and giving Susan Collins the talking points she needed to make her decisive speech in his favor.”

The Times did not note this link, and did not mention that Fifield had herself formed the aforementioned Ladies for Kavanaugh group that supported the right-wing justice’s confirmation. Unlike Platner, Kavanaugh was credibly accused of sexual assault.

It was also hard not to notice Thursday that Republican friends and acquaintances applauding Fifield for coming forward about Platner were similarly prominent boosters of Kavanaugh at the time – especially as they, themselves, compared the situation to that of Kavanaugh accuser Christine Blasey Ford.

Elsewhere, outlets including American Prospect surfaced alleged Facebook posts from 2025 in which Fifield, an ardent Zionist, appeared to post false claims about Mahmoud Khalil and Rümeysa Oztürk, students legally in the U.S. nonetheless targeted for deportation by the Trump administration over their anti-Israel speech.

According to screenshots of the alleged post, Fifield claimed Khalil and Oztürk “were not targeted for advocacy against Israel – they are being held accountable for destruction of property, organizing violent actions, making threats to Jewish students, and distributing materials funded by state designated terror groups.”

In reality, Oztürk was arrested by plainclothes ICE agents for co-authoring an op-ed in a Tufts University paper advocating for Palestinian rights. Khalil was arrested by ICE, and detained hundreds of miles away from his wife while she gave birth, for organizing pro-Palestinian protests at Columbia in New York. Neither has ever been charged with any kind of violent or destructive acts.

Clearly, the Times’s in-depth reporting on Platner’s alleged behavior should be taken seriously. Fifield was not the only woman quoted. Jenny Racicot, a “Maine Democrat,” said Platner had been “reckless” and “unsettling” and did not “respect women.” A third woman, anonymous, said she felt like “collateral damage to the world that is his.” Fifield’s allegations of “rough” behavior during arguments were not at all easy to read but, again, it is worth noting that the Times itself conceded that it “reviewed some of Ms. Fifield’s diary entries from after the relationship had ended, and spoke with two of her friends who confirmed that the pair had an emotionally volatile relationship but could not corroborate the physical altercations or the most controversial comments she described.”

The Times also detailed Fifield’s claim that Platner acknowledged the meaning of the “Totenkopf” chest tattoo, obtained as a Marine, that he now says he did not know was a Nazi symbol and has covered up. Fifield was quoted as saying Platner referred to his tattoo as “my totenkopf,” echoing the words of an anonymous acquaintance quoted by Jewish Insider when the story broke last October.

Speaking to MS Now, Platner said, “I think she is that same source – she’s the person who has been telling people this from the beginning.” Bethany Mandel, Fifield’s conservative, pro-Israel friend, seemed to confirm that Thursday too, tweeting: “She came forward with the tottenkompf many months ago,” believing it would be disqualifying. “Unfortunately, that was not a bridge too far, much to our dismay.”

Platner added on MS Now, “Anything alleging physicality, anything alleging that I knew what my tattoo was, these are the statements of someone who is politically motivated.”

Grim also pointed out that Fifield’s descriptions of Platner sharpening an ax while watching TV, keeping an AR-15, and speaking in crude terms about women and rape, while far from edifying or appealing to liberal sensibilities, hardly seemed the kinds of things a GOP operative would think disqualifying in a conservative man. (By the way, what does the Times think is the correct time or place to sharpen one’s ax?)

Does anyone really believe that the usually cautious New York Times would have run a piece making similarly incendiary claims against an establishment politician, based almost wholly on the uncorroborated allegations of a single source who just happened to be a long-standing activist from the opposing party? Is it any wonder that so many people online see this article as a hit job against an anti-oligarchy, anti-Israel populist?

Contacted for comment, a Times spokesperson defended “a deeply reported piece of journalism that draws from conversations with more than 20 people, including three women who dated Graham Platner and described his past actions toward them as unsettling.”

Fifield's “professional history and partisan affiliation,” the spokesperson said, “is made clear to readers in the reporting. She relayed her personal experiences with Mr. Platner from 2013-2015, as one source among several."

Still, surely any reasonable reader of the story, and subsequent reporting about its chief source, might digest allegations against Platner with concern, but also say to themselves some version of what Platner’s campaign said to the Times.

“Let’s be very clear: [Fifield] is a lifelong GOP operative who’s dedicated her career to electing Republicans.”

  • Vote vex:Calling the House war powers resolution that passed on Wednesday “meaningless,” Donald Trumpsaidit came “right in the middle of my final negotiations to end the War with the Islamic Republic of Iran” and rendered its backers “unpatriotic.”
  • Iran defiant:TehransaidForeign Minister Abbas Araghchi spoke by phone to Khalil Al-Hayya, head of Hamas in Gaza, to reaffirm Tehran’s support and demands for the cessation of Israeli attacks there and in Lebanon as a condition for ending the war.
  • No change:Three months after the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran, the UN nuclear watchdog, the International Atomic Energy Agency,madeno major changes to its assessment of Iran’s nuclear ambitions and capabilities.
  • ‘Hitting tank bottom’:Oil executives have told the Trump administration that the ongoing closure of the Strait of Hormuz means global energy prices will surge in the next several weeks,Politicoreported. The White House denied it.
  • Lebanon latest:Rejectingthat new ceasefire, reached in DC, Hezbollah leader Naim Kassem called the agreement “absurd, humiliating and insulting.” In DC, Trumpsaid, “it would be really nice if Lebanon could have some peace.” Israel, meanwhile, continued its strikes and forced displacement,killingat least five people today. The death toll since March hastoppedmore than 3,525, per Lebanese health officials.

On Thursday, Rep. Ro Khanna of California attempted to strip from the National Defense Authorization Act Section 224, which he and other critics say allows for unprecedented integration between the U.S. and Israeli militaries.

“We should have American sovereignty,” Khanna said, in a House Armed Services Committee mark-up session, “and make it clear that we strike 224. If we want to give aid to Israel, if we want to sell them weapons, that should be a vote for the entire Congress.”

In impassioned remarks, Khanna referred to a truth largely unacknowledged by other panel members: that U.S. public support for military aid to Israel, let alone closer co-operation, has deteriorated significantly amid Israel’s genocidal war in Gaza, the U.S. and Israel’s illegal war on Iran, and Israel’s brutal Lebanon campaign.

Just last month, the New York Times and Siena College found that 57% of Americans oppose aid to Israel.

Voters “want less cooperation and blank checks to Israel, not more,” Khanna said. “Only the United States Congress would dream up at this moment, ‘Let’s actually do more for Israel, not less,’ and that’s what Section 224 does.”

It was to no avail. Just one Democrat backed Khanna’s play. Sarah Jacobs, also from California, said: “If any other country… had been credibly accused of violating U.S. and international law again and again, of killing tens of thousands of civilians, of blocking food and medicine from reaching a starving population, we would not be moving to deepen and permanently expand our military ties with them. And yet that is exactly what Section 224 of this bill proposes.”

The amendment went down by voice vote. The effort isn’t over: Rep. Thomas Massie, Khanna’s frequent Republican ally, has said he will try to defeat Section 224 on the House floor.

  • Funding Trump’s crackdown:Senate Republicans early this morningpassedtheir $70 billion bill to fund Trump’s immigration crackdown through the end of the president’s term. The vote came after Republicans defeated attempts by Democrats, with support from some GOP senators, to insert language banning Trump’s$1.776 slush fundand barring the use of federal funds or private donations for the president’s ballroom without congressional approval. The bill now heads to the House.
  • Blanche blast:Senate Minority Leader Chuck SchumersaidDemocrats opposed Todd Blanche as attorney general, calling the acting AG “loyal to the president alone, not the constitution, not to the rule of law and certainly not to the American people.” In released testimony, fired AG Pam BondisaidBlanche “was in charge of the process and the entire release of the Epstein files.”
  • Bolton plea:Former Trump national security adviser John Boltonreportedlyagreed to plead guilty to mishandling classified information in writing a memoir. Prosecuted after Trump’s return to power, he faces a $2.25 million fine and potentially five years in prison.
  • ICE cold:In a memo to employeesobtainedby theWashington Post, acting ICE director David Venturella said the agency was eliminating its requirement to report deaths occurring within 30 days of people being released from its custody.
  • Centrist duds:Democratic Reps. Tom Suozzi and Adam Gray willintroducea pledge including the phrase “we are capitalist, not socialist,” in answer to the rise of left-wingers such as Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez and New York mayor Zohran Mamdani.
  • AIPAC attacked:Pro-Israel group AIPACfacedquestions about why it did not try to defeat Adam Hamawy, the progressive who won Tuesday’s Democratic primary in New Jersey’s deep blue 12th district. A spokesperson said AIPAC did not see a way to win.
  • On the fly:“Flesh-eating parasites” can be crossed off 2026 bingo cards, withscrewwormdetected in Texas. With beef prices already sky high, andDOGE cutshaving affected defenses against the problem, Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollinssaidofficials “do not believe this will be an infestation.”

How, according to Michael Wolff, did then Fox News supremo Roger Ailes memorably describe John Bolton after the 2016 election, when pushing the mustachioed war hawk for national security adviser in the first Trump administration?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:The death toll from Israeli strikes since the so-called “ceasefire” went into effect in October neared 950 after Israelkilledat least one Palestinian in attacks on tents sheltering forcibly displaced people in Khan Younis. At least 16 others were injured.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    Talks offer:In his first public message written to Vladimir Putin since Russia invaded in 2022, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskycalledfor face-to-face talks in a neutral country, in part because the U.S. under Donald Trump is not paying attention.
  • 🇨🇺 Cuba threat:Trumprepeatedhis threat to invade Cuba, telling reporters Havana had already “sort of collapsed” under the U.S. blockade. “I like to do one thing at a time,” Trump said. “We’ll take care of the Islamic Republic of Iran, and as soon as that is done, on our way back we’ll just make a brief stopover.”

  • 🇨🇴

    Lawyering up:Facing U.S. drug trafficking charges, former Colombian President Nicolás Maduroretainedan attorney who helped rapper Sean “Diddy” Combs beat charges of sex trafficking and racketeering. Combs went to prison on lesser counts.

  • 🇸🇴

    Somalia violence: Opposition party supportersclashedwith government security forces in the Somali capital, Mogadishu, for a second day, with heavy gunfire and explosions. Several buildings caught fire before the fighting subsided.


In the Oval Office, Donald Trump meandered off into a discussion of two of his favorite subjects: the supposed threat posed by trans kids, and the extreme attractiveness of male pro athletes. It is Pride Month, after all.

‘You Either Leave Right Now or You Die’ – Israel’s Ethnic Cleansing of a Village in Lebanon

Lylla Younes, a reporter based in Beirut, files a shattering dispatch for Drop Site News, about how Israeli soldiers forced residents of a Lebanese village out of their homes in minutes. Considering talks in Washington to end the war, one displaced resident says: “Nothing is coming from these negotiations. We go to sleep to death and wake up to death. They’ve destroyed everything. Tell me, what more do they want? What is left?”

How Pakistan is Using the Iran War to Reinvent Itself

For the New Yorker, Sudarsan Raghavan considers how the military regime in Islamabad has “evolved from something close to a pariah to a peacemaker,” by attempting to broker an end to Donald Trump’s illegal war. The gist: Pakistan understands that under Trump, “diplomacy has become more personal and transactional,” and therefore its leaders “flatter him shamelessly” and “present an image he likes,” to which they, as uniformed strongmen, are particularly suited.

Bill Pulte Was a ‘Degenerate’ Buffoon. Now He’s Head of U.S. Intelligence

Will Sommer of The Bulwark considers the bizarre rise of Donald Trump’s acting director of national intelligence, from property scion to “Twitter philanthropist”, meme-stock maven, Mar-a-Lago member, and MAGA mercenary. The following is a real sentence about U.S. politics in 2026: “The supposed Bed Bath & Beyond oracle who watched a dildo get slapped across a man’s face is now set to have access to the country’s most sensitive intelligence matters.”

Zeteo’s Andrew Perez contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Did the New York Times Just Run an Anti-Platner Hit Job?

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    Talks offer:In his first public message written to Vladimir Putin since Russia invaded in 2022, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskycalledfor face-to-face talks in a neutral country, in part because the U.S. under Donald Trump is not paying attention.
  • 🇨🇺 Cuba threat:Trumprepeatedhis threat to invade Cuba, telling reporters Havana had already “sort of collapsed” under the U.S. blockade. “I like to do one thing at a time,” Trump said. “We’ll take care of the Islamic Republic of Iran, and as soon as that is done, on our way back we’ll just make a brief stopover.”

  • 🇨🇴

    Lawyering up:Facing U.S. drug trafficking charges, former Colombian President Nicolás Maduroretainedan attorney who helped rapper Sean “Diddy” Combs beat charges of sex trafficking and racketeering. Combs went to prison on lesser counts.

  • 🇸🇴

    Somalia violence: Opposition party supportersclashedwith government security forces in the Somali capital, Mogadishu, for a second day, with heavy gunfire and explosions. Several buildings caught fire before the fighting subsided.


In the Oval Office, Donald Trump meandered off into a discussion of two of his favorite subjects: the supposed threat posed by trans kids, and the extreme attractiveness of male pro athletes. It is Pride Month, after all.

‘You Either Leave Right Now or You Die’ – Israel’s Ethnic Cleansing of a Village in Lebanon

Lylla Younes, a reporter based in Beirut, files a shattering dispatch for Drop Site News, about how Israeli soldiers forced residents of a Lebanese village out of their homes in minutes. Considering talks in Washington to end the war, one displaced resident says: “Nothing is coming from these negotiations. We go to sleep to death and wake up to death. They’ve destroyed everything. Tell me, what more do they want? What is left?”

How Pakistan is Using the Iran War to Reinvent Itself

For the New Yorker, Sudarsan Raghavan considers how the military regime in Islamabad has “evolved from something close to a pariah to a peacemaker,” by attempting to broker an end to Donald Trump’s illegal war. The gist: Pakistan understands that under Trump, “diplomacy has become more personal and transactional,” and therefore its leaders “flatter him shamelessly” and “present an image he likes,” to which they, as uniformed strongmen, are particularly suited.

Bill Pulte Was a ‘Degenerate’ Buffoon. Now He’s Head of U.S. Intelligence

Will Sommer of The Bulwark considers the bizarre rise of Donald Trump’s acting director of national intelligence, from property scion to “Twitter philanthropist”, meme-stock maven, Mar-a-Lago member, and MAGA mercenary. The following is a real sentence about U.S. politics in 2026: “The supposed Bed Bath & Beyond oracle who watched a dildo get slapped across a man’s face is now set to have access to the country’s most sensitive intelligence matters.”

Zeteo’s Andrew Perez contributed to this newsletter.

item.title: 7 Things To Know About Trump’s Ridiculous New MAGA Intel Chief
item.description: Donald Trump has named Bill Pulte – a 38-year-old housing regulator and staunch MAGA loyalist – as his acting director of national intelligence (DNI), after Tulsi Gabbard announced she would be stepping down. It’s a pick that’s baffling even some Republicans , who point out Pulte’s glaring lack of…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Donald Trump has named Bill Pulte – a 38-year-old housing regulator and staunch MAGA loyalist – as his acting director of national intelligence (DNI), after Tulsi Gabbard announced she would be stepping down.

It’s a pick that’s baffling even some Republicans, who point out Pulte’s glaring lack of an intelligence background. But like it or not, in a few short weeks, Pulte is set to start his post atop America’s 18 intel agencies.

Here are seven things to know about the man who is possibly the most unqualified Trump pick ever – and how he got here.

Pulte has never worked in an intelligence agency, never served in the military, nor has he held a national security post. North Carolina Republican Senator Thom Tillis says he doubts Pulte has ever even held a security clearance.

Unlike with the numerous other positions in the federal government the Trump administration has staffed with first-timer public servants, federal law states that anyone nominated as director of national intelligence “shall have extensive national security expertise,” which is a standard that also binds the deputy meant to run the office in a vacancy. Regardless, Trump installed him anyway, in an “acting” capacity – a move meant to sidestep a Senate confirmation vote Pulte would likely lose. In a House Foreign Affairs Committee hearing Wednesday, Trump Secretary of State Marco Rubio acknowledged he had never heard Pulte’s name brought up “In the context of intelligence.”

So why did Trump install Pulte? The president’s stated qualification is that Pulte already oversees trillions in the mortgage market at Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, as though running mortgage giants is interchangeable with running 18 spy agencies. Speaking of…

In his Truth Social announcement, Trump wrote that, in addition to serving as DNI, Pulte “will remain Director of the Federal Housing Finance Agency and Chairman of Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac.” That means Pulte will be splitting his time between overseeing the nation’s most guarded secrets, and an $81.9 billion intelligence budget across 18 agencies, while also heading the apparatus underwriting over $10 trillion in American mortgages.

There’s no precedent for this. The DNI role was created after 9/11 as a full-time job coordinating the intelligence agencies; the Fannie/Freddie conservatorship is itself a massive portfolio that prior directors treated as plenty.

Pulte, in his capacity as director of the Federal Housing Finance Agency (FHFA), used agency mortgage data to file criminal referrals against a roster of Trump foes, including Senator Adam Schiff, New York Attorney General Letitia James, and Federal Reserve governor Lisa Cook. The Schiff referral never produced charges – and a federal grand jury is now investigating how the case itself was handled, reportedly scrutinizing Pulte’s own role. James was indicted on bank fraud charges after Pulte’s campaign, though the case was later dismissed. Cook’s referral became Trump’s basis for trying to fire her. Pulte insisted it was apolitical, claiming the Cook case just “came across our desk.”

In a November letter to the Trump administration, eight Democratic senators cited public reports calling Pulte’s referral process “bizarre” and “unheard of,” since similar referrals normally run through the agency’s inspector general, not its director. A month later, the Government Accountability Office opened a formal probe into whether Pulte misused federal authority and resources.

When going after Lisa Cook, Pulte posted the referral on Twitter, including zip codes and part of her home address, and repeatedly published images of four of her signed mortgage checks. Filing a third referral, he tweeted, “3 strikes and you’re out.” Mortgage experts told American Banker that a director firing off dozens of posts about “occupancy fraud,” a real but rarely prosecuted violation, is extraordinarily unusual. Cook’s attorney called it “an obvious smear campaign,” arguing Pulte had posted about her 30-plus times in two days before any review of the facts.

According to Semafor, the notorious and longtime Trump ally Roger Stone was among the key hardline MAGA forces who pushed Trump to tap Pulte for the role. The Wall Street Journal similarly reported Stone advocated for Pulte to get the DNI job.

Stone was convicted in 2019 of lying to Congress, obstruction, and witness tampering in the probe relating to Russian interference in the 2016 election, then pardoned by Trump at the end of his first term. So the man deciding what gets declassified was handpicked, in part, by someone convicted of lying to Congress about a foreign-interference investigation.

When Pulte was first selected, the Wall Street Journal noted the decision “was met with skepticism from GOP lawmakers and, privately, some Trump administration officials.”
At a Senate Finance Committee hearing this week, Senator Thom Tillis pressed Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, who has feuded with Pulte for months over privatizing Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, on reports that the two had nearly come to blows. Tillis asked whether he’d really threatened to punch Pulte in the face. Bessent corrected the record, explaining that he “actually said I was going to kick his ass.”

“Good. I share the emotion,” the retiring senator replied.
Tillis is not the only GOP senator displeased with Tillis’s hiring. Republican Senate Majority Leader John Thune said, “We don’t need a weaponized DNI. We need professionals there.” Senator Mitch McConnell vowed that no nominee lacking the legally required experience “will earn my vote.” Senator Susan Collins said she, too, doesn’t know whether Pulte holds a security clearance.

Before joining the administration, Pulte was a fixture of the “meme stock” community, incessantly promoting Bed Bath & Beyond shares ($BBBY) even after the retailer filed for bankruptcy in 2023. He said he was “captivated” by the “incredible BBBY community,” appearing on podcasts and at events promoting the stock – sometimes in a bulletproof vest, out of an apparent fear that a shadowy cabal might try to kill him.

Unfortunately for the BBBY community, the company stayed dead: a number of believers never recovered their money, and the stock was delisted. A Pulte representative later told Rolling Stone he “never told anyone privately or publicly to buy the stock.”

As the Associated Press put it, before Pulte went after Trump’s enemies, “he practiced on his own family.” In a years-long feud over PulteGroup, the homebuilding empire his grandfather founded, he accused his grandfather’s widow of insider trading; Bill Pulte was allegedly the driving force behind a website branding his aunt as a “fake Christian,” and called another relative a “fat slob,” “weirdo,” and “grifter,” per court records.

When the family’s stake dropped below the threshold guaranteeing a board seat, the company removed Pulte in 2020. The family’s charitable foundation later clarified publicly that Pulte does not speak for them “in any capacity.”

So can Pulte keep the DNI job? On the merits, he shouldn’t be able to – even GOP Majority Leader John Thune says the country needs professionals, not a “weaponized” DNI.

However, the administration can attempt to skirt Senate approval entirely by keeping Pulte as the “acting” director for some time. The GAO is already investigating how Pulte turned one agency against Trump’s enemies – now we wait to see what he does with 18 intel agencies.

Zeteo’s Andrew McDonald contributed to this story.

item.title: Mehdi Goes Head-to-Head With Patrick Bet-David on Trump, Iran, Muslims, and Ilhan Omar
item.description: Zeteo’s editor-in-chief Mehdi Hasan and Trump-supporting conservative media personality and podcast host Patrick Bet-David disagree on a lot of things, but when Mehdi appeared on PBD’s latest show, the two managed to have a wide-ranging, mostly civil conversation – until it came to immigration,…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Zeteo’s editor-in-chief Mehdi Hasan and Trump-supporting conservative media personality and podcast host Patrick Bet-David disagree on a lot of things, but when Mehdi appeared on PBD’s latest show, the two managed to have a wide-ranging, mostly civil conversation – until it came to immigration, Muslims, and Ilhan Omar.

During the conversation, Mehdi and Bet-David, who was born in Iran, also discuss the Trump administration’s decision to effectively dismantle the asylum process for all refugees except white South Africans.

“Number one, that’s racist. 99.9% of your refugees are white people who are not refugees and you don’t allow in people from Iran, Iraq, Syria,” Mehdi says. “The second point is, Donald Trump wouldn’t have allowed you into this country, and yet you sit and support him.”

Watch the full video above to see Mehdi fact-check Bet-David on Trump’s so-called “landslide” victory in 2024, and point out that the president “got one of the narrowest wins in modern American history.” He also presses Bet-David about his praise for Trump on day one of the Iran war and asks if the podcaster accepts that it’s been an “absolute disaster,” noting that Trump “is not a war leader” and “he doesn’t understand foreign policy.”


Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: LIVESTREAM TICKETS for Zeteo and The Guardian’s Special Event in London
item.description: Young Turks founder Cenk Uygur and streamer Hasan Piker have been banned from the UK for their criticism of Israel, while Benjamin Netanyahu has ordered a deeper incursion into Lebanon, despite a so-called ceasefire. What does the crackdown on free speech in the UK mean for the state of liberal…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Young Turks founder Cenk Uygur and streamer Hasan Piker have been banned from the UK for their criticism of Israel, while Benjamin Netanyahu has ordered a deeper incursion into Lebanon, despite a so-called ceasefire.

What does the crackdown on free speech in the UK mean for the state of liberal democracy, and how does Israel’s assault on Lebanon threaten a “rules-based international order?”

For the first time ever, Zeteo is partnering with The Guardian for a very special and unique LIVE event in London on June 8, titled, ‘The Assault on Freedom.’ Mehdi will sit down for a riveting and timely conversation with The Guardian’s Arwa Mahdawi to unpack the alarming rise of populism and nationalism, and the global implications of the current seismic shifts in geopolitics.

Even if you don’t live in London or can’t make it in-person, you won’t have to miss a moment of this historic event. Livestream tickets are available now for the show, which airs at 7:30pm BST (2:30pm ET / 11:30am PT).

Not only will you be able to watch the event in real time – those who purchase these tickets will have exclusive access to a video of the entire event for several weeks afterwards.

Get Your Tickets Here

item.title: 😥 Team Trump Under ‘Maximum’ Pressure to Jail More of His Foes
item.description: On this day in 1919, Congress passed the 19th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, which, after ratification by the states, finally gave women the right to vote. We now have a defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, whose church network says that right should be taken away. Here’s to a lovely Thursday…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1919, Congress passed the 19th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, which, after ratification by the states, finally gave women the right to vote. We now have a defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, whose church network says that right should be taken away.

Here’s to a lovely Thursday morning: ☕ ☕ It’s Swin, again. Last month, we told you how the Pentagon has been put under a shocking amount of pressure by the White House and by higher-ups at the Pete Hegseth-fronted Defense Department to essentially cook its own Iran war data and twist its own intel before it is presented to Donald Trump. Because why hurt the Mad King president’s feelings with numbers that show he’s losing his illegal war?

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we take a look at other parts of the U.S. government that Trump and his White House are coaxing with a very simple message: the boss will be monumentally livid at you if you don’t get very serious – very soon – about jailing his political enemies. Elsewhere, Rashida Tlaib’s Lebanon war powers push infuriates Republicans while a senior Democrat warns about Bill Pulte’s election danger.

Two months ago, Donald Trump fired then-Attorney General Pam Bondi, because she wasn’t corrupt or zealously authoritarian enough for his liking. With her fall rose the acting AG, Todd Blanche, yet another of Trump’s former personal lawyers turned federalized hatchetmen. Off the bat, the Trump White House, including the president himself, made something clear to Blanche in private discussions, according to people familiar with the matter.

“You cannot fuck up like Pam,” one Trump adviser tells me, paraphrasing the president’s stern message to the nation’s top law enforcement official.

The sources say that so far, Trump has been largely pleased with Blanche’s performance; after all, he currently intends to nominate Blanche to serve permanently. However, administration officials also tell me that Trump’s goodwill will quickly evaporate, if (or more likely, when) the new attorney general’s crusade against Trump’s foes begins to stall.

People in and close to the Trump White House all describe a president placing enormous pressure on Bondi’s successor to crank the authoritarian onslaught up to 11, with Trump routinely peppering senior officials about where his “Todd” is at on specific prosecutions or investigations. One senior Trump administration official calls it a “maximum amount” of pressure.

The president had different gripes about Bondi’s time at the helm at Main Justice, including her handling of the Jeffrey Epstein debacle and cover-up. But the sources say Trump, in his demands that Blanche not “fuck up like Pam,” was specifically referring to her perceived sporadic unease with the president’s sustained pressure campaign on the Justice Department to prosecute his political enemies.

Bondi certainly tried, and her time atop the department saw its rapid transformation into a wholly owned subsidiary of President Trump’s whims and spite, as he ordered up from the Oval Office politically motivated, obviously frivolous criminal probes of his handpicked targets.

But whether fairly or not, whenever those prosecutions lost momentum as Bondi ran into inevitable constitutional roadblocks, and therefore wasn’t able to turn the United States of today into the Argentina of the 1970s, the president and some of his closest allies blamed her for such failures. They did not blame Trump’s own boneheaded authoritarianism. They didn’t blame the fact that they, despite Trump and the GOP’s best efforts, still operate in a largely democratic environment, in which the president doesn’t just get to automatically jail his opponents simply because they pissed him off.

Bondi had more than a year in the gig. Blanche has been there a fraction of that time. It’s only a matter of time before he slams full force into some of the same inconveniences (like, really good laws) that his predecessor did. But until then, the acting AG wants Trump, and the whole world, to know that he’s willing to be as corrupt and vindictive as the worst of them.

This is why, in recent weeks, you’ve likely noticed an onrush of Justice Department activity that can only be described as operating on the legal principle, Lawyer Intentionally Lobotomizes Himself So He Can Try To Make The God-Emperor Smile.

Blanche is ringleading Trump’s hyper-aggressive investigations into who leaked sensitive information to the media, the president having personally pressured him to get serious about making reporters choose between jail or betraying their sources. The Blanche-led DOJ was willing to file criminal charges against James Comey over a seashells Instagram post that the White House ludicrously claims meant the former FBI chief was calling for Trump’s death. The Blanche-led DOJ is criminally investigating a Chicago-based group that paid some of the legal bills of columnist E. Jean Carroll, who accused Trump of sexual abuse. In his first month on the job, Blanche announced criminal charges against the Southern Poverty Law Center, on the ridiculous legal theory (if you can call it that) that paying informants to snitch on white supremacists is the same as actively bankrolling the hate groups. Additionally, acting AG Blanche has publicly suggested that Trump’s mission to jail an array of his enemies isn’t some glaringly obvious sign of severe democratic backsliding – it is, according to the country’s top lawman, the sacred “ duty” of the supposed leader of the free world.

If Blanche and Trump get their way, this is just the start. One senior White House official tells me that when it comes to the Blanche-led blitzes against the president’s adversaries big and small, there’s “more to come” and that “Todd is a great man who knows what he’s doing.”

When I asked why it was at all appropriate (quaint, I know) for the White House to know what a nominally independent Department of Justice had in store for Trump’s enemies, I… didn’t get a reply.

I’m not holding my breath. But you all know the answer.

  • Better late than never:The House finallypasseda war powers resolution to halt military action against Iran, 215-208. Four Republicans voted yes. If the Senate passes such a measure, a historic face-off with the White House will follow.
  • ‘Pretty hard’:In House testimony,Marco Rubio said the war had “concluded.” But Trumpsaidthe U.S. had been “hitting [Iran] pretty hard,” adding: “In that part of the world, a ceasefire is when you’re shooting in a more moderate manner.”
  • Cost of war:Oil pricesroseon news of tit-for-tat strikes, while U.S. stocks fell.
  • Tehran terms:Iran reportedly wanted a proper ceasefire in Lebanon before resuming talks. Trumpconfirmedreports he recently called Israeli PM Benjamin Netanyahu “crazy” over attacks on Lebanon and threats to its capital, Beirut.
  • Lebanon latest:In Washington on Wednesday evening, Israeli and Lebanese officialsannouncedthey had agreed to renew the ceasefire. Hours later, Israel’s defense minister said the country wouldcontinueto strike Lebanon and wouldn’t withdraw from the south. The Health Ministryputthe death toll from such strikes at more than 3,515 since March.
  • Hill bill:In DC, it wasrevealedthat the current Senate Intelligence Authorization Act contains provisions to increase intelligence sharing with Israel and block any effort to reduce such co-operation.

Michigan Democratic Rep. Rashida Tlaib was at the center of House drama as her attempt to introduce a Lebanon war powers resolution and limit U.S. aid to Israel in its war on the country led a Republican to attack her character and, therefore, find himself banned from speaking.

Tlaib, the only Palestinian American in Congress, does not have her party’s full backing as she takes on such boorish opponents. Supported by fellow progressives, her resolution is opposed by so-called moderate Democrats.

Nonetheless, her aim is to at least force a vote on stopping U.S. support for Israel in a campaign the Netanyahu government says is against the Iran proxy group Hezbollah, which has fought back, but which Lebanese authorities say has killed more than 3,500, including over 200 children, as Israel has ignored a supposed ceasefire.

A new ceasefire was announced late Wednesday. A vote on Tlaib’s resolution was likely on Thursday.

On the House floor on Wednesday, Rep. Max Miller of Ohio said: “Hezbollah is a terrorist organization… and its members are butchers that you like to hang out with, to a certain extent.”

As Tlaib protested, Miller patronized her: “Oh, I’m sorry, are we getting a little emotional?” adding, “I certainly believe you advocate for [Hezbollah]. Yes, you advocate for terrorists on a daily basis.”

When presiding Republican Rep. Jay Obernolte managed to be heard, he said: “Is the gentlewoman from Michigan making a motion to take down?”

Tlaib said she was, adding: “That is a direct attack on my character.”

Obernolte engaged in a lengthy consultation before ruling that Miller had “impugn[ed] the patriotism and loyalty of the member of the House, which is not in order,” and prohibiting him from speaking for the rest of the day.

Rep. Brian Mast, another pro-Israel bigot in the House GOP caucus, then read a statement from Miller, saying: “Yes, I said it. I own it. I stand by it.”

In her own remarks, Tlaib said 11 children were killed every 24 hours in Lebanon. She also said Israel’s campaign in Lebanon was “not national security, Mr. Speaker. This is an ethnic cleansing campaign.”

  • Fund farce:Trumpsaidhis $1.776 billion “Anti-Weaponization” slush fund, which acting Attorney General Todd Blanchesaidon Tuesday had been dropped, “was a beautiful thing,” adding: “I love it. I think it’s so important.” Blanche has said he will not put in writing that the fund willnotbe created.
  • Off message:The Trump Presidential LibrarytoldtheWashington Postit could not find any Twitter DMs sent during Trump’s first term. If such messages were deleted, the paper said, Trump “may have violated the Presidential Records Act.”
  • Seeing pink:GOP Rep. Anna Paulina Lunaclaimedshe was “actually smacked” by Medea Benjamin of Code Pink, said she was filing charges, and demanded Speaker Mike Johnson ban the anti-war group from the U.S. House.Videoshowed the claim to be not just demonstrably false but farcical. Luna hastriedto have Code Pink banned before.
  • Maine event:Presumptive Democratic Senate candidate Graham Platner told senators in a closed-door meeting that no more damaging allegations were coming, whether of sexual misconduct or assault, theWall Street Journalreported.
  • Right’s wrong:Acceptance of LGBTQ+ people is falling, and the decline is being driven by the GOP. Since 2022, Gallupsaid, “the share [of Republicans] who say such relations are morally acceptable has fallen 21 points to 35%.”
  • Hate tweet:As if to prove a point, Tennessee GOP Rep. Andy Oglestweeted“Homosexuality has no place in America,” deleted it, then blamed a staffer.

Opposition to Trump’s extraordinary appointment of housing official-cum-MAGA warrior Bill Pulte as acting director of national intelligence could soon weaken actual U.S intelligence gathering, as Democrats threaten to delay renewal of a key section of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act, covering surveillance of targets outside the U.S.

Mark Warner, ranking Democrat on the Senate Intelligence Committee, is pushing Republicans to pressure Trump to withdraw Pulte’s appointment, to ensure FISA passage.

On Wednesday, Warner also warned that Pulte might help Trump interfere in the midterms.

Speaking to NPR, Warner said: “Acting folks get six months runway” before needing Senate confirmation. “If we can’t convince the president to change his mind, six months… will keep [Pulte] in the job until after the elections.

“I was extraordinarily concerned about the former director of national intelligence, Tulsi Gabbard, interfering in our election. She was the one who showed up, I believe inappropriately and illegally, on a criminal investigation, seizing ballots in Fulton County, Georgia.

“But she had some national security background. She’d been in the military. She served in Congress. I would doubt Mr. Pulte could even name the multiple intelligence agencies that exist.”

Donald Trump’s DMs may be missing, but what did Richard Nixon want to do to some records of his tumultuous presidency, thereby prompting Congress to pass the Presidential Records Act of 1978?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israelkilledat least 11 Palestinians, including several children, in overnight strikes on residential buildings and other areas of Gaza. Israeli PM Benjamin Netanyahu told CNBC that Hamas had to disarm for the strikes to stop. Hamas told Reuters that Israel should stop striking Gaza.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    Infrastructure strike:Ukrainian long-range droneshitan oil terminal in St. Petersburg as the Russian city prepared to host an economic forum.
  • 🇨🇩 Ebola news:DR Congo’s military said 16 people werekilledin an attack by an Islamic State-affiliated group, in an area affected by the ongoing Ebola outbreak. Congo reported 344 cases of the disease, resulting in 60 deaths.
  • 🇨🇺 Castro celebrated:Former Cuban President Raul Castrocelebratedhis 95th birthday, two weeks after the U.S. indicted him over the downing of planes flown by Cuban exiles in 1996, and with a U.S. blockade biting. President Miguel Diaz-Canel praised Castro for “service to the Homeland, to regional and world peace.”


Nothing much to see here: just Democratic Rep. Ted Lieu guiding Marco Rubio into clearly lying to Congress, via footage of Donald Trump sleeping next to his Secretary of State during Cabinet meetings. Rubio denied that the big boss had ever done such a thing.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: 😥 Team Trump Under ‘Maximum’ Pressure to Jail More of His Foes
item.description: On this day in 1919, Congress passed the 19th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, which, after ratification by the states, finally gave women the right to vote. We now have a defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, whose church network says that right should be taken away. Here’s to a lovely Thursday…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:

The Blanche-led DOJ is criminally investigating a Chicago-based group that paid some of the legal bills of columnist E. Jean Carroll, who accused Trump of sexual abuse. In his first month on the job, Blanche announced criminal charges against the Southern Poverty Law Center, on the ridiculous legal theory (if you can call it that) that paying informants to snitch on white supremacists is the same as actively bankrolling the hate groups. Additionally, acting AG Blanche has publicly suggested that Trump’s mission to jail an array of his enemies isn’t some glaringly obvious sign of severe democratic backsliding – it is, according to the country’s top lawman, the sacred “ duty” of the supposed leader of the free world.

If Blanche and Trump get their way, this is just the start. One senior White House official tells me that when it comes to the Blanche-led blitzes against the president’s adversaries big and small, there’s “more to come” and that “Todd is a great man who knows what he’s doing.”

When I asked why it was at all appropriate (quaint, I know) for the White House to know what a nominally independent Department of Justice had in store for Trump’s enemies, I… didn’t get a reply.

I’m not holding my breath. But you all know the answer.

  • Better late than never:The House finallypasseda war powers resolution to halt military action against Iran, 215-208. Four Republicans voted yes. If the Senate passes such a measure, a historic face-off with the White House will follow.
  • ‘Pretty hard’:In House testimony,Marco Rubio said the war had “concluded.” But Trumpsaidthe U.S. had been “hitting [Iran] pretty hard,” adding: “In that part of the world, a ceasefire is when you’re shooting in a more moderate manner.”
  • Cost of war:Oil pricesroseon news of tit-for-tat strikes, while U.S. stocks fell.
  • Tehran terms:Iran reportedly wanted a proper ceasefire in Lebanon before resuming talks. Trumpconfirmedreports he recently called Israeli PM Benjamin Netanyahu “crazy” over attacks on Lebanon and threats to its capital, Beirut.
  • Lebanon latest:In Washington on Wednesday evening, Israeli and Lebanese officialsannouncedthey had agreed to renew the ceasefire. Hours later, Israel’s defense minister said the country wouldcontinueto strike Lebanon and wouldn’t withdraw from the south. The Health Ministryputthe death toll from such strikes at more than 3,515 since March.
  • Hill bill:In DC, it wasrevealedthat the current Senate Intelligence Authorization Act contains provisions to increase intelligence sharing with Israel and block any effort to reduce such co-operation.

Michigan Democratic Rep. Rashida Tlaib was at the center of House drama as her attempt to introduce a Lebanon war powers resolution and limit U.S. aid to Israel in its war on the country led a Republican to attack her character and, therefore, find himself banned from speaking.

Tlaib, the only Palestinian American in Congress, does not have her party’s full backing as she takes on such boorish opponents. Supported by fellow progressives, her resolution is opposed by so-called moderate Democrats.

Nonetheless, her aim is to at least force a vote on stopping U.S. support for Israel in a campaign the Netanyahu government says is against the Iran proxy group Hezbollah, which has fought back, but which Lebanese authorities say has killed more than 3,500, including over 200 children, as Israel has ignored a supposed ceasefire.

A new ceasefire was announced late Wednesday. A vote on Tlaib’s resolution was likely on Thursday.

On the House floor on Wednesday, Rep. Max Miller of Ohio said: “Hezbollah is a terrorist organization… and its members are butchers that you like to hang out with, to a certain extent.”

As Tlaib protested, Miller patronized her: “Oh, I’m sorry, are we getting a little emotional?” adding, “I certainly believe you advocate for [Hezbollah]. Yes, you advocate for terrorists on a daily basis.”

When presiding Republican Rep. Jay Obernolte managed to be heard, he said: “Is the gentlewoman from Michigan making a motion to take down?”

Tlaib said she was, adding: “That is a direct attack on my character.”

Obernolte engaged in a lengthy consultation before ruling that Miller had “impugn[ed] the patriotism and loyalty of the member of the House, which is not in order,” and prohibiting him from speaking for the rest of the day.

Rep. Brian Mast, another pro-Israel bigot in the House GOP caucus, then read a statement from Miller, saying: “Yes, I said it. I own it. I stand by it.”

In her own remarks, Tlaib said 11 children were killed every 24 hours in Lebanon. She also said Israel’s campaign in Lebanon was “not national security, Mr. Speaker. This is an ethnic cleansing campaign.”

  • Fund farce:Trumpsaidhis $1.776 billion “Anti-Weaponization” slush fund, which acting Attorney General Todd Blanchesaidon Tuesday had been dropped, “was a beautiful thing,” adding: “I love it. I think it’s so important.” Blanche has said he will not put in writing that the fund willnotbe created.
  • Off message:The Trump Presidential LibrarytoldtheWashington Postit could not find any Twitter DMs sent during Trump’s first term. If such messages were deleted, the paper said, Trump “may have violated the Presidential Records Act.”
  • Seeing pink:GOP Rep. Anna Paulina Lunaclaimedshe was “actually smacked” by Medea Benjamin of Code Pink, said she was filing charges, and demanded Speaker Mike Johnson ban the anti-war group from the U.S. House.Videoshowed the claim to be not just demonstrably false but farcical. Luna hastriedto have Code Pink banned before.
  • Maine event:Presumptive Democratic Senate candidate Graham Platner told senators in a closed-door meeting that no more damaging allegations were coming, whether of sexual misconduct or assault, theWall Street Journalreported.
  • Right’s wrong:Acceptance of LGBTQ+ people is falling, and the decline is being driven by the GOP. Since 2022, Gallupsaid, “the share [of Republicans] who say such relations are morally acceptable has fallen 21 points to 35%.”
  • Hate tweet:As if to prove a point, Tennessee GOP Rep. Andy Oglestweeted“Homosexuality has no place in America,” deleted it, then blamed a staffer.

Opposition to Trump’s extraordinary appointment of housing official-cum-MAGA warrior Bill Pulte as acting director of national intelligence could soon weaken actual U.S intelligence gathering, as Democrats threaten to delay renewal of a key section of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act, covering surveillance of targets outside the U.S.

Mark Warner, ranking Democrat on the Senate Intelligence Committee, is pushing Republicans to pressure Trump to withdraw Pulte’s appointment, to ensure FISA passage.

On Wednesday, Warner also warned that Pulte might help Trump interfere in the midterms.

Speaking to NPR, Warner said: “Acting folks get six months runway” before needing Senate confirmation. “If we can’t convince the president to change his mind, six months… will keep [Pulte] in the job until after the elections.

“I was extraordinarily concerned about the former director of national intelligence, Tulsi Gabbard, interfering in our election. She was the one who showed up, I believe inappropriately and illegally, on a criminal investigation, seizing ballots in Fulton County, Georgia.

“But she had some national security background. She’d been in the military. She served in Congress. I would doubt Mr. Pulte could even name the multiple intelligence agencies that exist.”

Donald Trump’s DMs may be missing, but what did Richard Nixon want to do to some records of his tumultuous presidency, thereby prompting Congress to pass the Presidential Records Act of 1978?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israelkilledat least 11 Palestinians, including several children, in overnight strikes on residential buildings and other areas of Gaza. Israeli PM Benjamin Netanyahu told CNBC that Hamas had to disarm for the strikes to stop. Hamas told Reuters that Israel should stop striking Gaza.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    Infrastructure strike:Ukrainian long-range droneshitan oil terminal in St. Petersburg as the Russian city prepared to host an economic forum.
  • 🇨🇩 Ebola news:DR Congo’s military said 16 people werekilledin an attack by an Islamic State-affiliated group, in an area affected by the ongoing Ebola outbreak. Congo reported 344 cases of the disease, resulting in 60 deaths.
  • 🇨🇺 Castro celebrated:Former Cuban President Raul Castrocelebratedhis 95th birthday, two weeks after the U.S. indicted him over the downing of planes flown by Cuban exiles in 1996, and with a U.S. blockade biting. President Miguel Diaz-Canel praised Castro for “service to the Homeland, to regional and world peace.”


Nothing much to see here: just Democratic Rep. Ted Lieu guiding Marco Rubio into clearly lying to Congress, via footage of Donald Trump sleeping next to his Secretary of State during Cabinet meetings. Rubio denied that the big boss had ever done such a thing.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.
