item.title: This Is How the Iran War Has Exposed Israel's Weakness in the Middle East
item.description: To understand the policies of Benjamin Netanyahu’s government, we must recall that the Israeli prime minister is the political heir of Ze’ev Jabotinsky, the founder of the Revisionist Zionist movement, one of whose aides was Netanyahu’s father , Benzion. Jabotinsky outlined Israel’s stark reality…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
To understand the policies of Benjamin Netanyahu’s government, we must recall that the Israeli prime minister is the political heir of Ze’ev Jabotinsky, the founder of the Revisionist Zionist movement, one of whose aides was Netanyahu’s father, Benzion. Jabotinsky outlined Israel’s stark reality in an essay in 1923 titled “The Iron Wall.”

He argued that Jewish “colonization” of Palestine (and Jabotinsky had no hesitation in using that term) could only proceed behind an “iron wall” of Zionist military superiority. He accepted that Palestinian Arabs would, like “every native population in the world [resist] colonists as long as it has the slightest hope of being able to rid itself of the danger of being colonized.” This led Jabotinsky to conclude that the only way Arabs would ever agree to a Jewish presence in Palestine “is the iron wall, which is to say a strong power in Palestine that is not amenable to any Arab pressure. In other words, the only way to reach an agreement in the future is to abandon all idea of seeking an agreement at present.”

In many regards, this is the policy Netanyahu has long implemented – one of relying on Israel’s military supremacy and only really entering into agreements that consolidate this supremacy. But “an agreement in the future” can be a woolly notion if military superiority allows a country to continually put off the compromises necessary to bring about settlements.

This has led to an Israeli attitude that Carnegie scholar Nathan J. Brown has pithily described as “one of dominance without settlement, degradation without reconstruction, territorial denial without a political horizon, and war not as a bridge to an arrangement but the arrangement itself.”

These thoughts came to my mind on May 23, in reading a tweet by Danny Citrinowicz, a former head of the Iran branch in the Research and Analysis Division of Israeli defense intelligence, who has written lucidly and critically of the U.S. and Israeli war against Iran. Citrinowicz remarked that Netanyahu regarded the possible U.S. agreement with Iran being negotiated as a “personal and political defeat,” because, “[h]is approach was based on the belief that more pressure, more military power, and tighter coordination between Israel and the United States would eventually either force Iran into submission or destabilize the regime itself.”

Citrinowicz was not wrong, but he left something out of his analysis. Those mediating in the effort to persuade President Donald Trump to end military operations against Iran were members of a coalition of countries – Saudi Arabia, Turkey, Qatar, Egypt, and Pakistan – who are not only worried about what a continuation of the war might mean for their economies and regional stability, but who are also united in seeking to contain Israel, which they believe wants to impose its hegemony in the Middle East, supported by the United States.

What we’re seeing in the region is the emergence of a traditional balance-of-power mechanism. This is realpolitik, not personal, and if today the perceived danger is Israel, new coalitions could easily form again against other powers trying to achieve regional domination. While none of these countries is particularly sympathetic to Iran, which many once saw in the same way as they presently do Israel, they share with the Iranians a parallel interest in pushing back against Israel.

This willingness to take Iranian interests into consideration is partly what led Egypt, in November 2025, to come up with a new formula to address Hezbollah’s weapons. Last summer, Lebanon’s government decided the state should have a monopoly over weapons, meaning Hezbollah should be disarmed. Realizing this could lead to domestic conflict, as the party and its sponsor, Iran, rejected such an outcome, the Egyptians floated a compromise for “containment” of the weapons. The state would not disarm Hezbollah, but there would be an agreement to store the weapons and only use them as a deterrent against Israel.

Such dynamics show what Israel is up against in assuming it can sustain an iron wall approach to the region. It can surely continue to occupy and destroy parts of Lebanon, Gaza, and Syria, and call the ensuing wasteland peace; it can continue to try to ethnically cleanse Palestinians in Gaza and the West Bank; but the regional environment is bringing out the fundamental problem in the iron wall concept, namely that it allows Israel to defer political agreements to an indefinite, even unwanted, future. Its reliance on prodigious force, its refusal to cash in on this by securing stable political outcomes, facilitates the continuous emergence of adversaries who regard Israel as a menace and, therefore, will collaborate against it.

Perpetual war won’t bring perpetual peace for Israel. If anything, it will produce a more dangerous strategic reality. In the past, the Israelis deployed crushing military power to impose peace agreements on some of their neighbors, while ignoring the Palestinians entirely. Whatever the wisdom of such an approach, it did have a clear political endgame. Yet mass murder in Gaza brought about a new reality, namely that Middle Eastern publics now view Israel as an intolerable rogue state, unworthy of peace, reinforcing attitudes among their regimes that now also see it as a strategic threat.

Citrinowicz summarized the dilemma well: “Israel’s confrontation with Iran will not bring normalization with the Arab world, nor will it resolve Israel’s most fundamental security challenges, first and foremost, the Palestinian issue.” Instead, Israel has generated parallel interests among Saudi Arabia, Turkey, and Egypt as well, which, even if they seek balance, not open conflict, will make it much costlier in the future for the Israelis to advance their aims behind an iron wall of violence.

Last month, in a conversation with Arab leaders, several from the coalition forming against Israel, Trump asked them to join the Abraham Accords. This showed how out of touch he was with realities in the Middle East. Regional leaders believe that once U.S. officials assess the magnitude of the Iran fiasco, and Israel’s key role in bringing it about, they may be more predisposed to disengage from the region. This could leave Israel strategically isolated. If so, Arab states and Turkey would be in a better position to negotiate peace settlements that take their priorities into consideration.

Like Gaza, the Iran war is a turning point, one that should make Israelis think twice.

Michael Young is a senior editor at the Malcolm H. Kerr Carnegie Middle East Center in Beirut and editor of Diwan, Carnegie’s Middle East blog. Previously, he was opinion editor, as well as a columnist, for the Daily Star newspaper in Lebanon. He is the author of The Ghosts of Martyrs Square: An Eyewitness Account of Lebanon’s Life Struggle (Simon & Schuster, 2010).

item.title: Why Did Top Democrats Just Attend a Genocide Parade?
item.description: On this day in 1980, CNN made its debut as the world’s first 24-hour news network, a model that would change the face of TV newscasting – for the worse, critics say. Good morning, friends. Prem here. Wishing you a wonderful Monday wherever you are. Happy June! Today I’m grateful for the gift of…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1980, CNN made its debut as the world’s first 24-hour news network, a model that would change the face of TV newscasting – for the worse, critics say.

Good morning, friends. Prem here. Wishing you a wonderful Monday wherever you are. Happy June! Today I’m grateful for the gift of good sport. Seeing Arsenal vs. PSG go to penalties (sorry, Mayor Mamdani), and the young San Antonio Spurs surviving against the Oklahoma City Thunder was a blast. I’m super-conflicted on Spurs vs. Knicks, though. Love Spurs basketball, but also love the prospect of an NYC win and

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ a breakdown of all the genocidal Israeli officials who were happily parading in New York City – and all the Democrats who marched alongside them. Plus, Hasan Piker and Cenk Uygur are banned by the UK over their views on Israel and Donald Trump posts yet more evidence of his unfitness for office for all the world to see.

Let’s dig in!

In October 2025, Republican mayoral candidate Curtis Sliwa uttered a fateful phrase. “Every parade has the right to exist in New York City.”

But does that mean the mayor has to go to every parade?

On Sunday, New York was host to the Israel Day Parade. Crowds of pro-Israel marchers, Christian Zionists, Israeli officials, and U.S. politicians descended on the Upper East Side.

Mayor Zohran Mamdani attracted vicious criticism for being the first New York mayor in more than 60 years not to attend the event, which honors an apartheid state internationally that is credibly accused of genocide.

Mamdani avoided marching alongside Israeli officials who have expressed extremist, genocidal, and viciously racist sentiments about Palestinians – and at least one for whom the International Criminal Court is reportedly seeking an arrest warrant.

That did not stop many other Democrats from marching. New York Senator Chuck Schumer, Governor Kathy Hochul, Attorney General Letitia James, Police Commissioner Jessica Tisch, Council Speaker Julie Menin, disgraced former Mayor Eric Adams, disgraced former Governor and failed mayoral candidate Andrew Cuomo, Rep. Dan Goldman, Rep. Jerry Nadler (and Alex Bores, vying to take Nadler’s seat) – were in attendance. New York Republican Rep. Mike Lawler joined them.

So, who were all these New Yorkers marching with? Let’s take a survey.

Among the most vile attendees was Israeli Finance Minister and self-proclaimed homophobe, racist, and fascist Bezalel Smotrich, a settler who has helped lead efforts to illegally annex more of the West Bank. The International Criminal Court has reportedly filed an arrest warrant application against the man who said it would be “right and moral” to starve 2 million Palestinians “to death.”

Joining Smotrich were:

  • Amichai Eliyahu, the heritage minister who has called fornukingandwiping outGaza, and forstarvingPalestinians.
  • Yitzhak Wasserlauf, minister for Negev and Galilee affairs, whostormedAl-Aqsa Mosque, one of the holiest sites in Islam, andtoldIsraeli soldiers to target babies.

  • Minister of Diaspora Affairs

    Amichai Chikli, whosoughtto ban Mamdani’s wife from Israel,saidIsrael should settle northern Gaza,bannedaid groups from Gaza, andboosteda pro-Nazi Romanian politician.

  • Minister of Aliyah and Integration

    Ofir Sofer, who hasworked with Smotrichto financially reward people who move into illegal settlements in the West Bank.

  • Knesset Speaker

    Amir Ohana,who hassaidMuslims are prone to“cultural murderousness,”orderedjails not to vaccinate Palestinian detainees for COVID-19, andcalledfor the release of a Jewish man arrested in connection with a deadly shooting of an Arab-Israeli man, even saying,“Law-abiding citizens carrying weapons are a force multiplier for the authorities for the immediate neutralization of threat and danger.”
  • Meirav Ben Ari,an opposition politician who in October 2023said, as Israel attacked,“The children in Gaza have brought this upon themselves!”
  • Ariel Kallner, a Likud politician who hassaid,“Gazans are plump and healthy; it’s all nonsense;” “Whoever remains in the north [Gaza] – we will truly starve them;”and, in October 2023, “Right now, one goal: Nakba! A Nakba that will overshadow the Nakba of 48. Nakba in Gaza and Nakba to anyone who dares to join!”

… and more.

Also in attendance was Israeli Ambassador to the UN Danny Danon, who Benjamin Netanyahu once fired after Danon criticized him for “leftist feebleness” during the 2014 Gaza War, which killed some 2,300 Palestinians and wounded 10,000.

“This encounter at the Israel March says it all,” Danon posted. “An NYPD cop, a proud Jew with a kippah, representing the incredible mosaic of the community in New York and the uncompromising support for Israel. 🇺🇸🇮🇱.”

Unwittingly, that embodied the actual message of the day: that the NYPD wasn’t pursuing any of the allegedly genocidal officials in attendance.

Could you imagine if there was a parade in which even one prominent attendee was a Muslim, for example, who had called for violence against an entire group of people? Would so many Democrats so obsequiously champion such an affair?

Mamdani’s absence was entirely reasonable – all the more so given some pro-Israel marchers were among crowds who just last week screamed to deport him.

Pro-Israel actors and media figures sought to slander Mamdani.

“It is worth noting that this is nothing more than a peaceful and positive celebration of the existence of the only Jewish state in the world,” the New York and New Jersey chapter of the Anti-Defamation League said – a quaint way to describe a parade headlined by a murderer’s row of genocidal politicians.

Beth Miller, political director of Jewish Voice for Peace, countered the pro-Israel narrative.

“It’s antisemitic to conflate Jews and Israel. Which is exactly what the ADL is doing by calling the ‘Israel Day Parade’ a ‘Jewish celebration,’” she wrote.

“As a Jewish person who lives here, I’m pretty fucking glad we finally have a mayor who isn’t at a parade celebrating atrocity crimes.”

  • Art of No Deal:Donald Trumpdemandedtougher terms for Iran after being presented with a deal to extend the ceasefire, thereby extending uncertainty over control of the Strait of Hormuz, Iran’s nuclear ambitions, and other issues.
  • New attacks:U.S. Central Commandsaidit bombed radar and drone sites in the Iranian city of Geruk and on Qeshm Island after Tehran downed a U.S. drone over the weekend. Iran then said it responded to an American attack by targeting a U.S. base in the region, likely referring to reported attacks on Kuwait.
  • Ship strike:The U.S. military alsosaidit stopped a vessel trying to break its blockade of Iranian ports by firing a missile into its engine room. The U.S. did not board the Gambian-flagged ship, which was set adrift in the Gulf of Oman. TheNew York Timessaidan unnamed official told it the U.S. had helped around 70 ships through the Strait of Hormuz in the last three weeks.
  • Lebanon latest:Continuing to ignore a supposed ceasefire and citing Hezbollah fire into Israel, Benjamin Netanyahuordereda deeper incursion into Lebanese territory andstrikeson Beirut’s southern suburbs. Israeli forces seized Beaufort Castle, a900-year-old Crusader fortress. The death toll from Israeli strikes on Lebanon hastopped3,370 people.

Young Turks founder Cenk Uygur and his nephew, progressive streamer Hasan Piker, have been banned from the UK.

“I tried to get on a flight to London to attend SXSW London and give a speech at Oxford,” Uygur said. “I’ve been banned for criticizing Israel. Are we free anymore? This is oppression of Western citizens by our own governments on behalf of a different country!”

The Times reported, in an article Uygur posted, that UK Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood revoked Uygur’s visa “after concern over comments about Israel and grooming gangs.”

Uygur said the British government labeled him “a serious risk to the public order” because it deemed his position on Israel and its donations to members of Congress antisemitic. “I didn’t get banned for criticizing the UK, but for criticizing Israel,” he said, adding: “This is absolutely Kafkaesque.”

He then discussed the issue on YouTube.

Uygur had been due to speak to SXSW London on Wednesday, on the subject “Techno-Feudalism is Here. Who Are the Lords?” Piker was due to speak on Thursday, about “How the American Left Learned to Speak the Internet.” He was also due to appear at the Oxford Union.

Telling followers, “My visa has also been revoked,” Piker said he had been set to appear with figures including former Greek Finance Minister Yanis Varoufakis, former Labour leader Jeremy Corbyn, and current Greens leader Zack Polanski.

But last week, David Taylor, a Labour MP, called for Piker to be barred, saying his visit would not be “conducive to the public good.” Taylor claimed Piker had “openly supported a proscribed terrorist organisation and spouted… vile antisemitic rants.”

Piker strongly denies antisemitism, pointing out that criticism of Israel and support for Palestinian rights do not equate to such prejudice.

Piker said his barring from the UK was illustrative of “a sad state of affairs, where obviously the interests of Israel take the highest priority.”

This is Trump’s America.

item.title: Why Did Top Democrats Just Attend a Genocide Parade?
item.description: On this day in 1980, CNN made its debut as the world’s first 24-hour news network, a model that would change the face of TV newscasting – for the worse, critics say. Good morning, friends. Prem here. Wishing you a wonderful Monday wherever you are. Happy June! Today I’m grateful for the gift of…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1980, CNN made its debut as the world’s first 24-hour news network, a model that would change the face of TV newscasting – for the worse, critics say.

Good morning, friends. Prem here. Wishing you a wonderful Monday wherever you are. Happy June! Today I’m grateful for the gift of good sport. Seeing Arsenal vs. PSG go to penalties (sorry, Mayor Mamdani), and the young San Antonio Spurs surviving against the Oklahoma City Thunder was a blast. I’m super-conflicted on Spurs vs. Knicks, though. Love Spurs basketball, but also love the prospect of an NYC win and

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ a breakdown of all the genocidal Israeli officials who were happily parading in New York City – and all the Democrats who marched alongside them. Plus, Hasan Piker and Cenk Uygur are banned by the UK over their views on Israel and Donald Trump posts yet more evidence of his unfitness for office for all the world to see.

Let’s dig in!

In October 2025, Republican mayoral candidate Curtis Sliwa uttered a fateful phrase. “Every parade has the right to exist in New York City.”

But does that mean the mayor has to go to every parade?

On Sunday, New York was host to the Israel Day Parade. Crowds of pro-Israel marchers, Christian Zionists, Israeli officials, and U.S. politicians descended on the Upper East Side.

Mayor Zohran Mamdani attracted vicious criticism for being the first New York mayor in more than 60 years not to attend the event, which honors an apartheid state internationally that is credibly accused of genocide.

Mamdani avoided marching alongside Israeli officials who have expressed extremist, genocidal, and viciously racist sentiments about Palestinians – and at least one for whom the International Criminal Court is reportedly seeking an arrest warrant.

That did not stop many other Democrats from marching. New York Senator Chuck Schumer, Governor Kathy Hochul, Attorney General Letitia James, Police Commissioner Jessica Tisch, Council Speaker Julie Menin, disgraced former Mayor Eric Adams, disgraced former Governor and failed mayoral candidate Andrew Cuomo, Rep. Dan Goldman, Rep. Jerry Nadler (and Alex Bores, vying to take Nadler’s seat) – were in attendance. New York Republican Rep. Mike Lawler joined them.

So, who were all these New Yorkers marching with? Let’s take a survey.

Among the most vile attendees was Israeli Finance Minister and self-proclaimed homophobe, racist, and fascist Bezalel Smotrich, a settler who has helped lead efforts to illegally annex more of the West Bank. The International Criminal Court has reportedly filed an arrest warrant application against the man who said it would be “right and moral” to starve 2 million Palestinians “to death.”

Joining Smotrich were:

  • Amichai Eliyahu, the heritage minister who has called fornukingandwiping outGaza, and forstarvingPalestinians.
  • Yitzhak Wasserlauf, minister for Negev and Galilee affairs, whostormedAl-Aqsa Mosque, one of the holiest sites in Islam, andtoldIsraeli soldiers to target babies.

  • Minister of Diaspora Affairs

    Amichai Chikli, whosoughtto ban Mamdani’s wife from Israel,saidIsrael should settle northern Gaza,bannedaid groups from Gaza, andboosteda pro-Nazi Romanian politician.

  • Minister of Aliyah and Integration

    Ofir Sofer, who hasworked with Smotrichto financially reward people who move into illegal settlements in the West Bank.

  • Knesset Speaker

    Amir Ohana,who hassaidMuslims are prone to“cultural murderousness,”orderedjails not to vaccinate Palestinian detainees for COVID-19, andcalledfor the release of a Jewish man arrested in connection with a deadly shooting of an Arab-Israeli man, even saying,“Law-abiding citizens carrying weapons are a force multiplier for the authorities for the immediate neutralization of threat and danger.”
  • Meirav Ben Ari,an opposition politician who in October 2023said, as Israel attacked,“The children in Gaza have brought this upon themselves!”
  • Ariel Kallner, a Likud politician who hassaid,“Gazans are plump and healthy; it’s all nonsense;” “Whoever remains in the north [Gaza] – we will truly starve them;”and, in October 2023, “Right now, one goal: Nakba! A Nakba that will overshadow the Nakba of 48. Nakba in Gaza and Nakba to anyone who dares to join!”

… and more.

Also in attendance was Israeli Ambassador to the UN Danny Danon, who Benjamin Netanyahu once fired after Danon criticized him for “leftist feebleness” during the 2014 Gaza War, which killed some 2,300 Palestinians and wounded 10,000.

“This encounter at the Israel March says it all,” Danon posted. “An NYPD cop, a proud Jew with a kippah, representing the incredible mosaic of the community in New York and the uncompromising support for Israel. 🇺🇸🇮🇱.”

Unwittingly, that embodied the actual message of the day: that the NYPD wasn’t pursuing any of the allegedly genocidal officials in attendance.

Could you imagine if there was a parade in which even one prominent attendee was a Muslim, for example, who had called for violence against an entire group of people? Would so many Democrats so obsequiously champion such an affair?

Mamdani’s absence was entirely reasonable – all the more so given some pro-Israel marchers were among crowds who just last week screamed to deport him.

Pro-Israel actors and media figures sought to slander Mamdani.

“It is worth noting that this is nothing more than a peaceful and positive celebration of the existence of the only Jewish state in the world,” the New York and New Jersey chapter of the Anti-Defamation League said – a quaint way to describe a parade headlined by a murderer’s row of genocidal politicians.

Beth Miller, political director of Jewish Voice for Peace, countered the pro-Israel narrative.

“It’s antisemitic to conflate Jews and Israel. Which is exactly what the ADL is doing by calling the ‘Israel Day Parade’ a ‘Jewish celebration,’” she wrote.

“As a Jewish person who lives here, I’m pretty fucking glad we finally have a mayor who isn’t at a parade celebrating atrocity crimes.”

  • Art of No Deal:Donald Trumpdemandedtougher terms for Iran after being presented with a deal to extend the ceasefire, thereby extending uncertainty over control of the Strait of Hormuz, Iran’s nuclear ambitions, and other issues.
  • New attacks:U.S. Central Commandsaidit bombed radar and drone sites in the Iranian city of Geruk and on Qeshm Island after Tehran downed a U.S. drone over the weekend. Iran then said it responded to an American attack by targeting a U.S. base in the region, likely referring to reported attacks on Kuwait.
  • Ship strike:The U.S. military alsosaidit stopped a vessel trying to break its blockade of Iranian ports by firing a missile into its engine room. The U.S. did not board the Gambian-flagged ship, which was set adrift in the Gulf of Oman. TheNew York Timessaidan unnamed official told it the U.S. had helped around 70 ships through the Strait of Hormuz in the last three weeks.
  • Lebanon latest:Continuing to ignore a supposed ceasefire and citing Hezbollah fire into Israel, Benjamin Netanyahuordereda deeper incursion into Lebanese territory andstrikeson Beirut’s southern suburbs. Israeli forces seized Beaufort Castle, a900-year-old Crusader fortress. The death toll from Israeli strikes on Lebanon hastopped3,370 people.

Young Turks founder Cenk Uygur and his nephew, progressive streamer Hasan Piker, have been banned from the UK.

“I tried to get on a flight to London to attend SXSW London and give a speech at Oxford,” Uygur said. “I’ve been banned for criticizing Israel. Are we free anymore? This is oppression of Western citizens by our own governments on behalf of a different country!”

The Times reported, in an article Uygur posted, that UK Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood revoked Uygur’s visa “after concern over comments about Israel and grooming gangs.”

Uygur said the British government labeled him “a serious risk to the public order” because it deemed his position on Israel and its donations to members of Congress antisemitic. “I didn’t get banned for criticizing the UK, but for criticizing Israel,” he said, adding: “This is absolutely Kafkaesque.”

He then discussed the issue on YouTube.

Uygur had been due to speak to SXSW London on Wednesday, on the subject “Techno-Feudalism is Here. Who Are the Lords?” Piker was due to speak on Thursday, about “How the American Left Learned to Speak the Internet.” He was also due to appear at the Oxford Union.

Telling followers, “My visa has also been revoked,” Piker said he had been set to appear with figures including former Greek Finance Minister Yanis Varoufakis, former Labour leader Jeremy Corbyn, and current Greens leader Zack Polanski.

But last week, David Taylor, a Labour MP, called for Piker to be barred, saying his visit would not be “conducive to the public good.” Taylor claimed Piker had “openly supported a proscribed terrorist organisation and spouted… vile antisemitic rants.”

Piker strongly denies antisemitism, pointing out that criticism of Israel and support for Palestinian rights do not equate to such prejudice.

Piker said his barring from the UK was illustrative of “a sad state of affairs, where obviously the interests of Israel take the highest priority.”

  • Slop spree:Donald Trump went on a social media tear on Saturday, posting more than 50 times, much of it AI-generated. Combining favorite targets, one post about a meeting between Leo XIV and Brandon Johnsonread: “Someone should explain to the Pope that the Mayor of Chicago is useless, and that Iran cannot have a Nuclear Weapon.”
  • Out of tune:Trump alsosaidconcerts marking the country’s 250th birthday should be canceled, after acts dropped out of the events planned for the National Mall, citing their affiliation with Trump’s White House. Trump said he might hold a rally instead, because he was “the Number One Attraction anywhere in the World,” attracting “much larger audiences than Elvis in his prime.”Really.
  • Cry for help:In a bizarre appeal, “MAGA Lefty” and News Nation host Batya Ungar-SargonbeggedTrump to help “people who voted for you three times, who would walk over Legos coated in hot coals to vote for you, [who] are broke.”
  • ICE blast:Democratic Senator Andy Kim, last weekpepper-sprayedat a protest outside a New Jersey ICE facility,saidDHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin’s threat to retaliate for such protests by withdrawing customs officials from airports in “sanctuary cities” would “do so much economic damage” and “made no sense.”
  • Texts leak:Maine Democratic Senate candidate Graham Platner’s wife, Amy Gertner,addressedreports she disclosed to the campaign that she once found sexually explicit text messages on her husband’s phone —callingthe leak a breach of trust and criticizing media outlets for spreading “gossip.” Platner leads GOP incumbent Susan Collins in polling.

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:An Israeli airstrike killed at least two Palestinians and wounded 12 at a cafe busy with Eid holiday customers, health officialssaid. Israel continues to ignore a supposed U.S.-brokered ceasefire, with around 900 Palestinians killed since its supposed implementation last October.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

  • 🇨🇴

  • 🇨🇩🇺🇬

    Ebola update:As its chief, Tedros Anhanom Ghebreyesus, visited a new Ebola treatment center in DR Congo, the World Health Organizationreported134 cases in Congo and Uganda, resulting in 18 confirmed deaths.

This is Trump’s America.

  • Ken Paxton,the Republican Senate candidate in Texas, spoke toFox Business. Trotting out familiar attacks on DemocratJames Talerico, including for having “vegan campaign views,” the state AG also gave Talerico a freebie.   • Ex-VP

    Mike Pencetold CBSthat Trump’s DOJ “Anti-Weaponization Fund” should be dropped, andtold NBC: “It’s deeply offensive to me that you could… even possibly compensate people who assaulted police officers or vandalized the Capitol on January 6th.” True, some such rioters wanted to hang Pence. Also true, Pencehas a book outin which he cowers before Trump, praising his “conservative principles.”

item.title: 11 Harrowing Video Testimonies From Westerners Tortured and Raped by Israel
item.description: “They wrenched my trousers and my underwear down and… I was vaginally raped by one of the male soldiers.” “He came walking to me with a taser, saying that he was going to taser my genitals.” “I was pricked with some sort of syringe.” In the week since Israel released the hundreds of activists,…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
“They wrenched my trousers and my underwear down and… I was vaginally raped by one of the male soldiers.”

“He came walking to me with a taser, saying that he was going to taser my genitals.”

“I was pricked with some sort of syringe.”

In the week since Israel released the hundreds of activists, humanitarians, and journalists it abducted from the Global Sumud Flotilla that was attempting to break its siege on Gaza, testimony after testimony has revealed only a glimpse of the abuse, including sexual violence, inflicted on participants while in Israeli detention.

But like the decades of testimony given by Palestinians, mountains of human rights reports, and physical evidence, the harrowing details described in first-hand, on-the-record accounts by those who endured it are being largely ignored by Western media. Israel, of course, denies any abuse.

Had any other country in the Middle East been accused of torturing and raping Westerners, front pages worldwide would have read, ‘Threatened to Taser My Genitals’ or ‘I Was Raped.’ The fact that they haven’t underscores the dehumanization of Palestinians and anyone who supports them.

Since Western media isn’t, Zeteo is stepping in. We’ve rounded up the 11 of the most troubling accounts of the abuse Israel inflicted on the global aid flotilla participants:

2. ‘Peeing Blood’ After Being Kicked in the Genitals –

4. ‘Grabbing My Boobs’ –


Shockingly, there are many more testimonies like these. For example, Zeteo contributor Alex Colston wrote in detail about his own experience being kicked and threatened here.

In total, at least 35 participants suffered broken ribs, 14 reported being sexually abused, including reports of rape, and dozens of others suffered concussions, bruises, and nerve damage from beatings or being tightly handcuffed, according to Global Sumud Flotilla participants and organizers.

And while some Western governments have condemned Israel’s actions against their citizens, and in a few cases called for investigations, there have been no signs that Israel will face any meaningful consequences from those nations, many of which continue to arm the Israeli government.

Zeteo’s Kennedy Garmong and Aaliya Haque contributed to this piece

Check out more of Zeteo’s coverage of Palestine:

item.title: Mamdani vs. Spanberger: a Lesson in Leadership for the Dems
item.description: It is clear today that the Republican Party has long been planning to abolish American democracy, with a plan centered around the Supreme Court. First Republicans on the court stole the 2000 election for George W. Bush, then in 2010 they legalized nearly unlimited corporate and oligarch election…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
It is clear today that the Republican Party has long been planning to abolish American democracy, with a plan centered around the Supreme Court. First Republicans on the court stole the 2000 election for George W. Bush, then in 2010 they legalized nearly unlimited corporate and oligarch election spending. When Republicans won the 2010 midterms, they seized the opportunity to rig district boundaries as aggressively as they could – which helped in 2012, when Democrats won more votes for the House of Representatives, but Republicans got more seats.

Then in 2013 the court repealed the toughest parts of the Voting Rights Act, opening the door for aggressive disenfranchisement of Black Americans, which Republican states immediately seized. In 2019, the Supreme Court legalized partisan gerrymandering. In 2020, Donald Trump attempted an outright putsch, and when he was eventually prosecuted for that, the court bailed him out. When Colorado, following the plain language of the 14th Amendment, attempted to take his name off the 2024 ballot, the court put him back on.

Until very recently, the Democratic Party has been patently unwilling to fight fire with fire. They had two main chances to confront Republicans’ anti-democratic attacks this century, and both times they punted. In 2009-10, they had huge majorities in Congress, but did not even get rid of the Senate filibuster, much less confront the corrupt Supreme Court majority. The same thing happened in 2021-22, though with much smaller majorities. And thanks largely to pathetic dithering on the part of former Attorney General Merrick Garland, Trump escaped prosecution for Jan. 6 long enough to become the Republican nominee for president, at which point the court declared him a king in all but name.

It’s reasonably likely that Democrats will get one more chance in 2029, so long as they can defeat another probable coup attempt. It will be critical, therefore, that the Democratic Party nominate candidates at all levels, particularly for president, who will mount an all-out attack on Republican power, above the reactionary majority on court. The choice is illustrated by some fresh party leaders: On the one hand there is New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani; on the other is Virginia Governor Abigail Spanberger.

As Aaron Regunberg points out at The New Republic, Mamdani (who, alas, is not eligible to run for president) has racked up an impressive list of accomplishments, including free daycare for 2-year-olds, a bunch of new rec centers, improvements to the bike lane and public transit networks, 100,000 potholes filled, a tax on second homes worth more than $5 million, various efficiency savings, and most importantly, the closure of a $12 billion gap in the city budget.

They key point here isn’t that every one of these were progressive home runs – one could imagine Mitt Romney boasting about balancing the budget and streamlining regulations for small business owners – but rather how they demonstrate courage, aggression, and good political acumen. To get $4 billion from the state government, Mamdani started and won a high-stakes standoff with Governor Kathy Hochul, threatening to raise real-estate taxes if she did not agree, which likely would have made him very unpopular. But with his prior frankly transactional endorsement of Hochul (which irritated many of his socialist comrades), Mamdani signaled that he could be trusted, and he got about as much of what he wanted as could realistically be expected. It was a masterful piece of negotiation.

Spanberger, meanwhile, got a decent start with a plan to gerrymander the state’s congressional district boundaries, so as to compensate for Republican cheating in other states and make the national election fair. But when the state Supreme Court threw out the plan on a nonsense technicality – an unprecedented action that itself arguably violated the text of the Virginia state constitution – she gave up.

Worse, as the socialist writer Carl Beijer details, she then vetoed a whole bunch of progressive priorities, including among other things a measure to strengthen voting rights, one to protect state universities from Trump’s unprecedented assault on academia, one to legalize marijuana, and another to legalize collective bargaining for state employees.

Again, the key point is not that her actions were bad ideologically (though they were), it’s that they evinced weakness and poor political instincts. Standing up to right-wing judicial tyranny is the first thing the next Democratic president will have to do; Spanberger faced that exact test and failed immediately. Vetoing a bunch of progressive priorities deflates enthusiasm among Democratic voters. Breaking a promise to those voters indicates there is no affirmative point to voting Democrat.

Worst of all, breaking a promise to unions in particular undermines Spanberger’s own power. Labor has been the backbone of liberal and left power going back to the 19th century. For decades, American unions have spent millions of dollars and thousands of hours of organizer work trying to get Democrats elected, only for the party to lend them little more than rhetorical support, if not pushing through trade deals that devastate American jobs. (Joe Biden was a notable exception.)

Some observers hoped that as a former CIA officer, Spanberger would have the ruthlessness and nose for hard political realities necessary to do what needs to be done. Surely she could take a page out of Kermit Roosevelt’s playbook and break through any judicial obstacles – perhaps by bribing state judges with millions of dollars in gold bars? Instead, she’s turned out to be another limp dishrag centrist.

It’s also worth pointing out any argument for preventing public sector unions in Virginia is ridiculous. Conservative economists make a case that they necessarily make public services less efficient, but not only is that disproved by the Nordic countries (where public services are all heavily unionized and much better than in the U.S.), the actual reason the ban exists was literally Jim Crow apartheid. As Harold Meyerson points out at The American Prospect, the law dates back to the 1940s and was motivated by an incipient interracial union at a single state hospital.

Given all that, vetoing union rights for public sector workers is criminally stupid. I am reminded of former New York Governor Andrew Cuomo, who had a penchant for betraying his most loyal voting constituencies for no apparent reason other than spite.

I think it is no coincidence that Mamdani is a socialist and Spanberger a moderate liberal. It’s not impossible for someone with moderate policy views to be capable of tactical aggression, but in practice it is rare. People who are characterologically timid and hesitant will naturally gravitate towards small-bore policy, while bold and aggressive people will tend to prefer radical reform.

And boldness and aggression are what Democratic voters must demand from their candidates. If the party wins a trifecta and punts once more, they likely won’t get another chance.

Ryan Cooper is a senior editor at the American Prospect, and the author of ‘How Are You Going to Pay for That?: Smart Answers to the Dumbest Question in Politics.’ He is a Zeteo contributor.

item.title: This Week in Palestine: Israel Kills Over Two Dozen, Pushes Deeper into Gaza as Palestinians Mark Eid
item.description: Muslims across Palestine marked yet another Eid amid Israeli strikes, raids, and other violence. Those in Gaza were once again blocked from making the Hajj pilgrimage. Over the last week alone, Israel killed more than two dozen Palestinians in Gaza, including several children. One strike severed a…
item.author: This Week in Palestine | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Muslims across Palestine marked yet another Eid amid Israeli strikes, raids, and other violence. Those in Gaza were once again blocked from making the Hajj pilgrimage.

Over the last week alone, Israel killed more than two dozen Palestinians in Gaza, including several children. One strike severed a baby’s leg and reportedly killed his mother. At the same time, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu ordered the military to take control of 70% of Gaza – yet another clear example of how the supposed “ceasefire” was in name only.

In the occupied West Bank, Israeli forces killed a Palestinian man during a raid in Jenin on Eid al-Adha. They also stormed and closed Hebron’s Ibrahimi Mosque.

From strikes across Gaza to warnings about a worsening scabies outbreak in Israeli prisons to plans to strip Jordan of its long-time custodianship of Jerusalem’s Al-Aqsa Mosque, here’s just a glimpse of what happened in Palestine this week:

In Spain, the Basque regional police beat Global Sumud Flotilla activists returning home after being released from Israeli detention. At least four were arrested.

Journalist Ahmed Shihab-Eldin notes the scene underscores the fact that the Basque regional police force known as Ertzaintza “has a long-standing, multifaceted relationship with Israel that goes far beyond the Spanish government’s official positions on Palestine.”

The Palestinian Prisoner’s Society (PPS) warned that a “dangerous and rapidly escalating outbreak of scabies skin disease is sweeping through” Israeli prisons holding Palestinians due to poor conditions.

“Many infected prisoners had previously contracted the disease and partially recovered, only to become reinfected due to the continued deadly health conditions inside the prisons,” PPS said.

Middle East Eye reported that in an effort led by Donald Trump’s son-in-law, Jared Kushner, and U.S. Ambassador to Israel Mike Huckabee, the U.S. and Israel are “actively working” on a plan to remove Jordan as the custodian of the Al-Aqsa Mosque compound and declare the site a “multifaith center.”

“Israel would also have a major say over the appointment of imams, preachers and senior mosque officials, and would also be involved in signing off on the content mentioned in Friday sermons,” MEE wrote, citing unnamed sources.

Israel killed at least 12 Palestinians on the eve of Eid al-Adha, according to the UN.

One strike targeted the new leader of Hamas’s military wing, killing him, his wife, and two of his children. The killing of Mohammed Odeh came less than two weeks after Israel killed his predecessor.

Israel killed at least 10 Palestinians across Gaza on Eid al-Adha, including several children.

Meanwhile, Israeli forces shot and killed 45-year-old Nasser Ali Kamel al-Saadi during a raid in Jenin.

Netanyahu said he had ordered the Israeli military to expand its control to more than 70% of Gaza.

“At this point, we are fully in control of 60% of the territory of the Gaza Strip … and my directive is to get to … 70%,” Netanyahu said in footage aired by Israel’s Channel 12.

Al Jazeera noted that when someone said Israel should take over the entire enclave, Netanyahu simply said: “First of all, 70. Let’s start with that.”

Since Israel withdrew to the initial “yellow line” as part of the first phase of the so-called ceasefire, it has continued to move the line further into territory that was supposed to be under Hamas’s control. That has left Gaza’s population of more than 2 million crammed into a tiny area of land.

The UN on Friday released a report adding Israel to its blacklist for the use of sexual violence in conflict, saying it had documented 31 cases of conflict-related sexual violence, including as a form of torture, being inflicted on Palestinians in Gaza and the West Bank from 2023 to 2025.

Israel, a day earlier, said it was cutting ties with UN Secretary-General António Guterres over the report.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: In His Latest Humiliation, Trump’s Live Music ‘Party’ Falls Apart
item.description: When Donald Trump isn’t busy destroying the White House to build a highly corrupt ballroom or starting illegal wars , he’s excited about all his party-planning ahead of the Fourth of July. For months, two Trump advisers tell Zeteo , the U.S. president has actively involved himself in the minutiae…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
When Donald Trump isn’t busy destroying the White House to build a highly corrupt ballroom or starting illegal wars, he’s excited about all his party-planning ahead of the Fourth of July.

For months, two Trump advisers tell Zeteo, the U.S. president has actively involved himself in the minutiae of the planning and pageantry for events during and leading up to the United States Semiquincentennial, the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence’s adoption.

The president has been briefed many times on things like events details, the roster of booked celebrity appearances, pyrotechnics, military spectacle, and other logistics, and has enthusiastically proposed his own ideas. Trump has told advisers that it’ll be a great “party” between now and the July 4 festivities in Washington, D.C., and he’s taken a special interest in facets such as the days of musical performances, and the upcoming UFC fights in front of the White House marking Trump’s birthday.

But this week, something went wrong – and now President Trump’s big “party” might not be so great after all.

On Wednesday, a group called Freedom 250 announced a lineup of recording artists – including Martina McBride, Vanilla Ice, Milli Vanilli, the Commodores, Bret Michaels, Young MC, and Flo Rida – apparently scheduled to perform at Freedom 250’s “Great American State Fair,” a multi-day festival starting next month at the National Mall in DC. Within hours, announced musical guests began pulling out, publicly insisting that they did not realize they’d signed on to perform at a Trump-aligned affair.

The confusion was perhaps understandable – and it underscores how President Trump can’t seem to do anything, even simple party-planning, without attempting some weird autocratic power grab. The fiasco also highlights how grossly unpopular this president has become, and how his mission to hijack American culture in the name of MAGA supremacy is falling miserably flat.

Years before Trump’s second administration, a congressionally authorized, cross-partisan commission called America250 was created, with the purpose of planning for this historic anniversary. But Trump’s efforts to subsume or corrupt that commission simply wasn’t enough for him.

Last year, he signed an executive order to form Freedom 250, which features an explicitly Trump-allied White House task force. It has largely usurped authority from the congressionally sanctioned, bipartisan America250, and has prioritized decidedly Trumpian programming like the UFC matches.

Sources familiar with the matter tell Zeteo that people working on behalf of several artists billed as performing at the “Great American State Fair” thought they were communicating with America250, and claim that they did not know what Freedom 250 even was.

One entertainment industry source says that communications seemed almost purposefully vague at times, with Donald Trump’s name not being used once.

“I should have done my research,” this source concedes.

As of writing this, most of the marquee performers – including Young MC, McBride, Michaels, the Commodores, and Morris Day and the Time – have withdrawn from the Trump-aligned “State Fair,” with some saying they felt duped.

It’s getting so bad that Fox News is now floating suggestions to Trump for which musicians he “should call” immediately to fill the holes in the schedule.

Apparently Vanilla Ice is still doing it, for one, which is not exactly a surprise, given the rapper performed “Ice Ice Baby” at Trump’s most recent New Year’s Eve party at Mar-a-Lago club.

People with knowledge of Freedom 250’s operations say that discussions have already begun about possible replacement acts, though some of these sources couldn’t hide – albeit anonymously – Trumpworld’s general displeasure about the negative media attention.

“We can’t just have Kid Rock every day [of the state fair],” one Trump appointee says. “It’s a little fucking embarassing when you can’t hold on to Bret Michaels… But we’re confident we’ll get a good line-up.”

For now, however, it’s looking bleak for Trump, a man obsessed with the idea of surrounding himself with A-list celebrities whose approval he’s sought for decades. Nowadays, the president is more likely to threaten an A-lister with criminal investigation for hurting his feelings than he is to be seen bonding with one.

Moreover, one well-connected Hollywood agent tells Zeteo simply: “I don’t think most of my clients even need me to tell them this, but I would tell [them]: If someone approaches you about performing at Trump’s state fair, flush that invitation down the toilet if you know what’s good for your career.”

In the grand scheme of Trump’s political career, the rapid collapse of this line-up of his “Great American State Fair” isn’t by itself impactful. And it’s not the only way the president’s cultural takeover is failing – a judge just ordered that Trump’s name be removed from the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts, prompting a meltdown from Trump about how the judge “should be ashamed of himself!”

However, the speed at which his fair imploded casts yet another humiliating spotlight on a grim reality for the Trumpist personality cult that currently powers the Republican Party.

The president and his policy agenda have grown so monumentally toxic to the average American that well-known figures and institutions haven’t just reconsidered their previous flirtations with appeasing the MAGA right; they’re sprinting away from a wretchedly unpopular leader and his enablers.

According to several well-placed Hollywood and entertainment sources, shortly after Trump’s 2024 victory over Vice President Kamala Harris, there were numerous discussions among powerful CEOs and other executives in Hollywood about how they likely needed to pivot much more of their content to appeal specifically to MAGA-conservative culture. After all, Trump had, for once, finally defeated the Democratic presidential nominee in the popular vote, so in their estimation, surely they had to trot out numerous sops to MAGA America, if they wished to flourish in it.

That sentiment (or, for some, fear) has diminished in the year and a half since, the sources say, especially after Trump’s mediocre-to-begin-with poll numbers took a sharp nosedive into HellWorld territory.

Still, that hasn’t stopped even some Democrats from hoping that the eventual line-up of celebrities and musicians at Team Trump’s fair will be able to do so in celebration of the republic, not its current ruler.

“In what should be a unifying moment to come together and celebrate our common history, my hope is that the artists who participate in the concert will be allowed to emphasize Americans’ shared values rather than lift up any one individual or movement. This should not be a moment of division, but of unification, pride and celebration,” says Adrienne Elrod, a political strategist who has worked with high-profile celebrities in her roles leading multiple Democratic presidential campaigns’ outreach operations. “It is a shame that so many artists who were booked dropped out because they fear it will become too politicized.”

item.title: Israel's Latest Invasion of Lebanon Is About More Than Netanyahu
item.description: The Israeli army is invading deeper into Lebanon to “deal a crushing blow” to Hezbollah, less than two years after the previous Israeli invasion of Lebanon to deal a crushing blow to Hezbollah, 20 years since the 2006 Israeli invasion of Lebanon to deal a crushing blow to Hezbollah, and 44 years…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
The Israeli army is invading deeper into Lebanon to “deal a crushing blow” to Hezbollah, less than two years after the previous Israeli invasion of Lebanon to deal a crushing blow to Hezbollah, 20 years since the 2006 Israeli invasion of Lebanon to deal a crushing blow to Hezbollah, and 44 years since the start of 1982 Israeli invasion of Lebanon to deal a crushing blow to Palestinian militants that turned into an 18-year occupation that gave birth to Hezbollah in the first place.

Today, more than a million civilians have already been forced from their homes as the Israeli military issues new, illegal evacuation orders for major cities in the south. Thousands have been killed, including over 200 children, as well as about a dozen Lebanese government troops ostensibly on the same side as the Israeli forces bombing them.

Why is Benjamin Netanyahu really invading Lebanon, and does he really expect to defeat Hezbollah once and for all – again?

Days after Donald Trump and Netanyahu launched what looked to be a war of annihilation against Iran on February 28, Hezbollah launched rockets and drones across the border at Israeli military targets, which it said was retaliation for the assassination of Iran’s supreme leader in addition to constant Israeli violations of the ceasefire that nominally ended the previous Israel-Hezbollah conflict.

The Israeli government responded with a familiar playbook: Intense strikes intended to neutralize the perpetrators, deliberate collective punishment of the civilian population to increase deterrence per the “Dahiya doctrine” coined in the 2006 Lebanon war, and a ground invasion and occupation to establish a so-called “buffer zone” to protect Israel’s borders.

Israeli security chiefs might genuinely believe this cruel and largely illegal strategy will work this time, despite all historic evidence to the contrary; they also might just be out of ideas. These are the same guys behind a harebrained Iran regime change scheme to put Mahmoud Ahmadinejad in charge of Iran, and who couldn’t stop patting themselves on the back after their 2024 pager attacks and military campaign “dismantled” Hezbollah, the group they’re fighting again today.

But the newest wave of killing won’t keep Israelis safe. A protective buffer zone in Lebanon is something of a fantasy since Hezbollah possesses long-range weapons that can simply be fired from beyond any Israeli front line. Hezbollah’s recent adaptation of the cheap fiber optic cable-controlled drones popularized by the Ukraine war has further diminished the military value of holding Lebanese territory.

And ultimately, the Israeli government just isn’t interested in peace right now. In early April, Trump forced Netanyahu to agree to peace talks with the Lebanese government as a de facto ceasefire with Hezbollah in order to satisfy Tehran’s preconditions for a U.S.-Iran ceasefire (Washington and Tel Aviv have relegated the Lebanese government to the role of interlocutor for Hezbollah, though they also demand that the Lebanese army disarm Hezbollah on their behalf, a political and military impossibility). But Netanyahu refused to withdraw Israeli troops from Lebanon, rejected Hezbollah’s offer of “quiet for quiet,” and, after grudgingly agreeing to a ceasefire, proceeded to totally ignore it.

Since the October 7 attack shattered Netanyahu’s cultivated image as “Mr. Security,” the embattled prime minister has regularly turned to military escalation for redemption – or just to postpone court dates for his corruption trial. The Iran war, intended to cement Netanyahu’s legacy, has instead ended up as a political loser. Israelis felt they had sacrificed for nothing after Netanyahu agreed to Trump’s temporary ceasefire with Iran; over 50% opposed the deal.

Now, as the ever-embattled prime minister prepares for elections in October, escalating in Lebanon offers two lifelines, albeit desperate ones: First, there’s the chance that the current operation actually succeeds in pacifying Hezbollah to the extent that Netanyahu can claim a win. He might imagine this is possible, given the last Lebanon war did successfully subdue Hezbollah for a while. On the other hand, Netanyahu probably can’t pull off a surprise pager attack a second time.

Second, and less improbably, the Israeli escalation in Lebanon is a way for Netanyahu to spoil a permanent U.S.-Iran deal, prolonging the war and leaving open the possibility that Netanyahu could “finish the job” in Iran. The Iranian government has repeatedly emphasized that a ceasefire must encompass Lebanon as well. There is an ideological and strategic imperative for Tehran to demonstrate solidarity with the proxies and partners that fight on its behalf; if Tehran is seen to abandon Hezbollah, then it risks losing the confidence of the groups it backs in Iraq and Yemen. More importantly for Tehran, if Trump can’t restrain Netanyahu in Lebanon, it’s hard to trust his pledges to restrain Netanyahu in Iran. A running war with Hezbollah also gives Tel Aviv new opportunities to draw Tehran back into war; the then-unprecedented Israeli strike on IRGC officers in Syria in April 2024 was justified by Iranian support for proxies that were attacking Israel.

But the invasion of Lebanon is much bigger than the ambitions and delusions of one man. It’s impossible to understand the current Israeli invasion of Lebanon except as an extension of the open drive for ethnic cleansing and territorial expansionism that has dominated Israeli politics, especially since October 7. Hamas’s surprise attack immediately became an opportunity to rid Gaza of Palestinians for once and for all and free up the land for Israelis. If there was any dispute over the Israeli government’s intent back in 2023, just this week, Netanyahu bragged he had increased Israeli occupation of Gaza from 50% to 60% and planned to increase it to 70%. Hezbollah’s March attack presented a similar opportunity for expansionists.

Like in Gaza, the Israeli government’s declared objective in Lebanon is total disarmament of a militant group deeply rooted in the population, an impossible goal – as Israeli generals admitted in Gaza – that justifies open-ended war. Like in Gaza, Israeli operations in Lebanon are now focused as much, if not more, on methodical demolition of homes than combat operations against fighters. Bulldozers – not bombs – are responsible for most of the moonscape-like photos you’ve seen of Gaza, and, increasingly, flattened villages in southern Lebanon.

Like in Gaza, mass “evacuation” orders of entire regions aim to achieve ethnic cleansing on the cheap – if these orders were really about protecting civilians, the Israeli military wouldn’t be bulldozing homes so fleeing Lebanese villagers have nowhere to return to. At one point, the Israeli military attempted a particularly insidious form of ethnic cleansing, threatening to bomb Christian and Druze villages that harbored fleeing Shia Muslims.

And in Lebanon, as in Gaza, the writing is on the wall that the Israeli government will eventually populate seized territory with Jewish Israelis. The infamous Israeli Ministers Itamar Ben-Gvir and Bezalel Smotrich have both vowed to settle southern Lebanon or formally incorporate it into Israel. The pair are often described in U.S. media as “extremists” or “far-right,” but it’s unclear what makes them outliers in a government that seems to be gradually carrying out many of their key demands; Israel’s mainstream opposition coalition doesn’t appear to have objections to their views on territorial expansion either.

It’s important to understand that these three explanations for the Lebanon war – misguided Israeli self-defense, Netanyahu’s latest political gambit, and expansionist ethnic cleansing – are not mutually exclusive. Israeli defense officials could truly believe this is their best shot at securing the northern border and Netanyahu hopes this will save his career and the Israeli government has undeniably been gobbling up territory in Gaza, the West Bank, Syria, and Lebanon for the past two years with no intention of giving it back.

Harrison Mann is a former US Army major and executive officer of the Defense Intelligence Agency’s Middle East/Africa Regional Center who resigned in protest of his office’s support for Israel’s war in Gaza under the Biden administration. He is currently with the group Win Without War and recently became a Zeteo contributor.

item.title: AIPAC’s Most Hated 2028 Democratic Contenders, By the Numbers
item.description: On this day in 2019, former FBI Director Robert Mueller spoke about his investigation of Russian election interference and links between Donald Trump and Moscow. Contrary to Trump’s claims of exoneration, Mueller said: “If we had had confidence that the president did not commit a crime, we would…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 2019, former FBI Director Robert Mueller spoke about his investigation of Russian election interference and links between Donald Trump and Moscow. Contrary to Trump’s claims of exoneration, Mueller said: “If we had had confidence that the president did not commit a crime, we would have said so.”

Good morning, friends. Prem here. Wishing you a wonderful Friday wherever you are, and Eid Mubarak to all who celebrate. Can you BELIEVE it’s almost June? How are you enjoying the weather? Let me know in the comments… :)

There is no paywall in this installment of ‘First Draft’ thanks to today’s sponsor, Ground News!

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ an analysis of AIPAC’s Twitter account gives a better understanding of which Democrats actually challenge the U.S.-Israel relationship, a top CBS reporter departs with a warning, and Maine Senate hopeful Graham Platner lures Susan Collins into offering an open goal.

Let’s dig in!

You can tell a lot about someone by the enemies they attract. When you look at Democrats floated as possible contenders for the 2028 presidential nomination, very few have attracted the ire of the Israel lobby.

As the public has grown increasingly outraged by the genocide Israel has committed in Gaza with U.S. tax dollars, the American Israel Public Affairs Committee, or AIPAC, has scrambled to shore up support. It’s an effort that has involved dumping millions into elections across the U.S., using opaque super PACs to disguise such spending, and lobbying to curtail free speech. It has also fueled a very active presence on X, the platform formerly known as Twitter.

Since the start of 2025, AIPAC has fired off hundreds of tweets attacking U.S. politicians. But among the crowded field of Democrats floated as potential presidential picks, only three have been particular targets for AIPAC: Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, Rep. Ro Khanna, and Senator Chris Van Hollen.

Since 2025, according to a Zeteo analysis, AIPAC has gone after Khanna by name at least 147 times; Ocasio-Cortez at least 116 times; and Van Hollen at least 105 times.

In a December 2025 post, AIPAC slammed Van Hollen for… holding all actors to equal standards, whether adversary or foe.

In a February 2026 post, AIPAC mocked Ocasio-Cortez on foreign policy, saying: “If @AOC spent less time obsessively lying about Israel, she’d have more time to find Venezuela and learn about Taiwan.”

And in April, when Khanna said “Team America” should cut tax dollars to Israel, keep the Israeli prime minister out of the Situation Room, and move away from obsession over war in the Middle East, the pro-Israel lobby responded that Khanna wasn’t Team America, but rather “Team Ro.”

Of course, those three Democrats are not alone in being AIPAC targets. Attacks often feature names including Reps. Ilhan Omar, Pramila Jayapal, Rashida Tlaib, Summer Lee, Delia Ramirez, and Mark Pocan, as well as Senators Bernie Sanders, Tina Smith, Elizabeth Warren, Jeff Merkley, and Peter Welch.

As U.S. support for Israel has plummeted, particularly among Democrats and independents, Democratic politicians have changed their rhetoric, distancing themselves from AIPAC specifically or making relatively safe condemnations of the “Netanyahu government.” A sparse few, listed above, have gone further, criticizing the Israeli apartheid regime more broadly, and condemning pro-Israel interests beyond AIPAC.

You can’t necessarily tell who is sincere in such criticisms and who may be doing the bare minimum, perhaps with permission from the pro-Israel lobby, with an understanding that when push comes to shove, they’ll have Israel’s back.

But by looking at who AIPAC is explicitly attacking, you can get a better sense of who, among possible 2028 picks, the pro-Israel lobby is afraid of, and who may pose less of a threat.

In contrast to its attacks on Ocasio-Cortez, Khanna, and Van Hollen, AIPAC has thanked Senators Cory Booker and Mark Kelly once each since 2025, and Pennsylvania Governor Josh Shapiro twice (and Democratic leaders Chuck Schumer and Hakeem Jeffries repeatedly).

Other possible candidates have not attracted attacks. They include former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg, Arizona Senator Ruben Gallego, former Vice President Kamala Harris, Maryland Governor Wes Moore, Connecticut Senator Chris Murphy, California Governor Gavin Newsom, Georgia Senator Jon Ossoff, Illinois Governor JB Pritzker, and Michigan Governor Gretchen Whitmer (who said Thursday she would not run).

Of course, this isn’t conclusive. Ultimately, it’s just a survey of a social feed. And some of the politicians listed above have flashed signs that they could meaningfully stand up to the pro-Israel lobby and enforce U.S. and international law.

But that cuts both ways. It’s easy to vote to limit some weapons to Israel (as Ossoff and Gallego have) if you know the measure won’t pass. The pro-Israel lobby may not like it, but ultimately, it can swallow an action if it does not prompt actual change. Votes can be release valves for political pressure. You’re only a risk if you’re leading the charge, pushing the envelope, asking fundamental questions about the U.S.-Israel relationship, and being the reason for so much public momentum against Israel.

Accordingly, AIPAC evidently sees many possible 2028 Democratic contenders as persuadable - or at least possible to discipline.

Two-and-a-half years into Israel’s genocidal campaign against the Palestinians, amid its genocidal campaign in Lebanon, and its horrific joint war on Iran, if you are a Democrat still considered “gettable” by AIPAC… you have work to do with your base.

Given that the overwhelming opinion among grassroots Democrats is opposition to U.S. aid to Israel’s genocidal regime, Ocasio-Cortez, Khanna, and Van Hollen should all be counted as serious presidential contenders.

We used Ground News to find related stories focusing on AIPAC’s impact in elections. Take this feature on the Kentucky primary race and the influence of the pro-Israel lobby on critical seats.

Ground News is a news app and website that scans 50,000+ articles while offering built-in bias comparisons, fact checks, and funding transparency for every source.

For this story, we see that there is a split in the coverage with 43% left, 19% right, and 38% center . Many of the headlines are the same, but the coverage couldn’t be more different. Ground News makes it easy to see the difference in how both sides are covering the same story.

The built-in factuality and ownership features also highlight the hidden angles behind every article. With Ground News’ Vantage Plan, information becomes your superpower. Learn more at ground.news/zeteox and get 40% off the unlimited Vantage Plan.

(This portion of the post was sponsored content.)

  • Waiting on Trump:The U.S. and Iranreportedlyagreedto extend the ceasefire, reopen the Strait of Hormuz, and prepare a path to talks over issues including Tehran’s nuclear ambitions. JD Vance said, “I can’t guarantee that we’re going to get there, but right now I feel ​pretty good about it.” But his boss hasn’t made up his mind. AxiossaidDonald Trump “wants a couple days to think about it.”
  • Markets react:Oil pricesfellon news of a possible deal, with Brent below $90 a barrel after passing $109 last week.
  • New sanctions:Nonetheless, the U.S. Treasuryimposednew restrictions on Iran’s military oil trade and on entities trading with Tehran.
  • Lebanon latest:Lebanese authoritiessaidat least 19 people were killed and 58 injured in continuing Israeli strikes, as Israel confirmed it had hit the capital, Beirut, for the first time in weeks. Continuing to ignore that deal in the south, Israel forced thousands to flee in Tyre and other regions, as it continued to target civilian areas.
  • Deadly cost:The Lebanese Health Ministrysaidthe death toll from the Israeli onslaught since March 2 had reached at least 3,324, with 10,027 injured.

Fired from ‘60 Minutes’ by CBS News editor-in-chief-cum-Trump-shill Bari Weiss, veteran correspondent Cecilia Vega issued a powerful statement about the beloved but besieged news show.

“I very much fear what comes next,” she said.

Weiss jettisoned serious talent on Thursday, also firing correspondent Sharyn Afonsi and executive producer Tanya Simon. Nick Bilton was named as the new EP – a move backed by prominent Trump supporters.

Vega did not hold back about Weiss’s impact on CBS News, since the founder of The Free Press was installed by Trump ally David Ellison, the new owner of parent company Paramount.

“In recent months,” Vega said, “my producing teams and I have experienced efforts to insert political bias into our stories. Reporting teams have held back on submitting story pitches about important news topics out of fear of the internal repercussions.

“Let’s call this what it is: censorship, both imposed and self-driven. It is dangerous for the show and dangerous for democracy.

“I held the line and refused to incorporate suggestions that offend the conscience… I know… that many producing teams and correspondents working on the show today have had to fight to maintain editorial independence with regularity. I am far from the only 60 Minutes correspondent who has asked herself, ‘What is my personal red line? How much can I push back before I pay the price?’”

Noting that “this season alone I was part of teams that won… a George Polk award and a duPont-Columbia award for our coverage of Venezuelan migrants sent by the Trump administration to El Salvador’s Cecot prison,” Vega said she had “an honor no one can take from me: I was the first Latina correspondent to ever be on ‘60 Minutes.’”

Telling former colleagues to “hold the line,” her sign-off was “La lucha sigue.”

In English, it means “the struggle continues.”

  • Duly noted:Trump officials have pressed the Treasury for a $250 bill featuring Trump’s portrait, theWashington Postreported. Likenesses of living persons have been legally barred from U.S. banknotes since 1866. A top printing official who resigned over the issue told colleagues on leaving: “The buck stopped here.”
  • Corruption watch:Staggering stories of Trump corruption kept pouring out. A White House aidehurried througha $620 million deal for a company tied to Donald Trump Jr. Dell signed a $9.7 billion Pentagon deal after Trump Sr.acquired stock and praised the company. It goes on and on and on.
  • Weaponization watch:The DOJ is probing a nonprofit that helped pay legal bills for E. Jean Carroll, who won millions after Trump was found liable for her sexual abuse and defamation, theWashington Postsaid. Carroll is also atarget.
  • License to kill:The Trump administration’s murderous Pacific and Caribbean boat strikes face two legal deadlines on Friday, NOTUSsaid, citing suits brought by advocacy groups and relatives of two Trinidadian men killed in one such strike.
  • License to chill:Trump’s DOJ is seeking names, addresses, and banking details of Twitter and Reddit users who criticized ICE, Bloombergreported.
  • Recuse me?Supreme Court Justice Samuel Alito’s son works in a political role at the Treasury, NOTUSscooped. The Treasury said Philip Alito “does not counsel on any matters reasonably expected” to be placed before his dad.
  • Maine claims:Senator Susan CollinsattackedDemocrat Graham Platner for saying she voted to send him to Iraq, saying, “That was Platner’s decision to serve. He was not drafted.” Platnerresponded, “There’s nothing more Orwellian than voting to send 18-year-olds to die in a forever war – and then blaming them for it.”

When Justice Samuel Alito joined the Supreme Court in 2006, which Major League Baseball icon attended his welcome dinner – and what did Alito say he smelled like?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Benjamin NetanyahudirectedIsraeli forces to occupy 70% of Gaza, having already ignored a US-brokered truce by extending its footprint in the territory and continuing strikes, killing more than 920 Palestinians. Gaza officials said a strike on Wednesday night killed at least 10 people, including five children.

  • 🇮🇱

    Early protest:UN ambassador Danny DanonsaidIsrael would cut ties to UN Secretary General António Guterres after the UN “added Israel to the blacklist of sexual violence in conflict zones.” The UN’s annual report on such violence is presented to relevant states before publication.
  • 🇨🇺 Cuba latest:Axiosdescribedhow the Trump administration is trying to force the Cuban government to collapse, a source saying the U.S. expects summer unrest as its blockade cuts electricity for cooling and food storage.

  • 🇧🇷

    Brazil bans:The U.S.labeledtwo Brazilian criminal groups as foreign terrorist organizations, a move President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva has said he regards as interference favoring Senator Flávio Bolsonaro, his rival in October’s election.

  • 🇨🇩

    Aid arrives:European Union suppliesreachedBunia in north-eastern Congo, at the center of the outbreak of a rare form of Ebola. More such aid is expected.


Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin is threatening to withdraw Customs officers from airports serving “sanctuary cities,” e.g., places that reject Donald Trump’s fascistic deportation policies. Among appalled reactions, Aaron Reichlin-Melnick, senior fellow at the American Immigration Council, said such a move “would cause enormous economic damage and disrupt air travel nationwide.”

“That he’s even contemplating this publicly is a sign of madness.”

From Tom Dispatch, a look at the strange career of the Fox News host who became Donald Trump’s defense secretary, a man way out of his depth but nonetheless profoundly disturbing U.S. military culture. “Hegseth’s fusion of strength, religion, and violence was encapsulated in a poster allegedly displayed at a U.S. military installation in recent days,” Jasper Craven writes. “It featured Jesus Christ firing a mortar round.”

‘Seriously the Best Boss Ever’: Inside the World of Jeffrey Epstein’s Assistant

Jeffrey Epstein called his executive assistants “an extension of my brain” and a “social prosthesis.” Lesley Groff was one of the best. For The Guardian, Sophie Elmhirst provides a hugely detailed read on the world Groff entered, what she did regarding Epstein and his “girls,“ and what it might yet mean for Groff: on June 9, she will be interviewed in Congress.

How Venture Capital Benefits From Zombie Bankruptcies

Laptop nomads in Washington, Chicago, and Texas knew Foxtrot, a chain that offered lattés, wine, and a spot to work. One day in April 2024, it suddenly went bankrupt, costing hundreds of jobs. Now it’s back, propped up by venture capital, private equity, and laws that allow it to skirt huge debts. And as Hannah Harris Green notes at Mother Jones, cautionary tale of modern capitalism or no, the laptop hordes are back.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.
