item.title: Weiss Is Bari Mad at Colbert
item.description: Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’ Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we reveal how Stephen Colbert made Bari Weiss furious on his way out the door, CBS News ghosts one…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we reveal how Stephen Colbert made Bari Weiss furious on his way out the door, CBS News ghosts one of its top reporters, and The Free Press is offering its readers branded trips with their favorite writers. Also, if a right-wing podcast launches and no one listens, does it even exist? And whatever happened to that rescheduled WHCD? Let’s hop in…

CBS News editor-in-chief Bari Weiss was “incandescent” with rage over Stephen Colbert’s recent mockery of her and Tony Dokoupil, prompting the network’s leadership to direct ‘CBS Mornings’ to ignore the final episode of ‘The Late Show,’ sources told Zeteo.

Puck’s Matt Belloni first reported this week that following Colbert’s highly watched series finale, which saw ‘CBS Mornings’ star Gayle King attend the show’s afterparty and post a loving Instagram tribute to Colbert, CBS News president Tom Cibrowski ordered the morning program not to even mention the end of the iconic franchise.

Colbert “kicked colleagues when they were down,” a CBS News source told Belloni, adding that the late-night host’s actions were “unprofessional and unprovoked.”

During his final few days on the air, Colbert aired a video skit ridiculing Dokoupil and Weiss for reporting from “The Wrong China,” referencing the network’s failure to get the ‘CBS Evening News’ anchor a Chinese visa ahead of Donald Trump’s summit. The visa fiasco led to Dokoupil anchoring from Taiwan, resulting in a slew of embarrassing headlines and additional logistical and personnel headaches. The skit featured Dokoupil with a pumpkin on his head as Weiss, portrayed as an old lady, tried helping out the anchor by smacking him with a mallet.

Belloni also noted that CBS News leadership felt it had been supportive of Colbert during his final few months, covering the entire James Talarico/FCC controversy and inviting the comic to the morning show. Additionally, Cibrowski – who has been tasked with revamping ‘CBS Mornings’ – expressed frustration with Colbert’s bit during the following morning’s editorial call.

Though it is clear that Cibrowski was angry about the sketch, my sources told me that Weiss also pushed for the morning show to ignore Colbert’s finale because she was so incensed by the segment.

Describing Cibrowski as merely “the messenger” of the “no talking about Colbert” directive, a network insider said they were “told it came from Bari.” A CBS News senior staffer also said that Weiss was involved in the decision-making.

“It’s true. I was told that ‘management’ said no Colbert… which means Bari,” the staffer noted, adding: “Bari was incandescent about the Colbert sketch and was humiliated by it.”

Representatives for CBS News and Weiss did not respond to a request for comment.

This isn’t the only on-air segment that has recently left the network’s top editor furious.

Following his final episode of ‘60 Minutes,’ Anderson Cooper – who spurned Weiss’s overtures to become a bigger part of the network – delivered an unsubtle message to the anti-woke CBS chief, who plans on overhauling the venerable newsmagazine.

“I hope ‘60 Minutes’ remains ‘60 Minutes,’” Cooper said in an ‘Overtime’ segment about his 20-year run with the show. “There’s very few things that have been around for as long as ‘60 Minutes’ has and maintained the quality that it has.”

According to Status’s Oliver Darcy, Weiss was told in advance about Cooper’s remarks and “was effectively blindsided by them.” She was also displeased with the fact that the famed newsman used the network’s platform to “take a thinly veiled swipe at her.”

Amid the deluge of negative press surrounding the opinion journalist and her stewardship of the news network, rumors have circulated that The Free Press founder could see her duties scaled back as Paramount prepares to merge with Warner Bros. Discovery – and combine CBS News and CNN.

As for Colbert, he followed up his monumental final ‘Late Show’ episode – which attracted a record 6.7 million viewers – with a guest-hosting gig on the Michigan public access program ‘Only in Monroe.’

The low-tech Friday night broadcast, which saw Colbert crack jokes at Paramount’s expense and featured appearances by Jeff Daniels, Eminem, Steve Buscemi, and Jack White, came 11 years after he first hosted ‘Only in Monroe’ shortly after he was named David Letterman’s replacement for ‘The Late Show.’

Colbert’s one-night return to the local Michigan show also saw the launch of his own YouTube channel, which has already amassed over 180,000 subscribers. (The episode was produced by CBS Studios – likely his last partnership with his old corporate bosses – and also posted on ‘The Late Show’ and Monroe Community Media YouTube channels.)

While Colbert prepares for his next chapter, Donald Trump made sure to remind everyone that not only was he personally ecstatic that the comedian was no longer on late-night, but that Paramount’s “purely financial” reasons for ending ‘The Late Show’ are very much in dispute.

At one point, Trump shared AI slop showing himself tossing Colbert into a trash dumpster before dancing to ‘YMCA,’ seemingly taking credit for forcing Colbert off the air. In another Truth Social post, the president celebrated the show’s demise while suggesting others would soon follow.

“Stephen Colbert’s firing from CBS was the ‘Beginning of the End’ for untalented, nasty, highly overpaid, not funny, and very poorly rated Late Night Television Hosts,” Trump wrote. “Others, of even less talent, to soon follow. May they all Rest in Peace!”

I reached out to a Paramount representative for comment, asking if they agreed with the president that he’s the reason why Colbert was canceled. They did not respond prior to publication.

In a further sign of what’s to come at ‘60 Minutes,’ which is expected to face a significant overhaul under Bari Weiss, Sharyn Alfonsi – the correspondent who butted heads with Weiss over the now-infamous “Inside CECOT” story – is no longer under contract.

While it has long been expected that Alfonsi – who accused Weiss of political meddling on the “CECOT” report – would not be retained, it is the unprofessional manner in which the network has handled her contract that really stands out.

In an interview with the New York Times, Alfonsi said that her agent’s inquiries to CBS News about her contract – which expired on Saturday – have been met with silence. Alfonsi, who decried

In a separate statement, Alfonsi said, “don’t be misled” by CBS News trying to “hide behind corporate euphemisms” to eventually explain away her departure. “This was not a routine corporate transition; it was a deliberate choice to penalize a journalist for refusing to sanitize factually accurate reporting, and it sends a chilling message to the entire newsroom,” she declared.

With the possibility that Bari Weiss could have more free time on her hands as Paramount executives contemplate bringing in someone with actual broadcast TV experience, she’s apparently branching out into event planning.

Seven months after David Ellison handed her $150 million for her heterodox pro-Israel blog The Free Press, Weiss announced on Tuesday that all paid ‘Free Pressers’ would now have a “new world of community experiences” to choose from – including “The Free Press Retreats and Excursions!”

Yes, that’s right. If you ever dreamed of going on a cruise or spending a few days locked away with the folks who brought you Gaza famine denial and a monthslong investigation of Rama Duwaji’s tweets, then it looks like you’re in luck.

“We’re also building something new for 2027: immersive experiences for Free Press readers who want to continue the conversation trailside, poolside, or around a fire long after dessert,” Weiss wrote.

Noting the Free Press Retreats “will be an intimate long weekend built around big ideas, outdoor adventure, great meals, and the kinds of discussions that happen when curious people spend meaningful time together,” Weiss then unveiled the biggest prize of all.

“Free Press Excursions will take us farther afield: curated trips with Free Press writers and contributors exploring places that help explain the world we live in, from the battlefields of Gettysburg and Normandy to the Vatican in Rome,” she revealed.

“This doesn’t sound like a cult at all,” a CBS News reporter deadpanned to me about the announcement.

However, if that is all out of your price range (assuming all Free Press subscribers aren’t ultrawealthy tech bros and nepobaby media moguls), you can still be part of The Free Press Supper Club – where “your next best friend, co-founder, or future spouse may already be waiting at the table.”

Claiming that The Free Press is a “gathering place for independent minds” and for people who feel “politically homeless, and starved for real conversation,” Weiss urged her subscribers to “bring your spouse, your parents, your children—or come on your own.”

“I’m sure this has been in the works for a while, but I wonder if this is Weiss’s next thing if she’s being phased out,” a CBS News senior staffer wondered. “This ‘politically homeless’ and ‘independent thinkers’ thing is maddening when they’re just conservatives dressed as Democrats.”

Did you know that Media Research Center, the well-funded right-wing media watchdog that played a key role in the defunding of NPR and PBS, has a podcast? It’s understandable if you didn’t, because absolutely nobody is checking it out.

NewsBusters, the organization’s site “dedicated to exposing & combating liberal media bias,” launched the podcast 10 months ago and has published 67 episodes. Yet, despite MRC having over 160,000 subscribers to its YouTube channel, not one of NewsBuster’s episodes has passed 1,000 views.

Not even the presence of MAGA stalwarts like Brendan Carr and Scott Jennings as guests has made a ripple, with both of those episodes pulling only a few hundred views. And in recent weeks, the podcast – hosted by NewsBusters executive editor

Apparently, “Putting a Redsteeze Squeeze on Media Misbehavior” or “Just Call Them the Southern Poverty FRAUD Center” just doesn’t bring them in like it used to. Of course, considering that right-wing media has seen its traffic collapse over the past few months, perhaps none of this should come as much of a surprise.

When a shooting abruptly ended the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, Trump said he wanted the affair rescheduled within 30 days. It’s been over a month now.

Weijia Jiang, the White House Correspondents’ Association president who said earlier in May that the organization was still keen on holding the event, did not respond when I reached out to her for any updates.

Of course, the real question is – should this dinner even happen at this point? Wasn’t it already a bad look for the media elite to party with an authoritarian leader who is dismantling press freedoms on a daily basis?

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: ‘Trump Wants Good News’: the Pentagon Is Under Pressure to Sanitize Iran War Data
item.description: For months, staffers for Pete Hegseth’s Pentagon have been under tremendous pressure to produce data and internal reports that match President Donald Trump’s constant flow of self-serving propaganda on the Iran war. Two U.S. officials and another source familiar with the matter tell Zeteo that when…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
For months, staffers for Pete Hegseth’s Pentagon have been under tremendous pressure to produce data and internal reports that match President Donald Trump’s constant flow of self-serving propaganda on the Iran war.

Two U.S. officials and another source familiar with the matter tell Zeteo that when staff have produced data and analyses seen as too gloomy to the American side, higher-ranking officials in Trump’s Department of Defense have told them they need to frontload and emphasize the “good news,” before the reports get briefed to the White House and the president.

Materials that sources say have invited Trumpian edits have included internal data and reports on Iranian military capabilities, damage to Iranian targets, remaining munitions and weapons on all sides of the war, and other key metrics used to measure military success or failure.

All three sources say that within the Defense Department and elsewhere in the U.S. government, a common response to this among staff is: What good news?

One U.S. official compares the ongoing process to someone wanting to hear about a car they were buying, knowing that the car had a ton of documented problems, but then only wishing to receive descriptions of how great and top-of-the-line the new tires are.

The three people say that when analysts and others have tried to soften their reports and intel while still retaining a portrait of how disastrously the war is going for the Trump-Vance administration, there have been multiple times where their work has been edited by Pentagon officials above them, before making its way to Secretary of Defense Hegseth and President Trump.

The other U.S. official recounts to Zeteo an example from recent weeks when military intelligence produced a report that touched on Iranians’ continued robust access to their missile launch sites, despite the administration’s aggressive bombing. This official says staff were chastised by a higher-up for the way it was written and then ordered to “get the data right.”

The New York Times recently reported about there being “evidence that Iran has restored operational access to 30 of the 33 missile sites it maintains along the Strait of Hormuz,” the crucial waterway that Iran has closed since the start of the war, spiking energy prices and throttling the global economy.

Our sources’ accounts further underscore the degree to which the Trump White House has sought to downplay, or even cover up, how horribly their war has gone for them, as the conflict drives up prices for Americans during an election year – and as every reputable poll shows Trump’s war in Iran remains phenomenally unpopular with the U.S. electorate.

According to The Atlantic, Vice President JD Vance “has queried the accuracy of the information the Pentagon has provided about the war,” and has also expressed concerns about how thoroughly the U.S. has depleted its missile stockpiles. Vance

While the administration has disclosed the deaths of 13 troops since the start of the war – and Trump said this weekend he gets “a kick” out of people grousing over so few US deaths – the Intercept has reported that the US casualty count is actually far higher.

“President Trump and his national security team regularly receive full and accurate assessments from the Department of War,” White House spokesperson Anna Kelly tells Zeteo, adding that “the United States Military totally decimated Iran’s ballistic missiles, production facilities, navy, and more.”

Since the weekend, the president has struggled to figure out whether to cry uncle and sign a peace deal widely viewed as him surrendering to Iran – or, if he should instead escalate the conflict again, potentially with a planned spree of cataclysmic war crimes. President Trump is also trying to determine whether or not he should attack Cuba soon, even if he has not finished his illegal war on Iran yet, according to sources who’ve spoken to Trump about this.

But as the Trump administration continues to, per the sources, basically cook its own Iran war data before presenting it to the commander in chief and other top officials, whose fault this is depends on who you ask.

Within the Department of Defense, a number of officials and staffers don’t actually blame Hegseth and his lieutenants for this, even as a good chunk of the Pentagon’s staff call Hegseth mean nicknames – like “Dumb McNamara” – behind his back.

They blame Trump and his staff for creating a culture within the federal apparatus where this kind of behavior is encouraged or demanded.

“Trump wants good news, and he rejects bad news as fake,” the second U.S. official says. “He’s getting what he is paying for.”

item.title: How Bad Is Trump's Health? Medical Analyst Dr. Vin Gupta Weighs in
item.description: After President Donald Trump completed a medical exam at Walter Reed on Tuesday, he took to Truth Social to declare that “Everything checked out PERFECTLY.” But what do we actually know about the 79-year-old president’s health? Dr. Vin Gupta joins Zeteo’s John Harwood in a live conversation with…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
After President Donald Trump completed a medical exam at Walter Reed on Tuesday, he took to Truth Social to declare that “Everything checked out PERFECTLY.”

But what do we actually know about the 79-year-old president’s health? Dr. Vin Gupta joins Zeteo’s John Harwood in a live conversation with Zeteo subscribers on Substack to break down the facts.

Watch the full video above to hear Dr. Gupta unpack what Trump’s Iran genocidal threat revealed about his mental state, how Biden’s health compared to Trump’s, and why physicians around the president are willing to “debase” themselves – even claiming that Trump is in better health than Barack Obama was!

Free subscribers can watch a 2-minute preview. Consider upgrading to a paid subscription and never hit a paywall again!

This was a live conversation on Zeteo.com and the Substack app, where subscribers could ask questions. Be sure to subscribe to catch the next Substack Live.

Share

item.title: 💸 Trump’s Slush Fund Is an Even Bigger Disaster for Republicans Than They Feared
item.description: On this day in 1939, the St. Louis, a ship carrying 937 refugees from Nazi Germany, almost all of them Jewish, was turned away by Cuba. The U.S. and Canada turned the ship away too, forcing it back to Europe, where 254 of the passengers would be killed in the Holocaust. Happy Wednesday, everyone.…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1939, the St. Louis, a ship carrying 937 refugees from Nazi Germany, almost all of them Jewish, was turned away by Cuba. The U.S. and Canada turned the ship away too, forcing it back to Europe, where 254 of the passengers would be killed in the Holocaust.

Happy Wednesday, everyone. It’s Swin, again. For those in the U.S., we hope you spent the first day after the Memorial Day weekend doing something more productive than publicly raging at the “Dumocrats,” an insult ‘The Simpsons’ did decades ago.

If you managed to do better, Zeteo subscribers, then you did better than the president of the United States, who, when he’s not failing to secure peace in the Middle East and wrecking the global economy, or attending a third medical checkup in 13 months while insisting everything’s fine, is busy building a rancidly corrupt slush fund for his buddies.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we get into why, when it comes to this particular Trumpian abomination, the Republican elite is jacking the freak-out dial all the way up past 11. We also look to Texas, where Trump’s MAGA pick, Ken Paxton, duly hammered Senator John Cornyn in their runoff election. Let’s get to it.

If you’ve been following the news for the first year and a half of the second Trump administration, you’ve probably seen elite conservatives, otherwise so fast to sacrifice all oversight and leverage to their dear leader, occasionally pop up to finger-wag the president. Usually, this isn’t for any particular moral or constitutional reason. It’s because Republicans on Capitol Hill want to stay there and don’t like that Trump’s abysmal record and spiraling unpopularity are jeopardizing their job security.

This is why you’ve noticed such an uptick of notable Republicans coming out against the Trump administration’s $1.776 billion pot of taxpayer money, established to benefit the president’s allies and (alleged!) criminal associates who claim Democrats or prosecutors were too mean.

Trump lied that he “wasn’t involved” in this. As Zeteo reported over the weekend, he was deeply involved. The fund was created after Trump sued his own government over an Internal Revenue Service leak of his tax returns. Last week, Trump’s Department of Justice announced that Trump had struck a deal with his own administration, in which he would drop his absolutely bogus lawsuit in exchange for the establishment of what Democrats are now very credibly dubbing a full-on “ MAGA slush fund.” There was even a blisteringly corrupt addendum: immunity from tax-related investigation for Trump, his business, and his family.

To call this shockingly corrupt would be far too polite. And it appears elected Republicans are starting to get the memo.

Multiple sources with direct knowledge of the matter tell me that in recent days, private polling has circulated among a number of prominent Republican organizations. The non-public data shows that Trump’s nearly $1.8 billion slush fund is resoundingly unpopular with most American voters, including much coveted independents.

“This could really fuck us,” one well-connected national GOP consultant vents. “Why do you think everyone’s so upset?”

For starters, some of these numbers are worse than what Republican operatives, and everyone else, have seen in public surveys.

“You don’t need a poll to tell you that this fund was a bad idea,” a former Trump administration official familiar with the private data tells me.

“Far too many Americans now view President Trump as corrupt, and that is going to be a significant hurdle for Republicans this year at a time when the voters want to be hearing about how you are making life easier and cheaper for them or how you’re making the country safe — not about a fucking ballroom. This fund business just adds to that perception. And Donald Trump isn’t the one whose name is on the ballot this year, so he’s not going to be the one who really loses from his decisions or rhetoric.”

As Zeteo politics editor Andrew Perez and I reported over the weekend, multiple Trump officials told us they, along with other senior colleagues, were genuinely surprised by the scale of the GOP pushback they’ve sustained following the Todd Blanche-led announcement of the “Anti-Weaponization Fund.”

Yes. You read that right.

They insist they were surprised.

If so, that would be an astounding level of political miscalculation, on par with a husband being shocked that his spouse didn’t like it that he intentionally flooded their house’s basement, all so he could reenact action sequences from ‘Waterworld.’

Regardless, the president is enduring a backlash from his party of a sort he almost never experiences, even when he’s doing things like siccing wannabe death squads on American civilians and protecting the killers. It’s getting bad enough that Senate Republicans are whining that Trump’s slush fund could hobble his entire agenda.

“The Senate had planned to vote on a bill to fund ICE and the Border Patrol through the end of Trump’s term but scuttled it after the president sought to add $1.8 billion for the DOJ fund,” NBC reported last week.

Despite it all, two sources close to Trump tell me that he still privately supports the fund, still says it’s a great idea, and has not given these sources any reason to think he’s backing down.

But he and his White House may yet go wobbly under pressure. Because it’s no wonder the Republican professional class is freaking out. They didn’t even have to commission the private data. Just look at public YouGov polling that shows if you add up the percentages of U.S. adults who disapprove, vociferously disapprove, or just don’t support Trump’s slush fund, you get a supermajority.

And this happened when the polling question – “Do you approve or disapprove of the Trump administration creating a nearly $1.8 billion fund to pay people who say they were victims of misuse of government power?” – was phrased as diplomatically as you reasonably could in this era of Trump and his cronies’ habitual corruption.

Unfortunately for the president and his party, news of the fund is starting to break through to the median American voter. Don’t listen to those who claim otherwise.

  • ‘Bad faith’:IrandenouncedU.S. strikes Monday as a sign of “bad faith and unreliability” amid talks in Qatar, andsaidit might respond. Iran alsosaidit shot down one U.S. drone and turned away another and a jet that entered its airspace.
  • Troubled waters:Iranian semi-official Tasnim news agencysaid25 vessels passed through the Strait of Hormuz with Tehran’s permission. UK monitorssaida tanker off Oman reported an “external explosion.” Oil prices rose backabove$100 a barrel.
  • Nuclear no-no:The UN Security Councilcondemnedan attack on a nuclear plant in the UAE last week. The UAE said the drones were launched from Iraq.
  • Lebanon latest:Israeli strikes have killed more than 30 people in Lebanon in the past day,Al Jazeerareported, as Muslims worldwide mark Eid al-Adha. Meanwhile, as Benjamin Netanyahu called “large forces on the ground” pushed further north, Israel told residents of Nabatiyeh, as well as dozens of other towns and villages, to leave their homes. Lebanon’s Health Ministryputthe death toll since the latest escalation began at at least 3,213 people.

You could almost feel for John Cornyn, the senior senator from Texas, who took an absolute pounding in his runoff against state attorney general, scandal-magnet, and Trump pick Ken Paxton. Almost.

Paxton only picked up the president’s endorsement last week, but last night he took Cornyn to the cleaners, nearly two-to-one up with most votes counted.

Paxton and his supporters were not the only ones celebrating. Democrats think Paxton – an extremist on abortion, once indicted and impeached, even now embroiled in the kind of coverage that makes Daily Mail copy editors reach for words like “love nest,” “scorned wife,” and “divorce” – is beatable by their man, the progressive populist James Talarico.

Cornyn will watch with the rest of us. It won’t be easy. This is a man who was elected in 2002 and who then rose to the point where he narrowly missed out on becoming Senate majority leader, a clubbable sort who remained prominent under John Thune. A man whom fellow senators respected. A man who spoke disapprovingly of Jan. 6, but didn’t make the gauche mistake of voting to convict. The sort of senator you could see prowling the lobbies and lounges in a book by Robert Caro, setting directions, making deals, getting things done.

But he couldn’t shake off Paxton. And so, recognizing the realities of the moment, needing the president’s support, Cornyn went in for old-fashioned lickspittlery. He posed with a copy of The Art of the Deal, Trump’s 40-year-old potboiler, disowned by the man who actually wrote it. He proposed to name a highway in Trump’s honor.

Cornyn did what he thought he had to do to secure Trump’s favor. And it didn’t work.

Trump endorsed the MAGA creature, the attorney general who backed the attempted coup in 2020. Of course he did. And, despite all the media and podcast chatter about ‘MAGA splits’, this is still very much Donald Trump’s party. And so: Cornyn was absolutely thrashed in the runoff, his quarter-century Senate career reduced to swirling dust.

Crestfallen, facing supporters, Cornyn quoted the Apostle Paul and said he would "support the Republican ticket … in this general election."

You could almost feel for him. Almost.

  • Health check:Donald Trump, 80 next month,said“everything checked out PERFECTLY” at Walter Reed hospital, where he had a third health appointment in a little more than a year, amid widespreaddoubtsabout his fitness for office. The White House did not comment.
  • Big lies:Trump said the 2020 election was rigged at least 107 times in six months, Reutersreported. One expert said: “This is about the midterms. He’s trying to create a ​fog of disinformation… so then if he dials it up further with federal interference, the public will not react as surprised.”
  • Palmetto no:South Carolina Republicans dealt a blow to Trump’s hopes of using partisan gerrymandering to hold the US House in November,voting downa redraw that would’ve eliminated the seat of Democratic Rep. Jim Clyburn.
  • In stock:Rep. Tom Kean Jr, the New Jersey Republican missing from Congress for months, has continued to trade stocks in that time, NOTUSnoticed.
  • FBI firing:Director Kash Patel last week fired an analyst who helped assess the motives of the gunman who attacked Republican congressional baseball practice in 2017, MS Nowreported. Right-wingers have long claimed bias because the shooting was not initially labeled a domestic terror attack.
  • New targets:Wiredreportedthe federal government is responding to public anger over AI and data centers by creating a new target: “anti-technology extremists.”
  • Small win:A federal appeals courtgrantedan order that temporarily blocks Trump immigration officials from detaining Mahmoud Khalil again, as he appeals his case to the Supreme Court.

In 2013, what did then-Texas state senator Ken Paxton pick up and pocket next to a courthouse metal detector, before returning it to its rightful owner at a sheriff’s request?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Gaza officialssaidat least five people, including a woman, were killed and 20 were injured in an Israeli strike on an apartment building in Gaza City yesterday. Israelclaimedto have killed Hamas’s newly appointed Gaza military chief, Mohammed Odeh. Meanwhile,Israeli strikeson Gaza today – on Eid al-Adha – have killed at least six people.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    New barrage:Russia fired more than 100 drones and two ballistic missiles at Ukraine, Ukrainian forcessaid, though they added that Russian threats of “systemic strikes” on Kyiv had not produced anything new, and international diplomats Moscow said should leave the capital had not done so.

  • 🇸🇳

    Senegal stand-off:Lawmakers defied President Bassirou Diomaye Faye andelectedousted Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko as speaker, as the country headed for political deadlock amid a worsening debt crisis.

  • 🇬🇧

    Heat hits:Record temperaturesgrippedthe UK, France, and Spain, with reports of related deaths. In Edinburgh, Arthur’s Seat, a famous hill,caught fire.

Kennedy Jr. said of this video, “Cheryl cheerleads the removal of a pair of black racers from Dr Oz's patio.” In truth, his wife, Cheryl Hines, sounds more alarmed than enthusiastic, as her husband wrangles the snakes. It’s not known if RFK Jr. took them home for his freezer.

item.title: 💸 Trump’s Slush Fund Is an Even Bigger Disaster for Republicans Than They Feared
item.description: On this day in 1939, the St. Louis, a ship carrying 937 refugees from Nazi Germany, almost all of them Jewish, was turned away by Cuba. The U.S. and Canada turned the ship away too, forcing it back to Europe, where 254 of the passengers would be killed in the Holocaust. Happy Wednesday, everyone.…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1939, the St. Louis, a ship carrying 937 refugees from Nazi Germany, almost all of them Jewish, was turned away by Cuba. The U.S. and Canada turned the ship away too, forcing it back to Europe, where 254 of the passengers would be killed in the Holocaust.

Happy Wednesday, everyone. It’s Swin, again. For those in the U.S., we hope you spent the first day after the Memorial Day weekend doing something more productive than publicly raging at the “Dumocrats,” an insult ‘The Simpsons’ did decades ago.

If you managed to do better, Zeteo subscribers, then you did better than the president of the United States, who, when he’s not failing to secure peace in the Middle East and wrecking the global economy, or attending a third medical checkup in 13 months while insisting everything’s fine, is busy building a rancidly corrupt slush fund for his buddies.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we get into why, when it comes to this particular Trumpian abomination, the Republican elite is jacking the freak-out dial all the way up past 11. We also look to Texas, where Trump’s MAGA pick, Ken Paxton, duly hammered Senator John Cornyn in their runoff election. Let’s get to it.

If you’ve been following the news for the first year and a half of the second Trump administration, you’ve probably seen elite conservatives, otherwise so fast to sacrifice all oversight and leverage to their dear leader, occasionally pop up to finger-wag the president. Usually, this isn’t for any particular moral or constitutional reason. It’s because Republicans on Capitol Hill want to stay there and don’t like that Trump’s abysmal record and spiraling unpopularity are jeopardizing their job security.

This is why you’ve noticed such an uptick of notable Republicans coming out against the Trump administration’s $1.776 billion pot of taxpayer money, established to benefit the president’s allies and (alleged!) criminal associates who claim Democrats or prosecutors were too mean.

Trump lied that he “wasn’t involved” in this. As Zeteo reported over the weekend, he was deeply involved. The fund was created after Trump sued his own government over an Internal Revenue Service leak of his tax returns. Last week, Trump’s Department of Justice announced that Trump had struck a deal with his own administration, in which he would drop his absolutely bogus lawsuit in exchange for the establishment of what Democrats are now very credibly dubbing a full-on “ MAGA slush fund.” There was even a blisteringly corrupt addendum: immunity from tax-related investigation for Trump, his business, and his family.

To call this shockingly corrupt would be far too polite. And it appears elected Republicans are starting to get the memo.

Multiple sources with direct knowledge of the matter tell me that in recent days, private polling has circulated among a number of prominent Republican organizations. The non-public data shows that Trump’s nearly $1.8 billion slush fund is resoundingly unpopular with most American voters, including much coveted independents.

“This could really fuck us,” one well-connected national GOP consultant vents. “Why do you think everyone’s so upset?”

For starters, some of these numbers are worse than what Republican operatives, and everyone else, have seen in public surveys.

“You don’t need a poll to tell you that this fund was a bad idea,” a former Trump administration official familiar with the private data tells me.

“Far too many Americans now view President Trump as corrupt, and that is going to be a significant hurdle for Republicans this year at a time when the voters want to be hearing about how you are making life easier and cheaper for them or how you’re making the country safe — not about a fucking ballroom. This fund business just adds to that perception. And Donald Trump isn’t the one whose name is on the ballot this year, so he’s not going to be the one who really loses from his decisions or rhetoric.”

As Zeteo politics editor Andrew Perez and I reported over the weekend, multiple Trump officials told us they, along with other senior colleagues, were genuinely surprised by the scale of the GOP pushback they’ve sustained following the Todd Blanche-led announcement of the “Anti-Weaponization Fund.”

Yes. You read that right.

They insist they were surprised.

If so, that would be an astounding level of political miscalculation, on par with a husband being shocked that his spouse didn’t like it that he intentionally flooded their house’s basement, all so he could reenact action sequences from ‘Waterworld.’

Regardless, the president is enduring a backlash from his party of a sort he almost never experiences, even when he’s doing things like siccing wannabe death squads on American civilians and protecting the killers. It’s getting bad enough that Senate Republicans are whining that Trump’s slush fund could hobble his entire agenda.

“The Senate had planned to vote on a bill to fund ICE and the Border Patrol through the end of Trump’s term but scuttled it after the president sought to add $1.8 billion for the DOJ fund,” NBC reported last week.

Despite it all, two sources close to Trump tell me that he still privately supports the fund, still says it’s a great idea, and has not given these sources any reason to think he’s backing down.

But he and his White House may yet go wobbly under pressure. Because it’s no wonder the Republican professional class is freaking out. They didn’t even have to commission the private data. Just look at public YouGov polling that shows if you add up the percentages of U.S. adults who disapprove, vociferously disapprove, or just don’t support Trump’s slush fund, you get a supermajority.

And this happened when the polling question – “Do you approve or disapprove of the Trump administration creating a nearly $1.8 billion fund to pay people who say they were victims of misuse of government power?” – was phrased as diplomatically as you reasonably could in this era of Trump and his cronies’ habitual corruption.

Unfortunately for the president and his party, news of the fund is starting to break through to the median American voter. Don’t listen to those who claim otherwise.

  • ‘Bad faith’:IrandenouncedU.S. strikes Monday as a sign of “bad faith and unreliability” amid talks in Qatar, andsaidit might respond. Iran alsosaidit shot down one U.S. drone and turned away another and a jet that entered its airspace.
  • Troubled waters:Iranian semi-official Tasnim news agencysaid25 vessels passed through the Strait of Hormuz with Tehran’s permission. UK monitorssaida tanker off Oman reported an “external explosion.” Oil prices rose backabove$100 a barrel.
  • Nuclear no-no:The UN Security Councilcondemnedan attack on a nuclear plant in the UAE last week. The UAE said the drones were launched from Iraq.
  • Lebanon latest:Israeli strikes have killed more than 30 people in Lebanon in the past day,Al Jazeerareported, as Muslims worldwide mark Eid al-Adha. Meanwhile, as Benjamin Netanyahu called “large forces on the ground” pushed further north, Israel told residents of Nabatiyeh, as well as dozens of other towns and villages, to leave their homes. Lebanon’s Health Ministryputthe death toll since the latest escalation began at at least 3,213 people.

You could almost feel for John Cornyn, the senior senator from Texas, who took an absolute pounding in his runoff against state attorney general, scandal-magnet, and Trump pick Ken Paxton. Almost.

Paxton only picked up the president’s endorsement last week, but last night he took Cornyn to the cleaners, nearly two-to-one up with most votes counted.

Paxton and his supporters were not the only ones celebrating. Democrats think Paxton – an extremist on abortion, once indicted and impeached, even now embroiled in the kind of coverage that makes Daily Mail copy editors reach for words like “love nest,” “scorned wife,” and “divorce” – is beatable by their man, the progressive populist James Talarico.

Cornyn will watch with the rest of us. It won’t be easy. This is a man who was elected in 2002 and who then rose to the point where he narrowly missed out on becoming Senate majority leader, a clubbable sort who remained prominent under John Thune. A man whom fellow senators respected. A man who spoke disapprovingly of Jan. 6, but didn’t make the gauche mistake of voting to convict. The sort of senator you could see prowling the lobbies and lounges in a book by Robert Caro, setting directions, making deals, getting things done.

But he couldn’t shake off Paxton. And so, recognizing the realities of the moment, needing the president’s support, Cornyn went in for old-fashioned lickspittlery. He posed with a copy of The Art of the Deal, Trump’s 40-year-old potboiler, disowned by the man who actually wrote it. He proposed to name a highway in Trump’s honor.

Cornyn did what he thought he had to do to secure Trump’s favor. And it didn’t work.

Trump endorsed the MAGA creature, the attorney general who backed the attempted coup in 2020. Of course he did. And, despite all the media and podcast chatter about ‘MAGA splits’, this is still very much Donald Trump’s party. And so: Cornyn was absolutely thrashed in the runoff, his quarter-century Senate career reduced to swirling dust.

Crestfallen, facing supporters, Cornyn quoted the Apostle Paul and said he would "support the Republican ticket … in this general election."

You could almost feel for him. Almost.

  • Health check:Donald Trump, 80 next month,said“everything checked out PERFECTLY” at Walter Reed hospital, where he had a third health appointment in a little more than a year, amid widespreaddoubtsabout his fitness for office. The White House did not comment.
  • Big lies:Trump said the 2020 election was rigged at least 107 times in six months, Reutersreported. One expert said: “This is about the midterms. He’s trying to create a ​fog of disinformation… so then if he dials it up further with federal interference, the public will not react as surprised.”
  • Palmetto no:South Carolina Republicans dealt a blow to Trump’s hopes of using partisan gerrymandering to hold the US House in November,voting downa redraw that would’ve eliminated the seat of Democratic Rep. Jim Clyburn.
  • In stock:Rep. Tom Kean Jr, the New Jersey Republican missing from Congress for months, has continued to trade stocks in that time, NOTUSnoticed.
  • FBI firing:Director Kash Patel last week fired an analyst who helped assess the motives of the gunman who attacked Republican congressional baseball practice in 2017, MS Nowreported. Right-wingers have long claimed bias because the shooting was not initially labeled a domestic terror attack.
  • New targets:Wiredreportedthe federal government is responding to public anger over AI and data centers by creating a new target: “anti-technology extremists.”
  • Small win:A federal appeals courtgrantedan order that temporarily blocks Trump immigration officials from detaining Mahmoud Khalil again, as he appeals his case to the Supreme Court.

In 2013, what did then-Texas state senator Ken Paxton pick up and pocket next to a courthouse metal detector, before returning it to its rightful owner at a sheriff’s request?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Gaza officialssaidat least five people, including a woman, were killed and 20 were injured in an Israeli strike on an apartment building in Gaza City yesterday. Israelclaimedto have killed Hamas’s newly appointed Gaza military chief, Mohammed Odeh. Meanwhile,Israeli strikeson Gaza today – on Eid al-Adha – have killed at least six people.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    New barrage:Russia fired more than 100 drones and two ballistic missiles at Ukraine, Ukrainian forcessaid, though they added that Russian threats of “systemic strikes” on Kyiv had not produced anything new, and international diplomats Moscow said should leave the capital had not done so.

  • 🇸🇳

    Senegal stand-off:Lawmakers defied President Bassirou Diomaye Faye andelectedousted Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko as speaker, as the country headed for political deadlock amid a worsening debt crisis.

  • 🇬🇧

    Heat hits:Record temperaturesgrippedthe UK, France, and Spain, with reports of related deaths. In Edinburgh, Arthur’s Seat, a famous hill,caught fire.


Health Secretary and noted wildlife botherer Robert F. Kennedy Jr. said of this video, “Cheryl cheerleads the removal of a pair of black racers from Dr Oz's patio.” In truth, his wife, Cheryl Hines, sounds more alarmed than enthusiastic, as her husband wrangles the snakes. It’s not known if RFK Jr. took them home for his freezer.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

item.title: This Bipartisan Crypto Bill Could Make Trump Family ‘Vanderbilt-Level Rich’
item.description: As Donald Trump loots the U.S. Treasury Department and wages an illegal war in Iran that is devastating Americans’ finances, Congress is preparing a risky new gift for the Trump family and the billionaire crypto executives who are buying U.S. elections. If lawmakers were to pass the bipartisan…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
As Donald Trump loots the U.S. Treasury Department and wages an illegal war in Iran that is devastating Americans’ finances, Congress is preparing a risky new gift for the Trump family and the billionaire crypto executives who are buying U.S. elections.

If lawmakers were to pass the bipartisan crypto legislation moving through the U.S. Congress without also reining in Trump’s crypto graft – the bill contains no such restrictions now – it “could make the Trump family Vanderbilt-level rich if they play their cards right,” says Mark Hays, who leads crypto policy at Americans for Financial Reform and Demand Progress Action. “It just opens up many opportunities for them to leverage their political and business connections, and their control over the regulatory apparatus, to position their companies to make billions more.”

On May 14, the Senate Banking Committee passed the Clarity Act, legislation that would place light-touch regulations on cryptocurrencies and crypto platforms, and encourage banks to invest in, trade with, or speculate on risky crypto assets. The bill would also allow crypto firms to sell tokenized assets that supposedly mirror or represent specific stocks – without the regulations that a company must go through to register or offer a stock.

The Clarity Act fails to address how crypto companies can offer yield or interest on the use of stablecoins – which allows the firms to operate like banks, but without the same oversight. This situation could expose consumers to more risk, and limit the ability of banks and lenders to offer credit to customers, experts say.

“It’s introducing a level of systemic risk for no benefit, other than the benefit of the insiders and the handful of crypto companies that are going to make out like bandits,” says actor and investigative journalist Ben McKenzie, speaking about the broader bill.

Of course, Trump is perhaps the world’s best-connected crypto insider, and might be its biggest bandit. In just the first six months of his presidency, Trump and his family brought in an estimated $800 million from their crypto ventures. The president has already signed one weak, industry-backed crypto regulation bill this term, and his family stands to benefit further under the Clarity Act.

“President Trump only acts in the best interests of the American public – which is why they overwhelmingly re-elected him to this office, despite years of lies and false accusations against him and his businesses from the fake news media,” says White House spokesperson Davis Ingle. “President Trump’s assets are in a trust managed by his children. There are no conflicts of interest.”

Despite demands from some Democrats who are keen to support the legislation, the measure lacks “any provision to stop Trump’s crypto corruption,” McKenzie notes.

Democratic Senators Ruben Gallego of Arizona and Angela Alsobrooks of Maryland voted together to advance the Clarity Act out of the banking committee. Both said they aren’t guaranteed to vote for the final bill if it comes to the Senate floor, where it will need support from a handful of Democrats.

“Perhaps the toughest issue of all, we have come close but have not finished an agreement on ethics guardrails for elected officials, all elected officials,” Gallego said.

Last year, the freshman senator held a fundraiser with crypto kingpin and Trump megadonor Marc Andreessen, a founder of the venture capital firm Andreessen Horowitz. Gallego also saw the crypto industry spend $10 million to boost his 2024 campaign, with ads like this one.

Hurdles remain to passing the Clarity Act. The Senate banking version will need to be reconciled with a companion bill passed by the agriculture committee. If the legislation passes the Senate, it will need to be merged with the version that passed the House. Complicating matters, Congress has a lot of recess time planned this summer and fall, ahead of the midterm elections in November. Lawmakers are currently on a week-long Memorial Day vacation.

That this bill, which would only benefit a tiny segment of the ultra-wealthy, has continued to advance through Congress in a key election year, as Americans face a cost-of-living crisis that Trump keeps making worse, speaks to the power and influence of the crypto industry and the scale of its political spending.

As McKenzie puts it, how Congress should regulate crypto is simple – tightly, with crypto treated as securities. That is “not easy to accomplish politically, because of Citizens United,” he says, referring to the Supreme Court decision that allowed billionaires and corporations to spend unlimited amounts on elections.

The Biden administration did regulate crypto as securities, under existing laws, and that meant execs in some cases facing jail, lawsuits, and enforcement actions.

But Trump campaigned on leading a crypto-friendly administration, while his family began making forays into the industry.

Importantly, the crypto industry dumped $260 million into its own super PAC network during the 2024 election cycle to spend on primaries and elections, and made big donations to pro-Trump and Republican super PACs, too.

The crypto industry came out of 2024 with what it wanted: a GOP trifecta in Washington, new crypto-friendly regulators, and already one deregulatory law so far. As the industry heralds the Clarity Act’s passage through the Senate banking committee, its primary super PAC, Fairshake, has already raised $135 million this cycle.

More than a third of that money has come from billionaires Andreessen and Ben Horowitz, and their VC firm, Andreessen Horowitz, who this cycle make up the biggest donor in US politics.

The firm and Fairshake did not respond to a request for comment.

The rest of Fairshake’s largesse has mostly come from crypto exchange Coinbase and crypto firm Ripple, which both previously faced federal scrutiny for allegedly violating federal securities rules.

In this cycle and the last one, crypto industry money has been distributed through Fairshake and two other super PACs – Protect Progress, which gets involved in Democratic primaries, and Defend American Jobs, which works in Republican primaries.

Arkansas GOP Rep. French Hill, who championed the House version of the Clarity Act, won his primary in March with $419,000 in support from Defend American Jobs.

While Fairshake’s $10 million in spending against Democratic Senate candidate Juliana Stratton failed in Illinois, the $5 million that Defend American Jobs spent supporting Rep. Barry Moore in Alabama’s Republican Senate primary paid off last week. So did the $7.2 million it spent supporting Andy Barr in Kentucky’s GOP Senate primary.

In Georgia, the crypto industry’s super PACs spent roughly $4.3 million to boost Jasmine Clark in the 13th congressional district’s Democratic primary; $710,000 to support Houston Gaines in the 10th district’s Republican primary; $455,000 to boost Rep. Clay Fuller in the 14th district’s GOP primary; and $430,000 to support Jim Kingston in the 1st district Republican primary. All four candidates won their primaries last week.

In an unusual move, current Texas Rep. Jasmine Crockett, who will give up her seat next year after failing to win the Democrfatic primary for Senate, is appearing in crypto super PAC ads to boost Democratic Rep. Christian Menefee in the 18th congressional district run-off.

Protect Progress has spent $5 million supporting Menefee over Dem Rep. Al Green, who was drawn out of his district by a Republican gerrymander. The run-off will take place Tuesday.

“We need a new generation of fighters who are ready to throw down for what’s right,” Crockett says in an ad from Protect Progress. “My friend Christian Menefee is that fighter… In Congress, I see him doing the work firsthand every single day.”

Brendan Fischer, director of strategic investigations at the watchdog Campaign Legal Center, tells Zeteo, “I can’t think of another instance of a sitting lawmaker appearing in an industry-funded super PAC ad.”

Crockett went on TV last year to defend Green after the House censured him for shouting down Trump’s joint address to Congress. “I stand with Al Green,” she said at the time.

Menefee, 38, was first elected to Congress a few months ago. Green, 78, has represented Houston in DC for two decades.

In another ad from Protect Progress, a narrator says: “Al Green and I are about the same age, at almost 80 years old. He has been in public office for almost 50 years. It’s time to pass the torch.”

As the ad ends, images of Crockett and Menefee appear. “Christian Menefee, the fighter we need in Congress,” the narrator says. “Endorsed by Jasmine Crockett.”

Her office did not respond to a request for comment.

Crockett was first elected in 2022 with $2.7 million in support from two crypto industry super PACs. Both outside groups were funded by the original crypto megadonor, Sam Bankman-Fried, who was sentenced to 25 years in prison after his crypto firm blew up in a fraud scheme. Ultimately, the largest donor to the second super PAC that supported Crockett was Andreessen Horowitz – the firm and its founders.

On May 15, Green slammed crypto industry spending in a speech on the House floor.

“I rise as an unbought, liberated, unafraid Democrat, unbought by crypto crash, unbought by the millions of dollars that the crypto industry spends to elect people to Congress so they can have influence over legislation,” he said. “Unbossed, unbought, and unafraid to speak truth to the powerful crypto industry, and unafraid to speak truth about the powerful crypto industry.”

That same day, the crypto super PAC Protect Progress put another $1.3 million down on ads for Green’s opponent.

Zeteo’s Andrew McDonald contributed reporting to this story.
